title: MASEval: Extending Multi-Agent Evaluation from Models to Systems
summary:
The rapid adoption of LLM-based agentic systems has produced a rich ecosystem of frameworks (smolagents, LangGraph, AutoGen, CAMEL, LlamaIndex, i.a.). Yet existing benchmarks are model-centric: they fix the agentic setup and do not compare other system components. We argue that implementation decisions substantially impact performance, including choices such as topology, orchestration logic, and error handling. MASEval addresses this evaluation gap with a framework-agnostic library that treats the entire system as the unit of analysis. Through a systematic system-level comparison across 3 benchmarks, 3 models, and 3 frameworks, we find that framework choice matters as much as model choice. MASEval allows researchers to explore all components of agentic systems, opening new avenues for principled system design, and practitioners to identify the best implementation for their use case.
  MASEval is available under the MIT licence https://github.com/parameterlab/MASEval.

AI.tldr: MASEval is a framework-agnostic evaluation library that compares full LLM agent systems (not just models) and shows framework choice can matter as much as model choice.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based agentic systems focus almost exclusively on comparing models under a fixed agent setup, ignoring how implementation details like framework, agent topology, orchestration logic, and error handling affect performance. As a result, we lack systematic tools to evaluate and compare entire agentic systems across different frameworks and configurations.
AI.method: The authors design MASEval, a framework-agnostic evaluation library that treats the complete agentic system as the evaluation unit. They run a controlled, systematic comparison varying three axes: benchmarks, LLMs, and agent frameworks. By holding tasks and models constant while swapping frameworks and system design choices, they quantify the performance impact of those implementation decisions.
AI.result: Across experiments covering 3 benchmarks, 3 models, and 3 agent frameworks, they empirically find that the choice of framework and system-level design can impact performance as much as the choice of underlying model. This demonstrates that system implementation details are a first-order factor in overall agent performance, not a minor concern.
AI.conclusion: System-level design choices for LLM agentic systems—such as framework, topology, orchestration, and error handling—are crucial determinants of performance. MASEval provides a standardized, framework-agnostic way to evaluate and compare full systems, enabling researchers to study agentic architectures more systematically and practitioners to select the best-performing implementation for their application.

title: LDP: An Identity-Aware Protocol for Multi-Agent LLM Systems
summary: As multi-agent AI systems grow in complexity, the protocols connecting them constrain their capabilities. Current protocols such as A2A and MCP do not expose model-level properties as first-class primitives, ignoring properties fundamental to effective delegation: model identity, reasoning profile, quality calibration, and cost characteristics. We present the LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP), an AI-native communication protocol introducing five mechanisms: (1) rich delegate identity cards with quality hints and reasoning profiles; (2) progressive payload modes with negotiation and fallback; (3) governed sessions with persistent context; (4) structured provenance tracking confidence and verification status; (5) trust domains enforcing security boundaries at the protocol level. We implement LDP as a plugin for the JamJet agent runtime and evaluate against A2A and random baselines using local Ollama models and LLM-as-judge evaluation. Identity-aware routing achieves ~12x lower latency on easy tasks through delegate specialization, though it does not improve aggregate quality in our small delegate pool; semantic frame payloads reduce token count by 37% (p=0.031) with no observed quality loss; governed sessions eliminate 39% token overhead at 10 rounds; and noisy provenance degrades synthesis quality below the no-provenance baseline, arguing that confidence metadata is harmful without verification. Simulated analyses show architectural advantages in attack detection (96% vs. 6%) and failure recovery (100% vs. 35% completion). This paper contributes a protocol design, reference implementation, and initial evidence that AI-native protocol primitives enable more efficient and governable delegation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP), an AI-native protocol for multi-agent systems that makes model identity, reasoning profiles, costs, and provenance first-class features, and shows it can cut latency and token usage while improving governance and robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent AI protocols like A2A and MCP mostly treat models as interchangeable black boxes and focus on message passing. They lack explicit support for properties that matter for delegation—such as which model is being queried, how it reasons, what its expected quality and cost are, and how to track provenance and security boundaries. As systems grow more complex, these limitations hurt efficiency (e.g., routing tasks to the right model), governance (e.g., tracking trust and verification status), and robustness (e.g., detecting attacks or failures). The authors are motivated to design a protocol that is native to LLMs and explicitly encodes these delegation-relevant properties.
AI.method: The authors design the LLM Delegate Protocol (LDP) around five mechanisms: (1) identity cards for delegates that encode quality hints and reasoning profiles; (2) progressive payload modes that support negotiation and fallbacks, including semantic-frame representations; (3) governed sessions that maintain persistent context across interactions; (4) structured provenance that captures confidence and verification status; and (5) trust domains that enforce security boundaries. They implement LDP as a plugin in the JamJet agent runtime and then empirically compare it to A2A and random baselines. Experiments use local Ollama models and LLM-as-judge evaluation to measure latency, token usage, quality, and robustness. They also conduct simulations to probe attack detection and failure recovery characteristics of the architectures enabled by LDP.
AI.result: Identity-aware routing via LDP yields about 12x lower latency on easier tasks by specializing delegates, but in their small pool of delegates it does not increase overall answer quality. Using semantic frame payloads cuts token usage by 37% with statistically significant savings (p=0.031) and no observed drop in quality. Governed sessions reduce token overhead by 39% at 10 dialogue rounds. Surprisingly, adding noisy provenance metadata worsens synthesis quality relative to having no provenance at all, suggesting that confidence signals can be harmful without reliable verification. In simulated scenarios, LDP-style architectures dramatically outperform baselines in attack detection (96% vs. 6%) and failure recovery (100% vs. 35% task completion).
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that explicitly AI-native protocol primitives—delegate identity, structured payload negotiation, governed sessions, structured provenance, and trust domains—can significantly improve the efficiency, governance, and robustness of multi-agent AI systems. The LLM Delegate Protocol and its reference implementation in JamJet demonstrate practical gains in latency, token efficiency, and conversational overhead, as well as architectural advantages for security and reliability. However, the findings on noisy provenance caution that confidence metadata must be coupled with verification to be beneficial. Overall, the work provides an initial but promising case that better-designed inter-agent protocols are key to scalable, governable AI delegation.

title: Quantifying the Accuracy and Cost Impact of Design Decisions in Budget-Constrained Agentic LLM Search
comment: Accepted in 2026 Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC)
summary: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems combine iterative search, planning prompts, and retrieval backends, but deployed settings impose explicit budgets on tool calls and completion tokens. We present a controlled measurement study of how search depth, retrieval strategy, and completion budget affect accuracy and cost under fixed constraints. Using Budget-Constrained Agentic Search (BCAS), a model-agnostic evaluation harness that surfaces remaining budget and gates tool use, we run comparisons across six LLMs and three question-answering benchmarks. Across models and datasets, accuracy improves with additional searches up to a small cap, hybrid lexical and dense retrieval with lightweight re-ranking produces the largest average gains in our ablation grid, and larger completion budgets are most helpful on HotpotQA-style synthesis. These results provide practical guidance for configuring budgeted agentic retrieval pipelines and are accompanied by reproducible prompts and evaluation settings.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best configure agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems when there are strict limits on tool calls and tokens, and offers empirically grounded recommendations.
AI.motivation: Deployed RAG agents must operate under real-world budget constraints on both external tool calls (like search or retrieval) and LLM completion tokens. Existing work proposes deeper search, more complex planning, and richer retrieval strategies, but there is little systematic evidence about how these design choices trade off accuracy and cost under fixed budgets. Practitioners lack clear guidance on how many searches to perform, which retrieval strategy to use, and how to allocate token budgets for different QA tasks.
AI.method: The authors build Budget-Constrained Agentic Search (BCAS), a model-agnostic evaluation framework that explicitly tracks remaining budget and controls when the agent can call tools. Using BCAS, they run a controlled measurement study over six different LLMs and three QA benchmarks. They systematically vary: (1) search depth / number of tool calls, (2) retrieval strategies, including lexical, dense, and hybrid with re-ranking, and (3) completion token budgets. They then compare accuracy and cost across this ablation grid under fixed overall budgets.
AI.result: Empirically, they find: (1) Accuracy generally increases as the agent performs more searches, but only up to a small cap, after which gains saturate. (2) A hybrid retrieval setup combining lexical and dense retrieval, enhanced with lightweight re-ranking, yields the largest average accuracy improvements across models and datasets. (3) Larger completion budgets most significantly benefit multi-hop, synthesis-heavy tasks such as HotpotQA, where more text generation is required to integrate multiple pieces of evidence.
AI.conclusion: Agentic RAG systems benefit from modest but not excessive search depth, and from hybrid lexical+dense retrieval with simple re-ranking when operating under budget constraints. Token budget increases should be prioritized for complex, synthesis-oriented QA tasks rather than uniformly across all workloads. The provided BCAS framework, prompts, and evaluation settings enable reproducible comparisons and give practitioners concrete, empirically grounded guidelines for configuring budgeted agentic retrieval pipelines.

title: Interpretable Markov-Based Spatiotemporal Risk Surfaces for Missing-Child Search Planning with Reinforcement Learning and LLM-Based Quality Assurance
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures. Accepted at ICEIS 2026 (International Conference on Enterprise Information Systems)
summary: The first 72 hours of a missing-child investigation are critical for successful recovery. However, law enforcement agencies often face fragmented, unstructured data and a lack of dynamic, geospatial predictive tools. Our system, Guardian, provides an end-to-end decision-support system for missing-child investigation and early search planning. It converts heterogeneous, unstructured case documents into a schema-aligned spatiotemporal representation, enriches cases with geocoding and transportation context, and provides probabilistic search products spanning 0-72 hours. In this paper, we present an overview of Guardian as well as a detailed description of a three-layer predictive component of the system. The first layer is a Markov chain, a sparse, interpretable model with transitions incorporating road accessibility costs, seclusion preferences, and corridor bias with separate day/night parameterizations. The Markov chain's output prediction distributions are then transformed into operationally useful search plans by the second layer's reinforcement learning. Finally, the third layer's LLM performs post hoc validation of layer 2 search plans prior to their release. Using a synthetic but realistic case study, we report quantitative outputs across 24/48/72-hour horizons and analyze sensitivity, failure modes, and tradeoffs. Results show that the proposed predictive system with the three-layer architecture produces interpretable priors for zone optimization and human review.

AI.tldr: Guardian is an end-to-end geospatial decision-support system that turns messy missing-child case data into interpretable 0–72 hour search probability maps using a three-layer predictive architecture.
AI.motivation: Early hours in missing-child investigations are crucial, yet police work with fragmented, unstructured reports and lack rigorous, dynamic, geospatial tools to prioritize where and when to search. The paper aims to provide a principled, interpretable, and operationally usable predictive system to support early search planning.
AI.method: The system ingests heterogeneous, unstructured case documents and converts them into a structured spatiotemporal schema, enriching them with geocoding and transportation context. The predictive engine has three layers: (1) a Markov chain over space-time that models movement with road-access costs, seclusion preferences, corridor bias, and separate day/night parameters; (2) a reinforcement learning layer that converts Markov-based probability distributions into concrete, operational search plans; and (3) a large language model layer that performs post hoc validation and sanity-checking of RL-generated search plans before release. The paper evaluates this pipeline on a synthetic but realistic case study.
AI.result: Across 24/48/72-hour horizons on the synthetic case, the three-layer model generates quantitative probability distributions and associated search plans. Analysis of sensitivity, failure modes, and tradeoffs indicates the architecture can produce realistic, operationally relevant priors that support zone optimization and expert review.
AI.conclusion: The proposed three-layer Guardian architecture can transform raw missing-child case data into interpretable, probabilistic search products for the first 72 hours. Its Markov + RL + LLM stack yields structured priors and search plans that are suitable for human validation and operational use, suggesting promise as a decision-support tool for early missing-child search planning.

title: One Language, Two Scripts: Probing Script-Invariance in LLM Concept Representations
comment: Accepted at the UCRL Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Do the features learned by Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) represent abstract meaning, or are they tied to how text is written? We investigate this question using Serbian digraphia as a controlled testbed: Serbian is written interchangeably in Latin and Cyrillic scripts with a near-perfect character mapping between them, enabling us to vary orthography while holding meaning exactly constant. Crucially, these scripts are tokenized completely differently, sharing no tokens whatsoever. Analyzing SAE feature activations across the Gemma model family (270M-27B parameters), we find that identical sentences in different Serbian scripts activate highly overlapping features, far exceeding random baselines. Strikingly, changing script causes less representational divergence than paraphrasing within the same script, suggesting SAE features prioritize meaning over orthographic form. Cross-script cross-paraphrase comparisons provide evidence against memorization, as these combinations rarely co-occur in training data yet still exhibit substantial feature overlap. This script invariance strengthens with model scale. Taken together, our findings suggest that SAE features can capture semantics at a level of abstraction above surface tokenization, and we propose Serbian digraphia as a general evaluation paradigm for probing the abstractness of learned representations.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether features learned by Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) encode abstract semantic meanings rather than surface orthography, using Serbian’s two interchangeable scripts as a controlled setting.
AI.motivation: It is unclear if SAE features in language models primarily track deep semantic content or shallow properties like spelling and tokenization. Because most languages lack a setting where meaning is constant but orthography changes systematically, it is hard to disentangle these factors. Serbian digraphia (Latin and Cyrillic scripts with near-perfect character mapping) offers a unique, controlled environment to test whether learned representations are invariant to script while holding meaning fixed.
AI.method: The authors use Serbian, which can be written in both Latin and Cyrillic with almost one-to-one character mapping, so that sentences can be exactly equivalent in meaning but differ completely in orthography and tokenization. They run Sparse Autoencoders on internal activations of Gemma language models of varying sizes (270M–27B parameters), then compare which SAE features activate for: (1) identical sentences in Latin vs Cyrillic; (2) paraphrases within the same script; and (3) cross-script, cross-paraphrase combinations that are unlikely to appear in training data. They quantify feature overlap and representational divergence relative to random baselines and study how these effects change with model scale.
AI.result: Identical sentences written in Latin vs Cyrillic scripts activate highly overlapping sets of SAE features, substantially more than expected by chance, even though the tokenization between scripts shares no tokens. Changing script leads to less change in feature activations than paraphrasing the sentence within the same script, indicating that the features are more sensitive to meaning variations than to orthographic changes. Cross-script, cross-paraphrase sentence pairs—combinations that almost certainly were not memorized from training—still show significant feature overlap, arguing against simple memorization as an explanation. The degree of script invariance and semantic abstraction increases with model size across the Gemma model family.
AI.conclusion: Sparse Autoencoders can learn features that are largely invariant to orthography and reflect semantic content at a level more abstract than surface tokenization. Serbian digraphia provides a powerful controlled testbed for probing this abstraction, and the observed script invariance—especially in larger models—supports the view that SAE-based interpretability can reveal genuinely semantic structure in language model representations rather than artifacts of spelling or tokenization. The paper proposes Serbian digraphia as a general paradigm for evaluating the abstractness of learned representations in multilingual and multi-script settings.

title: AgentOS: From Application Silos to a Natural Language-Driven Data Ecosystem
summary: The rapid emergence of open-source, locally hosted intelligent agents marks a critical inflection point in human-computer interaction. Systems such as OpenClaw demonstrate that Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents can autonomously operate local computing environments, orchestrate workflows, and integrate external tools. However, within the current paradigm, these agents remain conventional applications running on legacy operating systems originally designed for Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) or Command Line Interfaces (CLIs). This architectural mismatch leads to fragmented interaction models, poorly structured permission management (often described as "Shadow AI"), and severe context fragmentation. This paper proposes a new paradigm: a Personal Agent Operating System (AgentOS). In AgentOS, traditional GUI desktops are replaced by a Natural User Interface (NUI) centered on a unified natural language or voice portal. The system core becomes an Agent Kernel that interprets user intent, decomposes tasks, and coordinates multiple agents, while traditional applications evolve into modular Skills-as-Modules enabling users to compose software through natural language rules. We argue that realizing AgentOS fundamentally becomes a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD) problem. The Agent Kernel must operate as a real-time engine for intent mining and knowledge discovery. Viewed through this lens, the operating system becomes a continuous data mining pipeline involving sequential pattern mining for workflow automation, recommender systems for skill retrieval, and dynamically evolving personal knowledge graphs. These challenges define a new research agenda for the KDD community in building the next generation of intelligent computing systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AgentOS, a new operating system paradigm where an AI agent is the central interface and coordinator instead of traditional GUIs/CLIs.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agent systems run as apps on legacy OSes designed for GUIs/CLIs, causing fragmented interactions, weak and ad-hoc permission models ("Shadow AI"), and context fragmentation. There is a need for an OS architecture natively built around intelligent agents and natural language interaction.
AI.method: Conceptual and architectural proposal: define a Personal Agent Operating System (AgentOS) whose core is an Agent Kernel that interprets user intent, decomposes tasks, and coordinates multiple agents. Replace the GUI desktop with a Natural User Interface based on natural language or voice, and reframe traditional applications as modular Skills-as-Modules that can be composed via natural language rules. Reinterpret the OS as a continuous KDD pipeline involving intent mining, workflow pattern mining, recommender systems, and evolving personal knowledge graphs.
AI.result: The paper does not report empirical results; instead, it articulates the architectural components of AgentOS and maps them to specific KDD problems like sequential pattern mining for workflow automation, recommendation for skill selection, and knowledge graph construction for personalization.
AI.conclusion: Building an AgentOS is fundamentally a Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining challenge. The proposed paradigm shifts the OS into a real-time data mining and intent understanding engine and outlines a new research agenda for the KDD community to develop next-generation intelligent computing systems centered on AI agents.

title: MultiGraSCCo: A Multilingual Anonymization Benchmark with Annotations of Personal Identifiers
summary: Accessing sensitive patient data for machine learning is challenging due to privacy concerns. Datasets with annotations of personally identifiable information are crucial for developing and testing anonymization systems to enable safe data sharing that complies with privacy regulations. Since accessing real patient data is a bottleneck, synthetic data offers an efficient solution for data scarcity, bypassing privacy regulations that apply to real data. Moreover, neural machine translation can help to create high-quality data for low-resource languages by translating validated real or synthetic data from a high-resource language. In this work, we create a multilingual anonymization benchmark in ten languages, using a machine translation methodology that preserves the original annotations and renders names of cities and people in a culturally and contextually appropriate form in each target language. Our evaluation study with medical professionals confirms the quality of the translations, both in general and with respect to the translation and adaptation of personal information. Our benchmark with over 2,500 annotations of personal information can be used in many applications, including training annotators, validating annotations across institutions without legal complications, and helping improve the performance of automatic personal information detection. We make our benchmark and annotation guidelines available for further research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multilingual benchmark dataset for anonymizing medical text, created via privacy-preserving machine translation and synthetic data, to support development and evaluation of personal information detection systems across ten languages.
AI.motivation: Obtaining real patient data with annotated personally identifiable information is difficult due to privacy regulations, which limits the development and evaluation of anonymization and PII-detection systems, especially for low-resource languages. There is a need for multilingual, legally unproblematic benchmarks and guidelines that can be shared across institutions and used to train models and human annotators.
AI.method: The authors generate synthetic and translated medical text with annotated personal information. They use neural machine translation to transfer English (or other high-resource language) data into ten target languages while preserving the span-level annotations of PII. Their MT methodology explicitly adapts personal names and locations to be culturally and contextually appropriate in each language. They then have medical professionals evaluate the translations and the handling of personal information. Finally, they package the resulting multilingual corpus and annotation guidelines as a benchmark.
AI.result: They produce a multilingual anonymization benchmark covering ten languages with more than 2,500 PII annotations. Professional evaluation confirms that the translations are of high quality overall and accurately translate and adapt personal information while preserving the annotation structure. The dataset and guidelines are released for research use.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic and machine-translated medical texts can be used to build high-quality, privacy-safe multilingual benchmarks for PII detection and anonymization. The released benchmark and guidelines facilitate training annotators, cross-institutional validation without legal barriers, and the improvement of automatic personal information detection systems across multiple languages.

title: A Consensus-Driven Multi-LLM Pipeline for Missing-Person Investigations
comment: Accepted to CAC: Applied Computing & Automation Conferences 2026. 16 pages, 6 figures
summary: The first 72 hours of a missing-person investigation are critical for successful recovery. Guardian is an end-to-end system designed to support missing-child investigation and early search planning. This paper presents the Guardian LLM Pipeline, a multi-model system in which LLMs are used for intelligent information extraction and processing related to missing-person search operations. The pipeline coordinates end-to-end execution across task-specialized LLM models and invokes a consensus LLM engine that compares multiple model outputs and resolves disagreements. The pipeline is further strengthened by QLoRA-based fine-tuning, using curated datasets. The presented design aligns with prior work on weak supervision and LLM-assisted annotation, emphasizing conservative, auditable use of LLMs as structured extractors and labelers rather than unconstrained end-to-end decision makers.

AI.tldr: Guardian is an end-to-end, multi-LLM pipeline designed to support early-stage missing-child investigations by extracting and structuring information to guide search planning.
AI.motivation: The first 72 hours in a missing-person case are crucial, yet investigators lack robust, systematic tools that can quickly process and structure large volumes of heterogeneous information for search planning. Guardian aims to fill this gap for missing-child cases.
AI.method: The paper proposes the Guardian LLM Pipeline, a coordinated, multi-model system in which task-specialized LLMs perform information extraction and processing for search operations. A consensus LLM engine compares outputs from different models and resolves disagreements, while QLoRA-based fine-tuning with curated datasets improves reliability and domain adaptation. The design follows principles of weak supervision and LLM-assisted annotation, constraining LLMs to act as structured extractors and labelers instead of autonomous decision-makers.
AI.result: The result is an LLM-based pipeline that can execute end-to-end information processing workflows for missing-child search planning, with improved robustness and auditability through consensus mechanisms and fine-tuning. It operationalizes weak supervision and annotation support to produce structured, reliable outputs from unstructured case information.
AI.conclusion: Guardian demonstrates that a carefully orchestrated, multi-LLM pipeline—augmented with consensus resolution and QLoRA fine-tuning—can support early-stage missing-child investigations by providing conservative, auditable information extraction and structured labeling rather than opaque, end-to-end decision-making.

title: ConFu: Contemplate the Future for Better Speculative Sampling
comment: accepted at ICLR 2026 workshop on Latent & Implicit Thinking - Going Beyond CoT Reasoning
summary: Speculative decoding has emerged as a powerful approach to accelerate large language model (LLM) inference by employing lightweight draft models to propose candidate tokens that are subsequently verified by the target model. The effectiveness of this paradigm critically depends on the quality of the draft model. While recent advances such as the EAGLE series achieve state-of-the-art speedup, existing draft models remain limited by error accumulation: they condition only on the current prefix, causing their predictions to drift from the target model over steps. In this work, we propose \textbf{ConFu} (Contemplate the Future), a novel speculative decoding framework that enables draft models to anticipate the future direction of generation. ConFu introduces (i) contemplate tokens and soft prompts that allow the draft model to leverage future-oriented signals from the target model at negligible cost, (ii) a dynamic contemplate token mechanism with MoE to enable context-aware future prediction, and (iii) a training framework with anchor token sampling and future prediction replication that learns robust future prediction. Experiments demonstrate that ConFu improves token acceptance rates and generation speed over EAGLE-3 by 8--11% across various downstream tasks with Llama-3 3B and 8B models. We believe our work is the first to bridge speculative decoding with continuous reasoning tokens, offering a new direction for accelerating LLM inference.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ConFu, a speculative decoding framework that lets draft models anticipate future tokens using cheap future-oriented signals from the target LLM, reducing error accumulation and improving speed over prior methods like EAGLE-3.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by having a small draft model propose tokens that a large target model then verifies. However, draft models typically condition only on the current prefix. Over multiple steps, their predictions drift from the target model due to error accumulation, limiting token acceptance rates and overall speedup. Existing state-of-the-art systems like EAGLE-3 still suffer from this prefix-only limitation, motivating a method that gives the draft model access to some notion of the target model's future direction without significantly increasing computation cost.
AI.method: ConFu (Contemplate the Future) augments speculative decoding with future-aware signals. (i) It introduces "contemplate tokens" and soft prompts, inexpensive auxiliary tokens whose representations are derived from the target model and injected into the draft model so it can align with the target model’s anticipated future generation. (ii) It uses a dynamic contemplate token mechanism combined with a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design so that the number and configuration of contemplate tokens adapt to the context, enabling more accurate, context-aware future prediction. (iii) It defines a training framework with anchor token sampling, where specific positions in the sequence are chosen as anchors, and future prediction replication, where the draft model is trained to replicate the target model’s future-oriented behavior at those anchors, thereby learning robust future prediction capabilities and reducing drift during inference.
AI.result: Across multiple downstream tasks evaluated with Llama-3 3B and 8B target models, ConFu achieves 8–11% improvements in token acceptance rates and generation speed compared to the strong EAGLE-3 baseline. These gains hold across different tasks, indicating that the method consistently reduces error accumulation and improves speculative decoding efficiency without sacrificing output quality.
AI.conclusion: ConFu successfully integrates continuous future-oriented reasoning signals into speculative decoding, allowing draft models to better align with the target model’s trajectory and thereby increasing acceptance rates and speed. The framework demonstrates that lightweight future prediction via contemplate tokens and context-aware mechanisms can meaningfully mitigate drift in speculative decoding. The authors position this as the first explicit bridge between speculative decoding and continuous reasoning tokens, suggesting a promising new direction for further acceleration and refinement of LLM inference methods.

title: The FABRIC Strategy for Verifying Neural Feedback Systems
summary: Forward reachability analysis is a dominant approach for verifying reach-avoid specifications in neural feedback systems, i.e., dynamical systems controlled by neural networks, and a number of directions have been proposed and studied. In contrast, far less attention has been given to backward reachability analysis for these systems, in part because of the limited scalability of known techniques. In this work, we begin to address this gap by introducing new algorithms for computing both over- and underapproximations of backward reachable sets for nonlinear neural feedback systems. We also describe and implement an integration of these backward reachability techniques with existing ones for forward analysis. We call the resulting algorithm Forward and Backward Reachability Integration for Certification (FaBRIC). We evaluate our algorithms on a representative set of benchmarks and show that they significantly outperform the prior state of the art.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FaBRIC, a method that integrates new backward reachability algorithms with existing forward reachability techniques to verify neural feedback systems more efficiently.
AI.motivation: Most existing verification work for neural feedback systems focuses on forward reachability for reach-avoid specifications. Backward reachability, which can more directly reason about which initial states lead to unsafe states, has seen limited use due to poor scalability of prior techniques. The authors aim to fill this gap and make backward analysis practical and scalable.
AI.method: They design new algorithms to compute both over-approximations and under-approximations of backward reachable sets for nonlinear systems controlled by neural networks. These algorithms are then combined with standard forward reachability methods into a unified framework called FaBRIC (Forward and Backward Reachability Integration for Certification).
AI.result: On a set of representative benchmarks, the proposed algorithms and the FaBRIC framework significantly outperform previous state-of-the-art methods for verifying neural feedback systems, in terms of scalability and/or verification power.
AI.conclusion: Backward reachability, when equipped with suitable over- and under-approximation algorithms and integrated with forward analysis, becomes a powerful and scalable tool for certifying neural feedback systems. The FaBRIC framework demonstrates that combining forward and backward reasoning leads to substantial performance gains over existing methods.

title: SciTaRC: Benchmarking QA on Scientific Tabular Data that Requires Language Reasoning and Complex Computation
summary: We introduce SciTaRC, an expert-authored benchmark of questions about tabular data in scientific papers requiring both deep language reasoning and complex computation. We show that current state-of-the-art AI models fail on at least 23% of these questions, a gap that remains significant even for highly capable open-weight models like Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, which fails on 65.5% of the tasks. Our analysis reveals a universal "execution bottleneck": both code and language models struggle to faithfully execute plans, even when provided with correct strategies. Specifically, code-based methods prove brittle on raw scientific tables, while natural language reasoning primarily fails due to initial comprehension issues and calculation errors.

AI.tldr: SciTaRC is a benchmark of expert-authored questions about scientific tables that tests deep language reasoning and complex computation, revealing significant failure rates and an execution bottleneck in current AI models.
AI.motivation: To rigorously evaluate and stress-test modern AI models on real-world, challenging tasks involving scientific tables, where both nuanced language understanding and nontrivial numerical/computational skills are required. Existing benchmarks underrepresent this combination of skills and may overestimate model capabilities.
AI.method: The authors construct SciTaRC, a benchmark consisting of expert-authored questions grounded in tabular data from scientific papers. They evaluate state-of-the-art AI systems, including code-based approaches and advanced language models such as Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, and perform error analysis to categorize where and why the models fail.
AI.result: State-of-the-art models fail on at least 23% of SciTaRC questions, with Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct failing on 65.5% of tasks. The evaluation and error analysis reveal that both code and language models share an 'execution bottleneck': even when the high-level reasoning strategy is correct, models often fail at faithfully executing the required steps. Code-based methods are brittle on raw scientific tables, while language models frequently suffer from miscomprehension and calculation errors.
AI.conclusion: SciTaRC exposes a fundamental execution bottleneck in current AI models on tasks requiring integrated table understanding, language reasoning, and computation. Robust progress will require methods that can better interpret raw scientific tables and reliably carry out multi-step reasoning and calculations, rather than just improving high-level planning or model scale.

title: Meissa: Multi-modal Medical Agentic Intelligence
summary: Multi-modal large language models (MM-LLMs) have shown strong performance in medical image understanding and clinical reasoning. Recent medical agent systems extend them with tool use and multi-agent collaboration, enabling complex decision-making. However, these systems rely almost entirely on frontier models (e.g., GPT), whose API-based deployment incurs high cost, high latency, and privacy risks that conflict with on-premise clinical requirements. We present Meissa, a lightweight 4B-parameter medical MM-LLM that brings agentic capability offline. Instead of imitating static answers, Meissa learns both when to engage external interaction (strategy selection) and how to execute multi-step interaction (strategy execution) by distilling structured trajectories from frontier models. Specifically, we propose: (1) Unified trajectory modeling: trajectories (reasoning and action traces) are represented within a single state-action-observation formalism, allowing one model to generalize across heterogeneous medical environments. (2) Three-tier stratified supervision: the model's own errors trigger progressive escalation from direct reasoning to tool-augmented and multi-agent interaction, explicitly learning difficulty-aware strategy selection. (3) Prospective-retrospective supervision: pairing exploratory forward traces with hindsight-rationalized execution traces enables stable learning of effective interaction policies. Trained on 40K curated trajectories, Meissa matches or exceeds proprietary frontier agents in 10 of 16 evaluation settings across 13 medical benchmarks spanning radiology, pathology, and clinical reasoning. Using over 25x fewer parameters than typical frontier models like Gemini-3, Meissa operates fully offline with 22x lower end-to-end latency compared to API-based deployment. Data, models, and environments are released at https://github.com/Schuture/Meissa.

AI.tldr: Meissa is a compact 4B-parameter offline medical multi-modal LLM that learns agent-like tool use and interaction strategies via trajectory distillation, matching or surpassing frontier API-based agents on many medical benchmarks with much lower cost and latency.
AI.motivation: Existing medical agent systems with strong image understanding and clinical reasoning depend on large proprietary frontier models accessed via APIs, which are expensive, slow, and problematic for privacy and on-premise clinical deployment. There is a need for an efficient, smaller, on-device medical MM-LLM that can still perform complex, tool-using, multi-step decision-making like frontier agent systems.
AI.method: The authors design Meissa, a 4B-parameter medical MM-LLM trained via trajectory distillation from frontier models. They introduce: (1) Unified trajectory modeling, representing reasoning and action traces within a single state-action-observation framework so one model can operate across diverse medical environments; (2) Three-tier stratified supervision, where the model’s own failures trigger escalation from pure reasoning to tool-augmented and then multi-agent interaction, teaching difficulty-aware strategy selection; (3) Prospective-retrospective supervision, combining exploratory forward trajectories with hindsight-rationalized execution traces to stabilize and improve learning of interaction policies. They train Meissa on 40K carefully curated trajectories involving tools and multi-agent interactions across medical tasks.
AI.result: Meissa, with only 4B parameters, matches or outperforms large proprietary frontier medical agents in 10 of 16 evaluation scenarios over 13 benchmarks that include radiology, pathology, and clinical reasoning tasks. It operates completely offline and achieves about 22x lower end-to-end latency than typical API-based deployments using models like Gemini-3, while using over 25x fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: Agentic capabilities such as tool use and multi-step interaction can be effectively distilled into a compact 4B-parameter medical MM-LLM, enabling offline, privacy-preserving, low-latency deployment suitable for clinical environments. The unified trajectory framework and stratified, prospective-retrospective supervision strategy are key to transferring and stabilizing these capabilities, and the released code, data, and environments enable further research in lightweight medical agent systems.

title: Automated Thematic Analysis for Clinical Qualitative Data: Iterative Codebook Refinement with Full Provenance
comment: Submitted to AMIA 2026 Annual Symposium (American Medical Informatics Association)
summary: Thematic analysis (TA) is widely used in health research to extract patterns from patient interviews, yet manual TA faces challenges in scalability and reproducibility. LLM-based automation can help, but existing approaches produce codebooks with limited generalizability and lack analytic auditability. We present an automated TA framework combining iterative codebook refinement with full provenance tracking. Evaluated on five corpora spanning clinical interviews, social media, and public transcripts, the framework achieves the highest composite quality score on four of five datasets compared to six baselines. Iterative refinement yields statistically significant improvements on four datasets with large effect sizes, driven by gains in code reusability and distributional consistency while preserving descriptive quality. On two clinical corpora (pediatric cardiology), generated themes align with expert-annotated themes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated, LLM-based thematic analysis framework that iteratively refines codebooks while tracking full analytic provenance, leading to higher-quality, more reusable, and more consistent themes across multiple health-related text corpora.
AI.motivation: Manual thematic analysis of health-related text (e.g., patient interviews) is labor-intensive, difficult to scale, and can suffer from poor reproducibility. Existing LLM-based automation helps but often produces codebooks that do not generalize well across datasets and lack transparency and auditability of how analytic decisions were made. There is a need for an automated TA method that both improves the quality and generalizability of themes and provides a clear, traceable analytic process suitable for rigorous health research.
AI.method: The authors develop an automated TA framework built around large language models that (1) generates an initial codebook, then (2) iteratively refines that codebook, using performance feedback to improve properties such as reusability and consistency, and (3) records full provenance of analytic steps so that all decisions and transformations can be audited. They evaluate their framework on five diverse text corpora—clinical interviews, social media data, and public transcripts—and compare it to six baseline automated or semi-automated TA approaches using a composite quality metric encompassing multiple aspects of code and theme quality.
AI.result: Across the five datasets, the proposed framework attains the best composite quality score on four out of five when compared to six baselines. Iterative codebook refinement leads to statistically significant quality improvements in four datasets, with large effect sizes, mainly by enhancing code reusability and ensuring more consistent code distributions across the data, while keeping descriptive quality at least as good as baselines. On two pediatric cardiology clinical interview corpora, the automatically generated themes closely match expert-annotated themes, indicating good alignment with domain experts’ judgments.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that an LLM-based TA framework with iterative codebook refinement and rigorous provenance tracking can outperform existing automated approaches in terms of overall theme quality, generalizability, and consistency, without sacrificing descriptive richness. The alignment with expert themes on clinical corpora suggests the method is suitable for real-world health research settings, offering a scalable, auditable alternative to fully manual thematic analysis.

title: MEMO: Memory-Augmented Model Context Optimization for Robust Multi-Turn Multi-Agent LLM Games
summary: Multi-turn, multi-agent LLM game evaluations often exhibit substantial run-to-run variance. In long-horizon interactions, small early deviations compound across turns and are amplified by multi-agent coupling. This biases win rate estimates and makes rankings unreliable across repeated tournaments. Prompt choice worsens this further by producing different effective policies. We address both instability and underperformance with MEMO (Memory-augmented MOdel context optimization), a self-play framework that optimizes inference-time context by coupling retention and exploration. Retention maintains a persistent memory bank that stores structured insights from self-play trajectories and injects them as priors during later play. Exploration runs tournament-style prompt evolution with uncertainty-aware selection via TrueSkill, and uses prioritized replay to revisit rare and decisive states. Across five text-based games, MEMO raises mean win rate from 25.1% to 49.5% for GPT-4o-mini and from 20.9% to 44.3% for Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, using $2,000$ self-play games per task. Run-to-run variance also drops, giving more stable rankings across prompt variations. These results suggest that multi-agent LLM game performance and robustness have substantial room for improvement through context optimization. MEMO achieves the largest gains in negotiation and imperfect-information games, while RL remains more effective in perfect-information settings.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MEMO, a self-play framework that stabilizes and improves multi-turn, multi-agent LLM game performance by optimizing inference-time context through memory and prompt evolution.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent, multi-turn LLM game evaluations suffer from high run-to-run variance and unreliable rankings due to compounding stochasticity over long horizons, multi-agent coupling, and sensitivity to prompt choice. This instability biases win-rate estimates and obscures true performance differences between models and prompts. The authors aim to develop a method that both boosts win rates and yields more stable, reproducible evaluations without retraining the base models.
AI.method: The authors propose MEMO (Memory-augmented MOdel context optimization), a self-play framework that operates purely at inference time. It has two key components: (1) Retention: a persistent memory bank that stores structured insights extracted from self-play trajectories and later injects these as priors into the model’s context for subsequent games, effectively shaping behavior through curated experience. (2) Exploration: a tournament-style prompt evolution procedure where multiple prompts compete, are scored via TrueSkill with uncertainty-aware selection, and are refined over iterations. Prioritized replay is used to revisit rare and decisive game states during self-play, allowing the system to better explore and learn from critical situations. Together, retention and exploration iteratively optimize the context and prompts without changing model parameters.
AI.result: Across five text-based games, MEMO significantly improves performance and stability for different LLMs. For GPT-4o-mini, the mean win rate increases from 25.1% to 49.5%; for Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct, it rises from 20.9% to 44.3%, using 2,000 self-play games per task. Additionally, run-to-run variance in win rates and rankings across prompt variations is notably reduced, yielding more reliable and reproducible tournament outcomes. The largest performance gains are observed in negotiation and imperfect-information games, while in perfect-information settings, reinforcement learning methods still outperform MEMO.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing inference-time context via MEMO—combining memory-augmented retention with prompt evolution-based exploration—can substantially improve both the effectiveness and robustness of multi-agent LLM play in text-based games, without modifying model weights. The approach reduces evaluation noise, provides more stable rankings, and is particularly beneficial in negotiation and imperfect-information environments, suggesting that there remains considerable untapped potential in context-level optimization for multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Learning When to Sample: Confidence-Aware Self-Consistency for Efficient LLM Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong reasoning performance through chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, yet often generate unnecessarily long reasoning paths that incur high inference cost. Recent self-consistency-based approaches further improve accuracy but require sampling and aggregating multiple reasoning trajectories, leading to substantial additional computational overhead. This paper introduces a confidence-aware decision framework that analyzes a single completed reasoning trajectory to adaptively select between single-path and multi-path reasoning. The framework is trained using sentence-level numeric and linguistic features extracted from intermediate reasoning states in the MedQA dataset and generalizes effectively to MathQA, MedMCQA, and MMLU without additional fine-tuning. Experimental results show that the proposed method maintains accuracy comparable to multi-path baselines while using up to 80\% fewer tokens. These findings demonstrate that reasoning trajectories contain rich signals for uncertainty estimation, enabling a simple, transferable mechanism to balance accuracy and efficiency in LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: Uses features from a single chain-of-thought trajectory to decide when to stick with one reasoning path vs. invoke multi-path self-consistency, preserving accuracy with far fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: LLMs with chain-of-thought can reason well but often produce overly long explanations, making inference expensive. Self-consistency improves accuracy further by sampling many reasoning paths, but this amplifies token cost. There is a need for a method that preserves the accuracy benefits of multi-path reasoning while significantly reducing computational overhead by intelligently deciding when extra reasoning is actually necessary.
AI.method: Propose a confidence-aware decision framework that inspects intermediate steps of a single completed CoT trajectory. From these steps, extract sentence-level numeric and linguistic features that act as uncertainty signals. Train a classifier on MedQA to predict, per question, whether single-path reasoning is sufficient or if multi-path sampling should be used. At inference time, run one CoT, compute these features, and adaptively select single-path or multi-path reasoning based on predicted confidence, without further fine-tuning on new benchmarks.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks (MedQA, MathQA, MedMCQA, MMLU), the method achieves accuracy close to that of full multi-path self-consistency baselines while reducing token usage by up to 80%. It generalizes from MedQA training data to other datasets without additional fine-tuning, indicating robustness and transferability of the confidence signals from CoT trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Single CoT trajectories embed rich information about model uncertainty. By exploiting this via a simple feature-based confidence estimator, one can dynamically trade off between single- and multi-path reasoning, maintaining near-best accuracy with drastically lower inference cost. This yields a practical, transferable mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM reasoning systems.

title: Time, Identity and Consciousness in Language Model Agents
comment: Accepted at AAAI 2026 Spring Symposium - Machine Consciousness: Integrating Theory, Technology, and Philosophy
summary: Machine consciousness evaluations mostly see behavior. For language model agents that behavior is language and tool use. That lets an agent say the right things about itself even when the constraints that should make those statements matter are not jointly present at decision time. We apply Stack Theory's temporal gap to scaffold trajectories. This separates ingredient-wise occurrence within an evaluation window from co-instantiation at a single objective step. We then instantiate Stack Theory's Arpeggio and Chord postulates on grounded identity statements. This yields two persistence scores that can be computed from instrumented scaffold traces. We connect these scores to five operational identity metrics and map common scaffolds into an identity morphospace that exposes predictable tradeoffs. The result is a conservative toolkit for identity evaluation. It separates talking like a stable self from being organized like one.

AI.tldr: They propose a way to test whether an AI agent’s ‘identity’ is genuinely persistent rather than just talk, by measuring how consistently its self-related claims stay grounded across time steps in a scaffolded setup.
AI.motivation: Existing machine consciousness and identity evaluations mostly rely on observing behavior (for LLM agents, language and tool use). Because LLM agents can say the ‘right’ things about themselves without the underlying constraints being jointly active at decision time, current tests risk confusing fluent self-talk with actual structural or organizational persistence of identity. The authors want a more conservative, structurally grounded way to evaluate identity that is robust to this temporal mismatch.
AI.method: They use Stack Theory’s notion of a temporal gap to design scaffolded agent trajectories, distinguishing between ingredients that appear somewhere in an evaluation window and those that are co-instantiated at a specific step. Within this framework, they instantiate Stack Theory’s Arpeggio and Chord postulates on grounded identity statements produced during instrumented scaffolds. From the resulting traces, they define and compute two quantitative persistence scores. They then relate these scores to five operational identity metrics and embed different scaffold designs into an ‘identity morphospace’ that characterizes systematic tradeoffs across setups.
AI.result: They obtain two persistence scores derived from scaffold traces that capture different aspects of identity stability over time, and show how these relate to five operational identity metrics. By placing common scaffold architectures into the proposed identity morphospace, they reveal predictable tradeoffs (e.g., between flexibility and stability, or expressive richness and structural consistency) in how identity is manifested and measured.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a conservative toolkit for evaluating identity in language-model-based agents. It provides a principled way to distinguish agents that merely talk like they have a stable self from those whose internal organization and behavior over time actually exhibit identity-like persistence, thus tightening the link between self-descriptions and underlying structure.

title: EPOCH: An Agentic Protocol for Multi-Round System Optimization
summary: Autonomous agents are increasingly used to improve prompts, code, and machine learning systems through iterative execution and feedback. Yet existing approaches are usually designed as task-specific optimization loops rather than as a unified protocol for establishing baselines and managing tracked multi-round self-improvement. We introduce EPOCH, an engineering protocol for multi-round system optimization in heterogeneous environments. EPOCH organizes optimization into two phases: baseline construction and iterative self-improvement. It further structures each round through role-constrained stages that separate planning, implementation, and evaluation, and standardizes execution through canonical command interfaces and round-level tracking. This design enables coordinated optimization across prompts, model configurations, code, and rule-based components while preserving stability, reproducibility, traceability, and integrity of evaluation. Empirical studies in various tasks illustrate the practicality of EPOCH for production-oriented autonomous improvement workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper presents EPOCH, a unified engineering protocol for multi-round optimization of autonomous AI systems, structuring how prompts, code, and model configurations are iteratively improved with traceable, reproducible workflows.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents are widely used to iteratively improve prompts, code, and ML systems, but current methods are usually ad-hoc, task-specific optimization loops. There is a lack of a general, standardized protocol to establish baselines, manage multi-round self-improvement, and ensure stability and traceability across heterogeneous components.
AI.method: The authors propose EPOCH, which organizes optimization into two main phases: (1) baseline construction, and (2) iterative self-improvement. Each optimization round is decomposed into role-constrained stages that explicitly separate planning, implementation, and evaluation. EPOCH also defines canonical command interfaces and round-level tracking mechanisms so different components (prompts, models, code, rules) can be optimized in a coordinated but controlled manner.
AI.result: Through empirical studies across multiple tasks, the authors show that EPOCH can be practically applied in real-world, production-oriented workflows for autonomous system improvement. The protocol supports coordinated optimization of diverse components while maintaining stability and evaluation integrity.
AI.conclusion: EPOCH offers a unified, structured protocol for multi-round optimization of autonomous AI systems, addressing fragmentation and ad-hoc design in current approaches. By enforcing phased optimization, role separation, standardized interfaces, and tracking, it enables more stable, reproducible, and traceable self-improvement processes that work across heterogeneous system components.

title: Bioalignment: Measuring and Improving LLM Disposition Toward Biological Systems for AI Safety
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on internet-scale corpora can exhibit systematic biases that increase the probability of unwanted behavior. In this study, we examined potential biases towards synthetic vs. biological technological solutions across four domains (materials, energy, manufacturing, and algorithms). A sample of 5 frontier and 5 open-weight models were measured using 50 curated Bioalignment prompts with a Kelly criterion-inspired evaluation framework. According to this metric, most models were not bioaligned in that they exhibit biases in favor of synthetic (non-biological) solutions. We next examined if fine-tuning could increase the preferences of two open-weight models, Llama 3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, for biological-based approaches. A curated corpus of ~22M tokens from 6,636 PMC articles emphasizing biological problem-solving was used first to fine-tune Llama 3B with a mixed corpus of continued training and instruction-formatted. This was then extended to Qwen 3B using instruction-formatted only. We found that QLoRA fine-tuning significantly increased the scoring of biological solutions for both models without degrading general capabilities (Holm-Bonferroni-corrected p < 0.001 and p < 0.01, respectively). This suggests that even a small amount of fine-tuning can change how models weigh the relative value of biological and bioinspired vs. synthetic approaches. Although this work focused on small open-weight LLMs, it may be extensible to much larger models and could be used to develop models that favor bio-based approaches. We release the benchmark, corpus, code, and adapter weights.

AI.tldr: The paper studies bias in large language models (LLMs) toward synthetic (non-biological) vs. biological technological solutions and shows that light fine-tuning can shift models to favor biological approaches without hurting overall performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained on broad internet data may systematically favor certain types of solutions, in this case synthetic technologies, which is undesirable when one wants models that recognize and promote the value of biological and bioinspired solutions in areas like materials, energy, manufacturing, and algorithms.
AI.method: The authors create a benchmark of 50 "Bioalignment" prompts spanning four application domains and evaluate 5 frontier and 5 open-weight LLMs using a Kelly-criterion-inspired scoring framework to quantify preferences for synthetic vs. biological solutions. They then fine-tune two small open-weight models (Llama 3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) using QLoRA on a ~22M-token corpus of 6,636 PMC articles emphasizing biological problem-solving, experimenting with mixed continued-training plus instruction tuning for Llama and instruction-only for Qwen, and compare bioalignment scores and general capabilities before and after tuning, using Holm–Bonferroni-corrected significance tests.
AI.result: Most evaluated models initially show a bias toward synthetic solutions, failing the authors’ bioalignment criterion. After QLoRA fine-tuning on the biology-focused corpus, both Llama 3B and Qwen 3B significantly increase their preference scores for biological solutions (with strong statistical significance: p < 0.001 and p < 0.01, respectively) while maintaining general capabilities on other tasks.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ implicit preferences for synthetic vs. biological technological solutions are measurable and systematically skewed toward synthetic approaches, but can be substantially shifted toward biological and bioinspired solutions through relatively small-scale, targeted fine-tuning without notable performance degradation. The proposed benchmark and released resources could support building larger, more bioaligned models that prioritize or better recognize biological approaches across technical domains.

title: From Days to Minutes: An Autonomous AI Agent Achieves Reliable Clinical Triage in Remote Patient Monitoring
comment: 46 pages, 11 figures, Abstract in metadata is shortened to meet arXiv character limits; see PDF for full version
summary:
Background: Remote patient monitoring (RPM) generates vast data, yet landmark trials (Tele-HF, BEAT-HF) failed because data volume overwhelmed clinical staff. While TIM-HF2 showed 24/7 physician-led monitoring reduces mortality by 30%, this model remains prohibitively expensive and unscalable.
  Methods: We developed Sentinel, an autonomous AI agent using Model Context Protocol (MCP) for contextual triage of RPM vitals via 21 clinical tools and multi-step reasoning. Evaluation included: (1) self-consistency (100 readings x 5 runs); (2) comparison against rule-based thresholds; and (3) validation against 6 clinicians (3 physicians, 3 NPs) using a connected matrix design. A leave-one-out (LOO) analysis compared the agent against individual clinicians; severe overtriage cases underwent independent physician adjudication.
  Results: Against a human majority-vote standard (N=467), the agent achieved 95.8% emergency sensitivity and 88.5% sensitivity for all actionable alerts (85.7% specificity). Four-level exact accuracy was 69.4% (quadratic-weighted kappa=0.778); 95.9% of classifications were within one severity level. In LOO analysis, the agent outperformed every clinician in emergency sensitivity (97.5% vs. 60.0% aggregate) and actionable sensitivity (90.9% vs. 69.5%). While disagreements skewed toward overtriage (22.5%), independent adjudication of severe gaps (>=2 levels) validated agent escalation in 88-94% of cases; consensus resolution validated 100%. The agent showed near-perfect self-consistency (kappa=0.850). Median cost was $0.34/triage.
  Conclusions: Sentinel triages RPM vitals with sensitivity exceeding individual clinicians. By automating systematic context synthesis, Sentinel addresses the core limitation of prior RPM trials, offering a scalable path toward the intensive monitoring shown to reduce mortality while maintaining a clinically defensible overtriage profile.

AI.tldr: An AI agent (Sentinel) autonomously triages remote patient monitoring vitals, matching or exceeding clinician performance with high sensitivity, consistency, and low cost, enabling scalable intensive monitoring.
AI.motivation: Remote patient monitoring for heart failure produces large volumes of vital-sign data that previously overwhelmed clinical staff, leading to failed major trials despite evidence (TIM-HF2) that continuous physician-led monitoring can significantly reduce mortality. There is a need for a scalable, affordable system that can safely triage these data without requiring 24/7 physician oversight.
AI.method: The authors built Sentinel, an autonomous AI agent that uses the Model Context Protocol (MCP) to orchestrate 21 clinical tools and perform multi-step reasoning to contextually triage RPM vital-sign readings into graded severity levels. They evaluated Sentinel via: (1) a self-consistency test over repeated runs on the same 100 readings; (2) performance comparison to simple rule-based threshold alerts; and (3) head-to-head comparison with six clinicians (three physicians and three nurse practitioners) using a connected matrix design. A leave-one-out analysis compared the agent to each clinician individually, and independent physician adjudication was used for severe overtriage disagreements.
AI.result: Against a human majority vote reference over 467 cases, Sentinel achieved 95.8% sensitivity for emergencies, 88.5% sensitivity and 85.7% specificity for all actionable alerts, and 69.4% exact four-level severity accuracy with a quadratic-weighted kappa of 0.778 and 95.9% of classifications within one severity level. In leave-one-out analysis, the agent outperformed all clinicians in emergency sensitivity (97.5% vs 60.0% aggregate) and actionable sensitivity (90.9% vs 69.5%). Disagreements were mostly overtriage (22.5%), and for cases where the agent’s severity was ≥2 levels higher, independent adjudication confirmed escalation in 88–94% of cases, with full validation after consensus resolution. Self-consistency was near perfect (kappa=0.850), and the median cost was $0.34 per triage.
AI.conclusion: Sentinel can autonomously triage RPM vital data with higher sensitivity than individual clinicians and strong agreement with expert consensus, while being inexpensive and highly consistent. By automating context-rich interpretation of vitals, it directly addresses the scalability barrier that undermined prior RPM trials and offers a clinically defensible, overtriage-leaning approach that could enable broad, cost-effective deployment of intensive remote monitoring associated with reduced mortality.

title: DuplexCascade: Full-Duplex Speech-to-Speech Dialogue with VAD-Free Cascaded ASR-LLM-TTS Pipeline and Micro-Turn Optimization
summary: Spoken dialog systems with cascaded ASR-LLM-TTS modules retain strong LLM intelligence, but VAD segmentation often forces half-duplex turns and brittle control. On the other hand, VAD-free end-to-end model support full-duplex interaction but is hard to maintain conversational intelligence. In this paper, we present DuplexCascade, a VAD-free cascaded streaming pipeline for full-duplex speech-to-speech dialogue. Our key idea is to convert conventional utterance-wise long turns into chunk-wise micro-turn interactions, enabling rapid bidirectional exchange while preserving the strengths of a capable text LLM. To reliably coordinate turn-taking and response timing, we introduce a set of conversational special control tokens that steer the LLM's behavior under streaming constraints. On Full-DuplexBench and VoiceBench, DuplexCascade delivers state-of-the-art full-duplex turn-taking and strong conversational intelligence among open-source speech-to-speech dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: DuplexCascade is a VAD-free, full-duplex speech-to-speech dialogue system that keeps strong LLM intelligence by turning long utterances into micro-turns and controlling the LLM with special conversational tokens, achieving state-of-the-art turn-taking and dialog quality.
AI.motivation: Existing cascaded ASR-LLM-TTS systems are intelligent but rely on VAD segmentation, which leads to half-duplex interaction and rigid, brittle control of turn-taking. End-to-end VAD-free systems can be full-duplex but struggle to match the conversational intelligence of strong text LLMs. There is a need for a system that combines full-duplex, low-latency interaction with the reasoning and dialog abilities of powerful text LLMs.
AI.method: They propose DuplexCascade, a VAD-free cascaded streaming pipeline for speech-to-speech dialog. Instead of long, utterance-level turns, it segments the interaction into small chunk-level micro-turns that are processed continuously. They design and use special conversational control tokens in the LLM prompts/outputs to coordinate turn-taking, response timing, and behavior under streaming constraints, while still using a capable text LLM at the core.
AI.result: On benchmarks Full-DuplexBench and VoiceBench, DuplexCascade achieves state-of-the-art performance in full-duplex turn-taking and maintains strong conversational intelligence compared with other open-source speech-to-speech dialogue systems.
AI.conclusion: A properly designed VAD-free cascaded pipeline with chunk-wise micro-turns and LLM control tokens can deliver full-duplex, low-latency interaction without sacrificing the intelligence of a text LLM, outperforming existing open-source speech-to-speech dialogue systems on both turn-taking and dialog quality.

title: Chaotic Dynamics in Multi-LLM Deliberation
comment: Main text: 6 pages, 4 figures; Supplementary Information: 14 pages, 7 supplementary figures
summary: Collective AI systems increasingly rely on multi-LLM deliberation, but their stability under repeated execution remains poorly characterized. We model five-agent LLM committees as random dynamical systems and quantify inter-run sensitivity using an empirical Lyapunov exponent ($\hatλ$) derived from trajectory divergence in committee mean preferences. Across 12 policy scenarios, a factorial design at $T=0$ identifies two independent routes to instability: role differentiation in homogeneous committees and model heterogeneity in no-role committees. Critically, these effects appear even in the $T=0$ regime where practitioners often expect deterministic behavior. In the HL-01 benchmark, both routes produce elevated divergence ($\hatλ=0.0541$ and $0.0947$, respectively), while homogeneous no-role committees also remain in a positive-divergence regime ($\hatλ=0.0221$). The combined mixed+roles condition is less unstable than mixed+no-role ($\hatλ=0.0519$ vs $0.0947$), showing non-additive interaction. Mechanistically, Chair-role ablation reduces $\hatλ$ most strongly, and targeted protocol variants that shorten memory windows further attenuate divergence. These results support stability auditing as a core design requirement for multi-LLM governance systems.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the stability of multi-LLM committee systems by modeling them as random dynamical systems and measuring inter-run sensitivity via an empirical Lyapunov exponent, finding that both role differentiation and model heterogeneity can cause instability even at temperature 0.
AI.motivation: As collective AI systems increasingly use multiple LLMs in deliberative committees for governance and decision-making, there is insufficient understanding of how stable or reproducible their outcomes are across repeated runs under identical conditions. Practitioners often assume temperature 0 implies determinism, but interactions between agents, roles, and models may still lead to unstable behavior. The paper aims to rigorously characterize and quantify this instability to inform safer, more reliable governance system design.
AI.method: The authors model five-agent LLM committees as random dynamical systems and track the evolution of committee mean preferences over time. They introduce an empirical Lyapunov exponent (\hatλ) to quantify trajectory divergence between runs started from slightly different initial conditions. Using 12 policy scenarios and a factorial design at temperature T=0, they systematically vary two factors—role differentiation (e.g., chair and other roles) and model heterogeneity (mixed vs homogeneous LLMs)—to isolate their effects on stability. They also perform role ablation (especially of the chair role) and test protocol variants that modify memory window length to examine mechanisms driving instability.
AI.result: In the HL-01 benchmark, committees exhibit measurable positive divergence even at T=0, implying sensitivity to initial conditions. Two independent routes to instability are identified: (1) role differentiation within homogeneous committees and (2) model heterogeneity in no-role committees. Quantitatively, these yield elevated empirical Lyapunov exponents of 0.0541 and 0.0947, respectively, compared to a still-positive 0.0221 for homogeneous no-role committees. The combined condition with both mixed models and roles is less unstable than mixed+no-role (0.0519 vs 0.0947), indicating a non-additive interaction between factors. Chair-role ablation most strongly reduces \hatλ, and shortening memory windows further lowers divergence, highlighting specific levers for stabilizing multi-LLM deliberation.
AI.conclusion: Multi-LLM committee systems can display significant run-to-run instability, even under temperature-0 settings commonly assumed to be deterministic. Both role structures and model heterogeneity can independently increase instability, and their interaction is non-linear. Specific architectural choices—such as the presence and design of a chair role and the length of memory windows—substantially affect stability. Therefore, stability auditing, using metrics like empirical Lyapunov exponents and systematic scenario testing, should be treated as a core design and evaluation requirement for multi-LLM governance and deliberation systems.

title: DEO: Training-Free Direct Embedding Optimization for Negation-Aware Retrieval
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) have enabled diverse retrieval methods. However, existing retrieval methods often fail to accurately retrieve results for negation and exclusion queries. To address this limitation, prior approaches rely on embedding adaptation or fine-tuning, which introduce additional computational cost and deployment complexity. We propose Direct Embedding Optimization (DEO), a training-free method for negation-aware text and multimodal retrieval. DEO decomposes queries into positive and negative components and optimizes the query embedding with a contrastive objective. Without additional training data or model updates, DEO outperforms baselines on NegConstraint, with gains of +0.0738 nDCG@10 and +0.1028 MAP@100, while improving Recall@5 by +6\% over OpenAI CLIP in multimodal retrieval. These results demonstrate the practicality of DEO for negation- and exclusion-aware retrieval in real-world settings.

AI.tldr: They propose a training-free method called Direct Embedding Optimization (DEO) to fix failures of current retrieval systems on negation and exclusion queries, achieving better text and multimodal retrieval without extra training.
AI.motivation: Current LLM- and RAG-based retrieval systems struggle with queries involving negation or exclusion (e.g., 'documents about cats but not dogs'). Prior fixes use embedding adaptation or fine-tuning, which are computationally expensive and complex to deploy. A lighter-weight, training-free solution is needed to make retrieval more accurate for such queries in real-world systems.
AI.method: DEO (Direct Embedding Optimization) decomposes a user query into positive and negative components, then directly optimizes the query embedding itself via a contrastive objective.
