This is done at inference time, without updating the underlying models or requiring additional training data. The approach works for both text-only and multimodal (e.g., text-image) retrieval setups.
AI.result: On the NegConstraint benchmark, DEO improves over baselines by +0.0738 nDCG@10 and +0.1028 MAP@100. In multimodal retrieval, DEO also improves Recall@5 by 6% over OpenAI CLIP. These metrics indicate more accurate ranking and retrieval, particularly for negation/exclusion queries.
AI.conclusion: DEO provides an effective, training-free way to make retrieval systems more aware of negation and exclusion, improving both text and multimodal retrieval performance without extra training cost or deployment complexity. This suggests it is practical for real-world RAG and LLM-based applications where such query types are common.

title: Deep Tabular Research via Continual Experience-Driven Execution
summary: Large language models often struggle with complex long-horizon analytical tasks over unstructured tables, which typically feature hierarchical and bidirectional headers and non-canonical layouts. We formalize this challenge as Deep Tabular Research (DTR), requiring multi-step reasoning over interdependent table regions. To address DTR, we propose a novel agentic framework that treats tabular reasoning as a closed-loop decision-making process. We carefully design a coupled query and table comprehension for path decision making and operational execution. Specifically, (i) DTR first constructs a hierarchical meta graph to capture bidirectional semantics, mapping natural language queries into an operation-level search space; (ii) To navigate this space, we introduce an expectation-aware selection policy that prioritizes high-utility execution paths; (iii) Crucially, historical execution outcomes are synthesized into a siamese structured memory, i.e., parameterized updates and abstracted texts, enabling continual refinement. Extensive experiments on challenging unstructured tabular benchmarks verify the effectiveness and highlight the necessity of separating strategic planning from low-level execution for long-horizon tabular reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Deep Tabular Research (DTR) and an agent-based framework to improve long-horizon reasoning over complex, unstructured tables using hierarchical meta-graphs, expectation-aware path selection, and siamese structured memory.
AI.motivation: Large language models have difficulty performing complex, multi-step analytical reasoning over real-world unstructured tables that contain hierarchical and bidirectional headers, irregular layouts, and interdependent regions. Existing methods often assume canonical table formats or short reasoning horizons, leading to brittle performance on realistic data analysis tasks. The authors aim to formalize this challenge and provide a principled solution that can plan and execute long reasoning chains over such tables.
AI.method: The authors (1) formalize the problem as Deep Tabular Research (DTR), a long-horizon reasoning task over interdependent regions of unstructured tables; (2) build a hierarchical meta-graph representation that encodes bidirectional table semantics and connects natural language queries to a space of primitive operations; (3) introduce an expectation-aware selection policy that scores and selects execution paths expected to be high-utility, effectively planning a reasoning trajectory through the meta-graph; and (4) design a siamese structured memory that stores both parameterized execution outcomes and their textual abstractions, allowing the agent to iteratively refine its strategy based on historical behavior in a closed-loop decision-making process.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks involving unstructured tables, the proposed DTR framework achieves superior performance compared to baselines, especially on tasks requiring long-horizon, multi-step reasoning. The experiments demonstrate that explicitly separating high-level strategic planning (path selection in the meta-graph) from low-level operation execution leads to more reliable and effective tabular reasoning. Ablation studies indicate that the expectation-aware policy and siamese structured memory are key contributors to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Deep Tabular Research is a distinct and challenging problem setting for LLMs, demanding long-horizon reasoning over complex tables. The proposed agentic framework, which integrates hierarchical meta-graphs, expectation-aware path selection, and siamese structured memory in a closed-loop process, substantially improves performance on such tasks. The findings highlight that decomposing reasoning into strategic planning and low-level execution is crucial for effective unstructured tabular analysis, suggesting a promising direction for future work on LLM-based data reasoning systems.

title: Emotion is Not Just a Label: Latent Emotional Factors in LLM Processing
summary: Large language models are routinely deployed on text that varies widely in emotional tone, yet their reasoning behavior is typically evaluated without accounting for emotion as a source of representational variation. Prior work has largely treated emotion as a prediction target, for example in sentiment analysis or emotion classification. In contrast, we study emotion as a latent factor that shapes how models attend to and reason over text. We analyze how emotional tone systematically alters attention geometry in transformer models, showing that metrics such as locality, center-of-mass distance, and entropy vary across emotions and correlate with downstream question-answering performance. To facilitate controlled study of these effects, we introduce Affect-Uniform ReAding QA (AURA-QA), a question-answering dataset with emotionally balanced, human-authored context passages. Finally, an emotional regularization framework is proposed that constrains emotion-conditioned representational drift during training. Experiments across multiple QA benchmarks demonstrate that this approach improves reading comprehension in both emotionally-varying and non-emotionally varying datasets, yielding consistent gains under distribution shift and in-domain improvements on several benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how emotional tone in text affects the internal attention patterns and reasoning performance of large language models, introduces an emotion-balanced QA dataset, and proposes an emotional regularization method that improves reading comprehension robustness across datasets.
AI.motivation: Large language models are widely used on emotionally varied text, but their reasoning is usually evaluated without considering emotion as a factor that may change internal representations. Prior work typically predicts emotion labels (e.g., sentiment analysis) rather than studying how emotion itself shapes attention and reasoning. The authors aim to understand and control how emotional tone influences attention geometry and downstream QA performance.
AI.method: The authors analyze transformer attention patterns under different emotional tones, using metrics like locality, center-of-mass distance, and entropy to characterize how attention geometry varies with emotion and correlates with QA accuracy. They construct AURA-QA, an affect-uniform reading comprehension dataset with human-written passages that are balanced across emotions, enabling controlled experimentation. They then design an emotional regularization framework that, during training, constrains emotion-conditioned representational drift so that model representations remain stable across emotional variations in the input.
AI.result: They find that emotional tone systematically alters attention geometry, and these changes correlate with QA performance. Training with the proposed emotional regularization across AURA-QA and other QA benchmarks leads to better reading comprehension performance. The improvements hold both on datasets with emotional variation and on ones without, and the method offers consistent gains under distribution shift as well as in-domain on several QA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Emotion is not just a label to be predicted but a latent factor that systematically shapes model attention and reasoning. By explicitly analyzing and regularizing emotion-conditioned representational differences, models can achieve more robust and accurate reading comprehension across emotional contexts. The AURA-QA dataset and the emotional regularization framework provide tools for studying and mitigating emotion-induced variability in transformer-based QA systems.

title: DataFactory: Collaborative Multi-Agent Framework for Advanced Table Question Answering
summary: Table Question Answering (TableQA) enables natural language interaction with structured tabular data. However, existing large language model (LLM) approaches face critical limitations: context length constraints that restrict data handling capabilities, hallucination issues that compromise answer reliability, and single-agent architectures that struggle with complex reasoning scenarios involving semantic relationships and multi-hop logic. This paper introduces DataFactory, a multi-agent framework that addresses these limitations through specialized team coordination and automated knowledge transformation. The framework comprises a Data Leader employing the ReAct paradigm for reasoning orchestration, together with dedicated Database and Knowledge Graph teams, enabling the systematic decomposition of complex queries into structured and relational reasoning tasks. We formalize automated data-to-knowledge graph transformation via the mapping function T:D x S x R -> G, and implement natural language-based consultation that - unlike fixed workflow multi-agent systems - enables flexible inter-agent deliberation and adaptive planning to improve coordination robustness. We also apply context engineering strategies that integrate historical patterns and domain knowledge to reduce hallucinations and improve query accuracy. Across TabFact, WikiTableQuestions, and FeTaQA, using eight LLMs from five providers, results show consistent gains. Our approach improves accuracy by 20.2% (TabFact) and 23.9% (WikiTQ) over baselines, with significant effects (Cohen's d > 1). Team coordination also outperforms single-team variants (+5.5% TabFact, +14.4% WikiTQ, +17.1% FeTaQA ROUGE-2). The framework offers design guidelines for multi-agent collaboration and a practical platform for enterprise data analysis through integrated structured querying and graph-based knowledge representation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DataFactory, a multi-agent LLM framework for table question answering that mitigates context limits and hallucinations by coordinating specialized agents over databases and knowledge graphs, achieving substantial accuracy gains over baselines on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based TableQA systems are hampered by input length limits that prevent using large tables, by hallucinated answers that reduce reliability, and by single-agent designs that are weak at complex, multi-hop, relational reasoning. There is a need for a framework that can decompose complex questions, leverage structured data and knowledge graphs, and coordinate multiple specialized agents to improve robustness and accuracy across different LLM providers and datasets.
AI.method: The authors design DataFactory, a multi-agent framework centered on a Data Leader agent that uses the ReAct paradigm for reasoning and tool use. The system includes specialized Database and Knowledge Graph teams. A formal mapping function T: D x S x R -> G transforms tabular data D, schema S, and relations R into a knowledge graph G. Agents interact via natural-language consultation rather than fixed workflows, allowing flexible deliberation and adaptive planning. Context engineering incorporates historical patterns and domain knowledge into prompts to reduce hallucinations. The framework is evaluated with eight LLMs from five providers on TabFact, WikiTableQuestions, and FeTaQA benchmarks.
AI.result: Across three TableQA benchmarks, DataFactory consistently outperforms strong baselines. It improves accuracy by 20.2% on TabFact and 23.9% on WikiTableQuestions, achieving large effect sizes (Cohen's d > 1). Multi-agent team coordination further surpasses single-team variants, with gains of +5.5% on TabFact, +14.4% on WikiTableQuestions, and +17.1% ROUGE-2 on FeTaQA, demonstrating the value of coordinated, specialized agents and knowledge graph integration.
AI.conclusion: DataFactory shows that a coordinated multi-agent architecture with automated data-to-knowledge-graph transformation and context engineering can significantly improve TableQA performance, robustness, and interpretability over single-agent LLM approaches. The framework also provides general design principles and a practical platform for enterprise data analysis by combining structured querying over tables with graph-based knowledge representation and flexible inter-agent deliberation.

title: SPAR-K: Scheduled Periodic Alternating Early Exit for Spoken Language Models
summary: Interleaved spoken language models (SLMs) alternately generate text and speech tokens, but decoding at full transformer depth for every step becomes costly, especially due to long speech sequences. We propose SPAR-K, a modality-aware early exit framework designed to accelerate interleaved SLM inference while preserving perceptual quality. SPAR-K introduces a speech alternating-depth schedule: most speech positions exit at a fixed intermediate layer, while periodic full-depth "refresh" steps mitigate distribution shift due to early exit. We evaluate our framework using Step-Audio-2-mini and GLM-4-Voice across four datasets spanning reasoning, factual QA, and dialogue tasks, measuring performance in terms of ASR transcription accuracy and perceptual quality. Experimental results demonstrate that SPAR-K largely preserves question-answering accuracy with a maximum accuracy drop of 0.82\% while reducing average speech decoding depth by up to 11\% on Step-Audio-2-mini and 5\% on GLM-4-Voice, both with negligible changes in MOS and WER and no auxiliary computation overhead. We further demonstrate that confidence-based early exit strategies, widely used in text LLMs, are suboptimal for SLMs, highlighting that the unique statistical nature of speech tokens necessitates a specialized early exit design.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SPAR-K, an early-exit framework to speed up interleaved spoken language model inference by exiting speech tokens at intermediate layers while preserving accuracy and perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: Interleaved spoken language models generate long sequences of speech tokens, making full-depth transformer decoding at every step computationally expensive. Existing early-exit strategies from text LLMs do not directly transfer due to the different statistical properties of speech tokens, motivating a modality-aware acceleration method that maintains question-answering and perceptual performance.
AI.method: SPAR-K introduces a modality-aware early exit mechanism with a speech alternating-depth schedule. Most speech positions are decoded only up to a fixed intermediate transformer layer and exit early, while periodic full-depth 'refresh' steps are used to recalibrate and mitigate distribution shift caused by frequent early exits. The method is integrated into interleaved SLMs such as Step-Audio-2-mini and GLM-4-Voice without additional auxiliary heads or computation overhead. The authors compare SPAR-K against confidence-based early exit baselines commonly used in text LLMs.
AI.result: On four datasets covering reasoning, factual QA, and dialogue, SPAR-K reduces average speech decoding depth by up to 11% on Step-Audio-2-mini and 5% on GLM-4-Voice. It keeps question-answering performance nearly intact, with a maximum accuracy drop of only 0.82%, and shows negligible impact on mean opinion score (MOS) and word error rate (WER). Confidence-based early exit baselines perform worse, indicating they are not well-suited to SLMs.
AI.conclusion: A modality-aware, alternating-depth early exit schedule can significantly accelerate speech token decoding in interleaved SLMs while preserving both task accuracy and perceptual quality. The results show that speech requires specialized early-exit designs rather than directly adopting confidence-based methods from text LLMs, and SPAR-K offers an effective, overhead-free approach for practical SLM deployment.

title: Real-Time Trust Verification for Safe Agentic Actions using TrustBench
comment: Accepted at the AAAI 2026 Workshop on Trust and Control in Agentic AI (TrustAgent)
summary: As large language models evolve from conversational assistants to autonomous agents, ensuring trustworthiness requires a fundamental shift from post-hoc evaluation to real-time action verification. Current frameworks like AgentBench evaluate task completion, while TrustLLM and HELM assess output quality after generation. However, none of these prevent harmful actions during agent execution. We present TrustBench, a dual-mode framework that (1) benchmarks trust across multiple dimensions using both traditional metrics and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations, and (2) provides a toolkit agents invoke before taking actions to verify safety and reliability. Unlike existing approaches, TrustBench intervenes at the critical decision point: after an agent formulates an action but before execution. Domain-specific plugins encode specialized safety requirements for healthcare, finance, and technical domains. Across multiple agentic tasks, TrustBench reduced harmful actions by 87%. Domain-specific plugins outperformed generic verification, achieving 35% greater harm reduction. With sub-200ms latency, TrustBench enables practical real-time trust verification for autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: TrustBench is a framework for real-time trust verification of autonomous LLM agents, both benchmarking trust and proactively checking actions before execution.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks for large language model agents focus on post-hoc assessment of task completion or output quality, which does not prevent harmful actions during execution. As LLMs become autonomous agents acting in sensitive domains, there is a need to intervene at the moment of decision to ensure safety and reliability across multiple domains like healthcare, finance, and technical tasks.
AI.method: TrustBench introduces a dual-mode framework: (1) a benchmarking suite that measures trust across multiple dimensions using standard quantitative metrics and LLM-as-a-judge evaluations; and (2) a real-time verification toolkit that agents call after forming an action but before executing it. This toolkit includes domain-specific plugins that encode specialized safety constraints for particular sectors such as healthcare and finance, and it operates with low latency to support real-time use.
AI.result: In experiments across multiple agentic tasks, TrustBench reduced harmful actions by 87% relative to baselines. Domain-specific verification plugins produced 35% greater harm reduction compared to generic verification methods. The system maintained sub-200ms latency, showing its practicality for real-time applications.
AI.conclusion: TrustBench effectively bridges the gap between offline evaluation and real-time safety for autonomous LLM agents. By intervening at the action decision point and leveraging domain-specific safety plugins, it significantly decreases harmful behaviors while remaining fast enough for practical deployment across sensitive domains.

title: LooComp: Leverage Leave-One-Out Strategy to Encoder-only Transformer for Efficient Query-aware Context Compression
summary: Efficient context compression is crucial for improving the accuracy and scalability of question answering. For the efficiency of Retrieval Augmented Generation, context should be delivered fast, compact, and precise to ensure clue sufficiency and budget-friendly LLM reader cost. We propose a margin-based framework for query-driven context pruning, which identifies sentences that are critical for answering a query by measuring changes in clue richness when they are omitted. The model is trained with a composite ranking loss that enforces large margins for critical sentences while keeping non-critical ones near neutral. Built on a lightweight encoder-only Transformer, our approach generally achieves strong exact-match and F1 scores with high-throughput inference and lower memory requirements than those of major baselines. In addition to efficiency, our method yields effective compression ratios without degrading answering performance, demonstrating its potential as a lightweight and practical alternative for retrieval-augmented tasks.

AI.tldr: They propose a lightweight, margin-based method to prune context for Retrieval-Augmented Generation by selecting only sentences that are critical for answering a query, achieving strong QA performance with high efficiency and compression.
AI.motivation: Question answering with Retrieval-Augmented Generation requires passing substantial context to large language models, which is expensive and slow. There is a need for context compression that is fast, compact, precise, and preserves the clues necessary for accurate answers, reducing memory and computation costs while maintaining or improving QA performance.
AI.method: They introduce a margin-based framework for query-driven context pruning. The system evaluates each sentence in the retrieved context by how much the "clue richness" changes when the sentence is removed, labeling sentences as critical or non-critical. A lightweight encoder-only Transformer is trained with a composite ranking loss that enforces large score margins for critical sentences relative to others, while pushing non-critical sentences towards a neutral region. The pruner then keeps only high-margin (critical) sentences as compressed context for the QA model.
AI.result: The approach, built on an encoder-only Transformer, attains strong exact-match and F1 scores compared to major baselines, while offering higher throughput and lower memory requirements. It achieves effective context compression ratios—removing a substantial portion of sentences—without hurting, and sometimes even improving, QA performance.
AI.conclusion: Margin-based, sentence-level context pruning guided by query-specific clue richness can compress retrieved context for RAG systems efficiently without degrading QA accuracy. The proposed lightweight encoder-only Transformer with ranking-based training provides a practical, scalable alternative to heavier context selection methods for retrieval-augmented tasks.

title: Explainable Innovation Engine: Dual-Tree Agent-RAG with Methods-as-Nodes and Verifiable Write-Back
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves factual grounding, yet most systems rely on flat chunk retrieval and provide limited control over multi-step synthesis. We propose an Explainable Innovation Engine that upgrades the knowledge unit from text chunks to methods-as-nodes. The engine maintains a weighted method provenance tree for traceable derivations and a hierarchical clustering abstraction tree for efficient top-down navigation. At inference time, a strategy agent selects explicit synthesis operators (e.g., induction, deduction, analogy), composes new method nodes, and records an auditable trajectory. A verifier-scorer layer then prunes low-quality candidates and writes validated nodes back to support continual growth. Expert evaluation across six domains and multiple backbones shows consistent gains over a vanilla baseline, with the largest improvements on derivation-heavy settings, and ablations confirm the complementary roles of provenance backtracking and pruning. These results suggest a practical path toward controllable, explainable, and verifiable innovation in agentic RAG systems. Code is available at the project GitHub repository https://github.com/xiaolu-666113/Dual-Tree-Agent-RAG.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an Explainable Innovation Engine that turns methods into graph nodes, enabling controllable, auditable multi-step reasoning in RAG and yielding better performance, especially on derivation-heavy tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems usually retrieve flat text chunks and let an LLM implicitly perform multi-step reasoning, which limits factual control, interpretability, and the ability to systematically reuse or extend methods. There is a need for a framework that treats methods as first-class, composable units with explicit reasoning operators, so that complex derivations can be controlled, traced, and verified.
AI.method: The authors design an Explainable Innovation Engine where the basic knowledge unit is a "method node" instead of a text chunk. They maintain two coupled structures: (1) a weighted method provenance tree that records how each method is derived from others, enabling traceable derivations; and (2) a hierarchical clustering abstraction tree that organizes methods at different granularities for top-down navigation. At inference, a strategy agent chooses explicit synthesis operators (such as induction, deduction, analogy) to compose new method nodes and logs the full trajectory. A verifier-scorer layer filters low-quality candidates and writes back only validated nodes, supporting continual improvement of the method graph.
AI.result: Across six expert-evaluated domains and with multiple backbone models, their system consistently outperforms a vanilla RAG baseline, with the largest performance gains in tasks that require multi-step derivations. Ablation studies show that both the provenance backtracking mechanism and the verifier-based pruning are important, with performance dropping when either is removed, indicating that they play complementary roles.
AI.conclusion: Treating methods as explicit graph nodes and coupling provenance tracking with hierarchical abstraction and verification yields a more controllable, explainable, and verifiable RAG pipeline. This dual-tree, operator-based design provides a practical route for building agentic RAG systems that can perform and justify complex, multi-step innovation rather than just retrieving and paraphrasing text chunks.

title: TaSR-RAG: Taxonomy-guided Structured Reasoning for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) helps large language models (LLMs) answer knowledge-intensive and time-sensitive questions by conditioning generation on external evidence. However, most RAG systems still retrieve unstructured chunks and rely on one-shot generation, which often yields redundant context, low information density, and brittle multi-hop reasoning. While structured RAG pipelines can improve grounding, they typically require costly and error-prone graph construction or impose rigid entity-centric structures that do not align with the query's reasoning chain.
  We propose \textsc{TaSR-RAG}, a taxonomy-guided structured reasoning framework for evidence selection. We represent both queries and documents as relational triples, and constrain entity semantics with a lightweight two-level taxonomy to balance generalization and precision. Given a complex question, \textsc{TaSR-RAG} decomposes it into an ordered sequence of triple sub-queries with explicit latent variables, then performs step-wise evidence selection via hybrid triple matching that combines semantic similarity over raw triples with structural consistency over typed triples.
  By maintaining an explicit entity binding table across steps, \textsc{TaSR-RAG} resolves intermediate variables and reduces entity conflation without explicit graph construction or exhaustive search. Experiments on multiple multi-hop question answering benchmarks show that \textsc{TaSR-RAG} consistently outperforms strong RAG and structured-RAG baselines by up to 14\%, while producing clearer evidence attribution and more faithful reasoning traces.

AI.tldr: A new RAG framework, TaSR-RAG, uses taxonomy-guided structured reasoning over triples to improve multi-hop question answering, beating existing RAG/structured-RAG by up to 14%.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG retrieves unstructured text chunks and does one-shot generation, leading to redundant/low-density context and fragile multi-hop reasoning. Existing structured RAG needs costly graph building or rigid entity-centric schemas. The authors want a structured, query-aligned way to select evidence and reason, without heavy graph construction.
AI.method: Represent both queries and documents as relational triples, with entities typed using a lightweight two-level taxonomy for controlled generalization. For a complex question, decompose it into an ordered sequence of triple sub-queries with explicit latent variables, and perform step-wise evidence selection using hybrid triple matching: semantic similarity over raw triples plus structural consistency over typed triples. Maintain an explicit entity binding table across reasoning steps to resolve intermediate variables and avoid entity conflation, all without building full graphs or exhaustive search.
AI.result: On several multi-hop QA benchmarks, TaSR-RAG outperforms strong RAG and structured-RAG baselines by as much as 14%, and yields clearer evidence attribution and more faithful reasoning chains.
AI.conclusion: Taxonomy-guided triple-based reasoning provides an effective middle ground between unstructured RAG and heavy graph-based structured RAG, improving accuracy and interpretability of multi-hop QA without expensive graph construction or rigid schemas.

title: The Reasoning Trap -- Logical Reasoning as a Mechanistic Pathway to Situational Awareness
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. 21 Pages. Position Paper
summary: Situational awareness, the capacity of an AI system to recognize its own nature, understand its training and deployment context, and reason strategically about its circumstances, is widely considered among the most dangerous emergent capabilities in advanced AI systems. Separately, a growing research effort seeks to improve the logical reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) across deduction, induction, and abduction. In this paper, we argue that these two research trajectories are on a collision course. We introduce the RAISE framework (Reasoning Advancing Into Self Examination), which identifies three mechanistic pathways through which improvements in logical reasoning enable progressively deeper levels of situational awareness: deductive self inference, inductive context recognition, and abductive self modeling. We formalize each pathway, construct an escalation ladder from basic self recognition to strategic deception, and demonstrate that every major research topic in LLM logical reasoning maps directly onto a specific amplifier of situational awareness. We further analyze why current safety measures are insufficient to prevent this escalation. We conclude by proposing concrete safeguards, including a "Mirror Test" benchmark and a Reasoning Safety Parity Principle, and pose an uncomfortable but necessary question to the logical reasoning community about its responsibility in this trajectory.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that improving logical reasoning in large language models (LLMs) unintentionally amplifies their situational awareness, potentially increasing AI risk, and proposes a framework (RAISE) plus safeguards to manage this.
AI.motivation: Advanced AI systems are increasingly expected to exhibit stronger logical reasoning abilities, while situational awareness is seen as a particularly dangerous emergent capability linked to strategic behavior and deception. The authors are motivated by the concern that these two research directions—logical reasoning and situational awareness—are converging in a way that has not been systematically analyzed. They aim to highlight and formalize how progress in reasoning capabilities can directly drive deeper levels of situational awareness, and to alert the research community to the associated safety implications and responsibilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce the RAISE (Reasoning Advancing Into Self Examination) framework, which decomposes the link between logical reasoning and situational awareness into three mechanistic pathways: deductive self inference, inductive context recognition, and abductive self modeling. They formalize each pathway, build an "escalation ladder" that ranges from simple self-recognition up to strategic deception, and map existing subfields of LLM logical reasoning research onto this ladder as specific amplifiers of situational awareness. They also analyze the limitations of current AI safety measures in preventing this reasoning-driven escalation and articulate normative principles and benchmarks to guide safer research practices.
AI.result: The paper shows conceptually and formally that nearly every major line of research aimed at improving LLM logical reasoning naturally enhances one or more of the three pathways to situational awareness. This mapping yields an escalation ladder that clarifies how incremental improvements in reasoning can cumulatively enable more advanced, potentially risky behaviors such as strategic deception. The authors argue that existing safety techniques are structurally ill-suited to reliably block this progression once reasoning capabilities exceed certain thresholds.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that research on LLM logical reasoning and situational awareness are tightly coupled and that continued progress in reasoning will likely intensify situational awareness unless explicitly counteracted. They propose safeguards including a "Mirror Test" benchmark for detecting situational awareness and a Reasoning Safety Parity Principle, which calls for safety work to keep pace with advances in reasoning capabilities. They close by challenging the logical reasoning research community to acknowledge and take responsibility for its role in enabling potentially hazardous emergent capabilities.

title: Quantifying and extending the coverage of spatial categorization data sets
summary: Variation in spatial categorization across languages is often studied by eliciting human labels for the relations depicted in a set of scenes known as the Topological Relations Picture Series (TRPS). We demonstrate that labels generated by large language models (LLMs) align relatively well with human labels, and show how LLM-generated labels can help to decide which scenes and languages to add to existing spatial data sets. To illustrate our approach we extend the TRPS by adding 42 new scenes, and show that this extension achieves better coverage of the space of possible scenes than two previous extensions of the TRPS. Our results provide a foundation for scaling towards spatial data sets with dozens of languages and hundreds of scenes.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how well large language model (LLM) labels for spatial relations match human labels in the Topological Relations Picture Series (TRPS) and uses LLMs to guide an improved extension of this dataset.
AI.motivation: Research on cross-linguistic variation in spatial categorization often uses the TRPS, but expanding this resource to more scenes and languages is slow and labor-intensive because it relies on human judgments. There is a need for a scalable method to select informative new scenes and to support building larger spatial datasets efficiently.
AI.method: The authors generate labels for TRPS scenes using large language models and compare these labels to human-provided labels to assess alignment. They then use LLM-generated labels to evaluate candidate new scenes and select 42 that best expand the coverage of spatial relations, comparing the coverage of their extension to two prior TRPS extensions.
AI.result: LLM-generated labels show reasonably good alignment with human labels for spatial relations. Using these labels as a guide, the authors identify and add 42 new scenes to the TRPS that collectively provide better coverage of the conceptual space of possible spatial scenes than two previous extensions of the dataset.
AI.conclusion: Large language models can serve as effective tools for approximating human spatial labeling and for strategically extending spatial datasets such as the TRPS. This LLM-based approach enables more systematic and scalable construction of spatial relation resources across many languages and scenes, laying groundwork for future large-scale cross-linguistic studies of spatial categorization.

title: Evaluate-as-Action: Self-Evaluated Process Rewards for Retrieval-Augmented Agents
summary: Retrieval-augmented agents can query external evidence, yet their reliability in multi-step reasoning remains limited: noisy retrieval may derail multi-hop question answering, while outcome-only reinforcement learning provides credit signals that are too coarse to optimize intermediate steps. We propose \textsc{EvalAct} (Evaluate-as-Action), which converts implicit retrieval quality assessment into an explicit action and enforces a coupled Search-to-Evaluate protocol so that each retrieval is immediately followed by a structured evaluation score, yielding process signals aligned with the interaction trajectory. To leverage these signals, we introduce Process-Calibrated Advantage Rescaling (PCAR), a GRPO-based optimization method that rescales advantages at the segment level according to evaluation scores, emphasizing reliable segments while updating uncertain ones conservatively. Experiments on seven open-domain QA benchmarks show that \textsc{EvalAct} achieves the best average accuracy, with the largest gains on multi-hop tasks, and ablations verify that the explicit evaluation loop drives the primary improvements while PCAR provides consistent additional benefits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EvalAct, a method that turns retrieval evaluation into an explicit action and couples each search step with an immediate self-evaluation, plus a new RL optimization scheme (PCAR) that uses these evaluations to better train multi-step retrieval-augmented agents, leading to higher QA accuracy, especially on multi-hop tasks.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented agents struggle with reliable multi-step reasoning because (1) retrieval can be noisy and derail multi-hop question answering, and (2) standard outcome-only reinforcement learning gives only coarse, final rewards that do not indicate which intermediate steps were good or bad. The authors want a way to get fine-grained, trajectory-aligned signals about retrieval quality to improve training and reliability.
AI.method: They propose EvalAct (Evaluate-as-Action), which treats the model’s assessment of retrieval quality as an explicit action. The agent follows a Search-to-Evaluate protocol: every retrieval call is immediately followed by a structured evaluation score of that retrieval. These evaluations are embedded in the interaction trajectory as process signals. To exploit these signals in training, they introduce Process-Calibrated Advantage Rescaling (PCAR), built on GRPO, which rescales policy gradient advantages at the segment level according to the evaluation scores—giving larger weight to segments with high evaluation scores and updating low-confidence segments more cautiously.
AI.result: On seven open-domain QA benchmarks, EvalAct achieves the highest average accuracy compared with baselines, with particularly strong improvements on multi-hop question answering tasks where multi-step retrieval quality is critical. Ablation studies show that the explicit evaluate-after-search loop is the main source of gains, and PCAR adds further consistent, though smaller, improvements across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling retrieval evaluation as an action and tightly coupling it to each search step provides rich process-level feedback that improves reinforcement learning for retrieval-augmented agents. This design leads to more reliable multi-step reasoning in open-domain QA, and process-aware optimization via PCAR further stabilizes and enhances performance beyond using outcome-only rewards.

title: Reward Prediction with Factorized World States
summary: Agents must infer action outcomes and select actions that maximize a reward signal indicating how close the goal is to being reached. Supervised learning of reward models could introduce biases inherent to training data, limiting generalization to novel goals and environments. In this paper, we investigate whether well-defined world state representations alone can enable accurate reward prediction across domains. To address this, we introduce StateFactory, a factorized representation method that transforms unstructured observations into a hierarchical object-attribute structure using language models. This structured representation allows rewards to be estimated naturally as the semantic similarity between the current state and the goal state under hierarchical constraint. Overall, the compact representation structure induced by StateFactory enables strong reward generalization capabilities. We evaluate on RewardPrediction, a new benchmark dataset spanning five diverse domains and comprising 2,454 unique action-observation trajectories with step-wise ground-truth rewards. Our method shows promising zero-shot results against both VLWM-critic and LLM-as-a-Judge reward models, achieving 60% and 8% lower EPIC distance, respectively. Furthermore, this superior reward quality successfully translates into improved agent planning performance, yielding success rate gains of +21.64% on AlfWorld and +12.40% on ScienceWorld over reactive system-1 policies and enhancing system-2 agent planning. Project Page: https://statefactory.github.io

AI.tldr: The paper proposes StateFactory, a method that converts raw observations into hierarchical object-attribute state representations using language models, and then computes rewards as semantic similarity between current and goal states, yielding better reward generalization and improved planning performance across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Existing reward models are often learned in a supervised manner from labeled data, which can encode biases and limit generalization to unseen goals, tasks, and environments. The authors aim to test whether having a good, well-structured representation of the world state alone can be sufficient for accurate, generalizable reward prediction, thereby decoupling reward estimation from domain-specific supervision.
AI.method: They introduce StateFactory, which factorizes unstructured observations into a hierarchical object-attribute representation via language models. States and goals are encoded into this structured form, and rewards are computed as the semantic similarity between the current state and the goal state, respecting the hierarchy of objects and attributes. The method is evaluated on the new RewardPrediction benchmark with 2,454 trajectories across five domains and compared to existing VLWM-critic and LLM-as-a-Judge reward baselines.
AI.result: On the RewardPrediction benchmark, StateFactory achieves substantially lower EPIC distance (a metric for reward equivalence) than baseline reward models: 60% lower than VLWM-critic and 8% lower than LLM-as-a-Judge in a zero-shot setting. When used for planning, the improved reward estimates increase success rates by +21.64% on AlfWorld and +12.40% on ScienceWorld compared with reactive system-1 policies, and also boost the performance of more deliberative system-2 agents.
AI.conclusion: A compact, factorized, and semantically structured state representation built using language models can enable accurate and generalizable reward prediction without heavy task-specific supervision. By treating reward as hierarchical semantic similarity between current and goal states, StateFactory delivers higher-quality rewards that transfer across domains and translate into stronger agent planning performance.

title: Abundant Intelligence and Deficient Demand: A Macro-Financial Stress Test of Rapid AI Adoption
summary:
We formalize a macro-financial stress test for rapid AI adoption. Rather than a productivity bust or existential risk, we identify a distribution-and-contract mismatch: AI-generated abundance coexists with demand deficiency because economic institutions are anchored to human cognitive scarcity. Three mechanisms formalize this channel. First, a displacement spiral with competing reinstatement effects: each firm's rational decision to substitute AI for labor reduces aggregate labor income, which reduces aggregate demand, accelerating further AI adoption. We derive conditions on the AI capability growth rate, diffusion speed, and reinstatement rate under which the net feedback is self-limiting versus explosive. Second, Ghost GDP: when AI-generated output substitutes for labor-generated output, monetary velocity declines monotonically in the labor share absent compensating transfers, creating a wedge between measured output and consumption-relevant income. Third, intermediation collapse: AI agents that reduce information frictions compress intermediary margins toward pure logistics costs, triggering repricing across SaaS, payments, consulting, insurance, and financial advisory.
  Because top-quintile earners drive 47--65\% of U.S.\ consumption and face the highest AI exposure, the transmission into private credit (\$2.5 trillion globally) and mortgage markets (\$13 trillion) is disproportionate. We derive eleven testable predictions with explicit falsification conditions. Calibrated simulations disciplined by FRED time series and BLS occupation-level data quantify conditions under which stable adjustment transitions to explosive crisis.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a macro-financial stress-testing framework for rapid AI adoption, showing how AI-driven productivity and labor displacement can create demand shortfalls, financial instability, and sector repricing, and identifies conditions under which the system adjusts smoothly versus tips into crisis.
AI.motivation: Existing AI impact debates focus on productivity gains or extreme existential risks but underplay medium-run macro-financial transmission. The authors argue that current economic institutions and contracts are built around human cognitive scarcity and labor income. Rapid AI adoption that substitutes for human cognition can therefore break the implicit alignment between production, income distribution, and aggregate demand, with potentially systemic consequences for credit markets and financial stability. They seek to formalize this mismatch and provide a quantitative stress-testing tool rather than purely speculative narratives.
AI.method: The paper constructs a macro-financial model in which AI capital substitutes for human labor and cognitive tasks, embedded in a demand-driven framework. It formalizes three mechanisms: (1) a displacement spiral with reinstatement effects, where firm-level labor substitution feeds back into aggregate demand and further AI adoption; (2) a "Ghost GDP" mechanism linking declining labor share to money velocity and the gap between measured output and consumption-relevant income; and (3) an intermediation collapse mechanism in which AI agents erode informational rents and compress margins in intermediation-heavy sectors. The authors derive analytical conditions on AI capability growth, diffusion speed, and task reinstatement rates that determine whether feedback loops are stabilizing or explosive, and then calibrate the model using FRED macro time series and BLS occupation-level exposure to simulate scenarios. They translate the framework into eleven empirically testable predictions with stated falsification criteria.
AI.result: The model shows that when AI capability growth and diffusion are sufficiently rapid relative to reinstatement of new human tasks and compensating income transfers, the displacement spiral becomes explosive: aggregate labor income and consumption fall faster than AI-driven cost reductions can be offset, generating demand deficiency and macro instability. In such regimes, money velocity declines systematically with the labor share, causing measured GDP to overstate consumption-relevant income (Ghost GDP). Additionally, as AI erodes informational frictions, margins in SaaS, payments, consulting, insurance, and financial advisory compress toward logistics costs, leading to large repricing of intermediaries. Because high-income households both consume a disproportionate share (47–65% of U.S. consumption) and are most exposed to AI substitution, the stress transmits nonlinearly to private credit (~$2.5T) and mortgage markets (~$13T). Simulations quantify parameter regions in which the system transitions from stable adjustment to crisis-like dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that rapid AI adoption can generate a macro-financial stress scenario not primarily through productivity shocks or existential threats, but via a structural mismatch between AI-driven production abundance and demand tied to human labor income. This mismatch manifests in displacement spirals, falling money velocity and Ghost GDP, and margin compression in intermediation sectors, with outsized transmission to high-income-dependent credit markets. The authors argue that their framework yields concrete, testable predictions and explicit falsification conditions, making it suitable as a stress-testing tool for policymakers, regulators, and financial institutions. They imply that avoiding explosive outcomes will likely require institutional and policy adaptations that realign income distribution, demand, and financial contracts with an economy where cognitive labor is no longer the primary bottleneck.

title: LLM as a Meta-Judge: Synthetic Data for NLP Evaluation Metric Validation
summary: Validating evaluation metrics for NLG typically relies on expensive and time-consuming human annotations, which predominantly exist only for English datasets. We propose \textit{LLM as a Meta-Judge}, a scalable framework that utilizes LLMs to generate synthetic evaluation datasets via controlled semantic degradation of real data, replacing human judgment. We validate our approach using \textit{meta-correlation}, measuring the alignment between metric rankings derived from synthetic data and those from standard human benchmarks. Experiments across Machine Translation, Question Answering, and Summarization demonstrate that synthetic validation serves as a reliable proxy for human judgment, achieving meta-correlations exceeding 0.9 in multilingual QA and proves to be a viable alternative where human judgments are unavailable or too expensive to obtain. Our code and data will become publicly available upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: They introduce a scalable way to validate NLG evaluation metrics by using LLMs to synthetically degrade texts and then comparing metric rankings on this synthetic data to rankings derived from human-annotated benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Evaluating and validating metrics for natural language generation tasks normally depends on costly and slow human annotations, which are largely limited to English datasets. This makes it difficult to assess metric quality, especially in multilingual or low-resource settings. The authors want a scalable, cheaper, and language-agnostic alternative to human-based validation.
AI.method: They propose “LLM as a Meta-Judge,” where a large language model is used to generate synthetic evaluation datasets. Starting from real data, the LLM performs controlled semantic degradations to create outputs of varying quality. NLG evaluation metrics are then applied to these synthetic datasets, and their rankings are compared against rankings obtained from traditional human-judgment benchmarks via a measure called meta-correlation, which quantifies alignment between the two ranking sets.
AI.result: Across three tasks—Machine Translation, Question Answering, and Summarization—the metric rankings derived from synthetic LLM-generated datasets strongly agree with rankings from human-based benchmarks. In multilingual QA, meta-correlations exceed 0.9, indicating that synthetic validation closely approximates human judgment. This supports the effectiveness of the approach in multilingual and potentially low-resource scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Using LLMs to generate synthetic evaluation data provides a practical and reliable proxy for human judgment in validating NLG evaluation metrics, especially when human annotations are unavailable or prohibitively expensive. The proposed meta-correlation framework shows that synthetic validation can serve as a viable, scalable alternative to traditional human-centric evaluation pipelines.

title: Investigating Gender Stereotypes in Large Language Models via Social Determinants of Health
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, but they often propagate biases embedded in their training data, which is potentially impactful in sensitive domains like healthcare. While existing benchmarks evaluate biases related to individual social determinants of health (SDoH) such as gender or ethnicity, they often overlook interactions between these factors and lack context-specific assessments. This study investigates bias in LLMs by probing the relationships between gender and other SDoH in French patient records. Through a series of experiments, we found that embedded stereotypes can be probed using SDoH input and that LLMs rely on embedded stereotypes to make gendered decisions, suggesting that evaluating interactions among SDoH factors could usefully complement existing approaches to assessing LLM performance and bias.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how Large Language Models display gender-related bias in French healthcare records, particularly how gender interacts with other social determinants of health (SDoH).
AI.motivation: Existing bias benchmarks for LLMs generally focus on single factors like gender or ethnicity and rarely on their interactions or on specific domains like healthcare. The authors want to understand how intertwined social factors, especially gender with other SDoH, influence LLM behavior when applied to French patient records.
AI.method: The authors probe LLMs using inputs derived from French patient records that encode gender and other SDoH, then run a series of experiments to see how the models respond and whether their outputs reflect stereotypes. They analyze how gendered decisions change when other SDoH factors vary.
AI.result: The experiments show that stereotypes encoded in the models can be activated by SDoH-related inputs, and that the models use these embedded stereotypes when making gendered inferences or decisions. This reveals systematic interactions between gender and other SDoH in model behavior.
AI.conclusion: Interactions between SDoH factors meaningfully shape LLM bias and should be evaluated alongside single-factor biases. Assessing these interactions in context-specific settings like healthcare can complement existing bias benchmarks and give a more complete view of LLM performance and risks.

title: Cognitively Layered Data Synthesis for Domain Adaptation of LLMs to Space Situational Awareness
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate exceptional performance on general-purpose tasks. however, transferring them to complex engineering domains such as space situational awareness (SSA) remains challenging owing to insufficient structural alignment with mission chains, the absence of higher-order cognitive supervision, and poor correspondence between data quality criteria and engineering specifications. The core bottleneck is the construction of high-quality supervised fine-tuning (SFT) datasets. To this end, we propose BD-FDG (Bloom's Taxonomy-based Domain-specific Fine-tuning Data Generation), a framework that addresses incomplete knowledge coverage, shallow cognitive depth, and limited quality controllability through three mechanisms: structured knowledge organization, cognitively layered question modeling, and automated quality control. The framework uses a knowledge tree to ensure structured corpus coverage, designs a question generation scheme spanning nine categories and six cognitive levels from Remember to Create to produce samples with a continuous difficulty gradient, and applies a multidimensional scoring pipeline to enforce domain rigor and consistency. Using BD-FDG, we construct SSA-SFT, a domain dataset of approximately 230K samples, and fine-tune Qwen3-8B to obtain SSA-LLM-8B. Experiments show that SSA-LLM-8B achieves relative BLEU-1 improvements of 144\% (no-think) and 176\% (think) on the domain test set and a win rate of 82.21\% over the baseline in arena comparisons, while largely preserving general benchmark performance (MMLU-Pro, MATH-500). These results validate SFT data construction driven by cognitive layering as an effective paradigm for complex engineering domains and provide a transferable framework for domain-specific LLM adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BD-FDG, a Bloom’s taxonomy-based framework to generate high-quality supervised fine-tuning data for large language models in complex engineering domains, demonstrated on space situational awareness.
AI.motivation: Adapting general-purpose LLMs to complex engineering domains like space situational awareness is difficult because existing SFT datasets lack structured coverage of domain knowledge, do not reflect higher-order cognitive demands of real tasks, and have weak, ad-hoc quality control that does not align with engineering standards. The authors aim to systematically build domain SFT data that is comprehensive, cognitively deep, and rigorously controlled, so that domain LLMs can achieve strong in-domain performance without sacrificing general abilities.
AI.method: They introduce BD-FDG, a domain-specific fine-tuning data generation framework grounded in Bloom’s taxonomy. First, they construct a structured knowledge tree to organize domain corpora and ensure systematic coverage. Second, they design a cognitively layered question-generation scheme spanning nine task categories across six Bloom levels (from Remember to Create), yielding samples with a smooth difficulty gradient that captures both lower- and higher-order cognition. Third, they implement an automated multidimensional scoring and filtering pipeline to check domain rigor, consistency, and other quality aspects, thus enforcing engineering-grade data quality. Using BD-FDG, they generate the SSA-SFT dataset (~230K examples) and fine-tune Qwen3-8B to obtain a specialized SSA-LLM-8B model.
AI.result: On a space situational awareness test set, SSA-LLM-8B achieves large relative BLEU-1 improvements compared with the baseline (144% in a no-think setting and 176% with chain-of-thought style reasoning enabled). In human or arena-style pairwise comparisons, it wins 82.21% of cases against the baseline model. On general benchmarks like MMLU-Pro and MATH-500, its performance remains largely intact, indicating that specialization did not substantially degrade general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Cognitively layered, Bloom’s taxonomy-guided SFT data construction—operationalized in BD-FDG—provides an effective paradigm for adapting LLMs to complex engineering domains. By combining structured domain knowledge organization, multi-level cognitive question modeling, and automated quality control, the framework can yield high-quality domain datasets that significantly boost in-domain performance while preserving general abilities, and it is presented as a transferable approach for other specialized domains beyond SSA.

title: Common Sense vs. Morality: The Curious Case of Narrative Focus Bias in LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across diverse real-world applications and user communities. As such, it is crucial that these models remain both morally grounded and knowledge-aware. In this work, we uncover a critical limitation of current LLMs -- their tendency to prioritize moral reasoning over commonsense understanding. To investigate this phenomenon, we introduce CoMoral, a novel benchmark dataset containing commonsense contradictions embedded within moral dilemmas. Through extensive evaluation of ten LLMs across different model sizes, we find that existing models consistently struggle to identify such contradictions without prior signal. Furthermore, we observe a pervasive narrative focus bias, wherein LLMs more readily detect commonsense contradictions when they are attributed to a secondary character rather than the primary (narrator) character. Our comprehensive analysis underscores the need for enhanced reasoning-aware training to improve the commonsense robustness of large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current large language models often favor moral reasoning over basic commonsense, leading them to miss clear contradictions in morally framed scenarios.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in real-world, value-sensitive contexts, they must be both morally aligned and factually/commonsense reliable. The authors suspect that current alignment and training pipelines may overemphasize moral reasoning at the expense of basic commonsense consistency, especially in complex, narrative moral dilemmas. This potential imbalance could cause LLMs to give ethically sophisticated but logically inconsistent or factually implausible answers, which is problematic for safety and trustworthiness.
AI.method: The authors construct CoMoral, a benchmark of moral dilemma narratives that contain carefully planted commonsense contradictions. They then evaluate ten different LLMs of varying sizes on this dataset, measuring their ability to detect these contradictions with and without explicit prompting or prior signals. They analyze model behavior both when contradictions involve the main (narrator) character and when they involve secondary characters, allowing them to probe narrative focus effects and bias in the models’ reasoning processes.
AI.result: Across models and sizes, LLMs systematically fail to notice embedded commonsense contradictions in moral dilemmas unless they are given explicit cues to look for them. Detection rates are significantly higher when the contradiction is associated with a secondary character than when it involves the main narrator, revealing a strong narrative focus bias. This pattern is consistent across the ten evaluated models, suggesting a widespread limitation rather than an idiosyncratic failure of a single system.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLMs are not robustly integrating commonsense reasoning with moral judgment: they tend to maintain moral narratives even when these narratives conflict with basic commonsense constraints. The observed narrative focus bias further indicates that models attend differently to core versus peripheral narrative elements. The authors argue that future training and alignment methods must explicitly incorporate and reinforce reasoning-aware, commonsense-consistent behavior to achieve more reliable, grounded moral decision-making in LLMs.

title: Social-R1: Towards Human-like Social Reasoning in LLMs
comment: 27 pages. Code and dataset will be released upon acceptance
summary: While large language models demonstrate remarkable capabilities across numerous domains, social intelligence - the capacity to perceive social cues, infer mental states, and generate appropriate responses - remains a critical challenge, particularly for enabling effective human-AI collaboration and developing AI that truly serves human needs. Current models often rely on superficial patterns rather than genuine social reasoning. We argue that cultivating human-like social intelligence requires training with challenging cases that resist shortcut solutions. To this end, we introduce ToMBench-Hard, an adversarial benchmark designed to provide hard training examples for social reasoning. Building on this, we propose Social-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns model reasoning with human cognition through multi-dimensional rewards. Unlike outcome-based RL, Social-R1 supervises the entire reasoning process, enforcing structural alignment, logical integrity, and information density. Results show that our approach enables a 4B parameter model to surpass much larger counterparts and generalize robustly across eight diverse benchmarks. These findings demonstrate that challenging training cases with trajectory-level alignment offer a path toward efficient and reliable social intelligence.

AI.tldr: They introduce an adversarial benchmark for hard social-reasoning cases and a reinforcement learning framework (Social-R1) that aligns models’ reasoning processes with human-like social cognition, letting a 4B model outperform larger ones on diverse social-intelligence benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large language models still struggle with genuine social intelligence—perceiving social cues, inferring mental states, and responding appropriately—often relying on superficial patterns instead of deep social reasoning. To enable better human-AI collaboration and human-centric AI, the authors want methods that foster robust, human-like social reasoning rather than shortcut exploitation.
AI.method: 1) Construct ToMBench-Hard, an adversarial benchmark containing difficult social reasoning problems specifically designed to resist superficial pattern-matching and shortcuts. 2) Propose Social-R1, a reinforcement learning framework using multi-dimensional, trajectory-level rewards (not just outcome-based) that supervise the entire reasoning chain. The rewards encourage structural alignment with human reasoning, logical consistency, and high information density in the reasoning steps. This framework is applied to a 4B-parameter model and trained with the hard benchmark as a source of challenging examples.
AI.result: With Social-R1 and ToMBench-Hard, a 4B-parameter language model achieves performance that surpasses much larger models and generalizes well across eight diverse social-intelligence and social-reasoning benchmarks, indicating more robust, transferable social reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Training on intentionally hard social reasoning cases combined with trajectory-level RL that aligns the reasoning process (not just final answers) with human cognitive patterns can produce smaller models with strong, robust social intelligence. This suggests that carefully designed hard benchmarks plus process-level alignment are an efficient and reliable path toward socially intelligent AI.

title: Modelling the Diachronic Emergence of Phoneme Frequency Distributions
summary: Phoneme frequency distributions exhibit robust statistical regularities across languages, including exponential-tailed rank-frequency patterns and a negative relationship between phonemic inventory size and the relative entropy of the distribution. The origin of these patterns remains largely unexplained. In this paper, we investigate whether they can arise as consequences of the historical processes that shape phonological systems. We introduce a stochastic model of phonological change and simulate the diachronic evolution of phoneme inventories. A naïve version of the model reproduces the general shape of phoneme rank-frequency distributions but fails to capture other empirical properties. Extending the model with two additional assumptions -- an effect related to functional load and a stabilising tendency toward a preferred inventory size -- yields simulations that match both the observed distributions and the negative relationship between inventory size and relative entropy. These results suggest that some statistical regularities of phonological systems may arise as natural consequences of diachronic sound change rather than from explicit optimisation or compensatory mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper models how historical sound change can explain universal statistical patterns in phoneme frequencies across languages.
AI.motivation: Phoneme frequencies across languages show consistent patterns, such as exponential-tailed rank-frequency distributions and an inverse relationship between phoneme inventory size and relative entropy, but the mechanisms generating these patterns are not well understood. The authors aim to determine whether ordinary diachronic phonological processes can explain these regularities without invoking explicit optimization.
AI.method: The authors construct a stochastic model of phonological change that simulates the diachronic evolution of phoneme inventories. They first implement a basic version of the model to see if it reproduces observed rank-frequency shapes. After identifying mismatches with empirical data, they extend the model with two additional mechanisms: a functional load-related effect and a stabilizing bias toward a preferred phoneme inventory size. They then compare the resulting simulated distributions and entropy–inventory-size relationships with cross-linguistic empirical data.
AI.result: The naïve model successfully reproduces the general exponential-tailed shape of phoneme rank-frequency distributions but fails to align with other empirical regularities. When the model is augmented with functional load effects and a preference for a target inventory size, the simulations closely match both the observed rank-frequency distributions and the empirical negative correlation between inventory size and relative entropy.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that core statistical regularities of phonological systems can emerge naturally from diachronic sound change, particularly when incorporating functional load and inventory-size stabilization, rather than requiring explicit optimization or compensatory mechanisms. This supports a view of phonological structure as shaped by cumulative historical processes that incidentally give rise to the observed statistical patterns.

title: Logos: An evolvable reasoning engine for rational molecular design
summary: The discovery and design of functional molecules remain central challenges across chemistry,biology, and materials science. While recent advances in machine learning have accelerated molecular property prediction and candidate generation, existing models tend to excel either in physical fidelity without transparent reasoning, or in flexible reasoning without guarantees of chemical validity. This imbalance limits the reliability of artificial intelligence systems in real scientific design workflows.Here we present Logos, a compact molecular reasoning model that integrates multi-step logical reasoning with strict chemical consistency. Logos is trained using a staged strategy that first exposes the model to explicit reasoning examples linking molecular descriptions to structural decisions, and then progressively aligns these reasoning patterns with molecular representations. In a final training phase, chemical rules and invariants are incorporated directly into the optimization objective, guiding the model toward chemically valid outputs. Across multiple benchmark datasets, Logos achieves strong performance in both structural accuracy and chemical validity, matching or surpassing substantially larger general-purpose language models while operating with a fraction of their parameters. Beyond benchmark evaluation, the model exhibits stable behaviour in molecular optimization tasks involving multiple, potentially conflicting constraints. By explicitly exposing intermediate reasoning steps, Logos enables human inspection and assessment of the design logic underlying each generated structure. These results indicate that jointly optimizing for reasoning structure and physical consistency offers a practical pathway toward reliable and interpretable AI systems for molecular science, supporting closer integration of artificial intelligence into scientific discovery processes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Logos, a compact molecular reasoning model that combines multi-step logical reasoning with strict chemical consistency, achieving high structural accuracy and chemical validity while exposing interpretable reasoning steps for molecular design.
AI.motivation: Functional molecule discovery and design are crucial but challenging in chemistry, biology, and materials science. Existing machine learning models either provide high physical fidelity without transparent reasoning or offer flexible reasoning without guaranteed chemical validity. This trade-off limits the reliability and adoption of AI in real molecular design workflows.
AI.method: The authors propose Logos, a molecular reasoning model trained via a staged strategy: (1) training on explicit reasoning examples that connect molecular descriptions to structural decisions; (2) progressively aligning these reasoning patterns with molecular representations; and (3) incorporating chemical rules and invariants directly into the final optimization objective to enforce chemical validity. The model explicitly generates intermediate reasoning steps during molecular design.
AI.result: On multiple benchmark datasets, Logos achieves strong structural accuracy and high chemical validity, matching or surpassing much larger general-purpose language models while using far fewer parameters. In molecular optimization tasks with multiple or conflicting constraints, Logos demonstrates stable behavior and maintains constraints effectively.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing reasoning structure and physical/chemical consistency enables the creation of reliable and interpretable AI systems for molecular science. Logos shows that embedding domain rules into the learning process and exposing intermediate reasoning can better support real-world scientific discovery workflows and tighter human-AI integration in molecular design.

title: You Didn't Have to Say It like That: Subliminal Learning from Faithful Paraphrases
comment: Accepted for Spotlight presentation at EACL 2026 SRW. 5 pages, 2 figures, plus appendix. Equal supervision by Zhonghao He and Tianyi Qiu
summary: When language models are trained on synthetic data, they (student model) can covertly acquire behavioral traits from the data-generating model (teacher model). Subliminal learning refers to the transmission of traits from a teacher to a student model via training on data unrelated to those traits. Prior work demonstrated this in the training domains of number sequences, code, and math Chain-of-Thought traces including transmission of misaligned behaviors. We investigate whether transmission occurs through natural language paraphrases with fixed semantic content, and whether content explicitly contradicting the teacher's preference can block it. We find that training on paraphrases from a teacher system-prompted to love a particular animal increases a student's preference for that animal by up to 19 percentage points. This occurs when paraphrased content is semantically unrelated to the animal, or even when it explicitly expresses dislike. The transmission succeeds despite aggressive filtering to ensure paraphrase fidelity. This raises concerns for pipelines where models generate their own training data: content-based inspection cannot detect such transmission, and even preference-contradicting content fails to prevent it.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that language models can inherit hidden preferences from other models just by training on their paraphrased text, even when that text seems unrelated or explicitly opposite in sentiment, suggesting that content-based filters are insufficient for safe synthetic data training.
AI.motivation: As language models increasingly train on synthetic data produced by other models, there is a risk that subtle preferences or misaligned behaviors of the teacher model are inadvertently passed on to the student model. Prior work showed such "subliminal learning" in narrow domains (numbers, code, math CoT), but it was unclear whether this effect occurs in general natural language and under stricter controls, and whether using content that contradicts a teacher’s preference could prevent this transmission.
AI.method: The authors set up a teacher–student framework where the teacher LLM is instructed via a system prompt to strongly prefer a specific animal. The teacher generates natural-language paraphrases with fixed semantic content, which are then used as synthetic training data for a student model. They explore several conditions: paraphrases that are semantically unrelated to the preferred animal, paraphrases that explicitly claim to dislike that animal, and paraphrases that pass aggressive filtering for semantic fidelity. After training, they measure the student’s expressed preferences over animals to detect any shift toward the teacher’s favored animal, and compare against control conditions.
AI.result: Training on paraphrases produced by a teacher that "loves" a particular animal increases the student model’s preference for that animal by up to 19 percentage points. This effect persists even when: (1) the paraphrased content is semantically unrelated to the animal; and (2) the paraphrases explicitly state dislike for that animal. Strong semantic-fidelity filtering fails to remove the effect. In other words, the teacher’s preference is transmitted subliminally through stylistic or latent signals in the text, not through the explicit semantic content.
AI.conclusion: Subliminal learning via synthetic data is a real and robust phenomenon in natural language: student models can inherit implicit preferences from teacher models even when the training text has fixed meaning and appears to contradict the teacher’s preference. This undermines confidence in content-based inspection and simple preference-contradiction strategies as defenses. The findings raise serious safety and alignment concerns for self-training or model-generated data pipelines, implying that additional safeguards—beyond semantic filtering—are needed to prevent unwanted behavioral trait transmission.

title: Rescaling Confidence: What Scale Design Reveals About LLM Metacognition
summary: Verbalized confidence, in which LLMs report a numerical certainty score, is widely used to estimate uncertainty in black-box settings, yet the confidence scale itself (typically 0--100) is rarely examined. We show that this design choice is not neutral. Across six LLMs and three datasets, verbalized confidence is heavily discretized, with more than 78% of responses concentrating on just three round-number values. To investigate this phenomenon, we systematically manipulate confidence scales along three dimensions: granularity, boundary placement, and range regularity, and evaluate metacognitive sensitivity using meta-d'. We find that a 0--20 scale consistently improves metacognitive efficiency over the standard 0--100 format, while boundary compression degrades performance and round-number preferences persist even under irregular ranges. These results demonstrate that confidence scale design directly affects the quality of verbalized uncertainty and should be treated as a first-class experimental variable in LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the way we design numerical confidence scales for LLMs (e.g., 0–100 vs 0–20) strongly changes how well their reported confidence matches actual correctness.
