AI.motivation: Verbalized confidence is increasingly used to quantify uncertainty from LLMs in black-box settings, but researchers usually take the standard 0–100% scale for granted. The authors suspect that the structure of this scale itself may bias responses and reduce the quality of uncertainty estimates, so they want to study how scale design impacts metacognitive performance.
AI.method: The authors test six different LLMs on three datasets and ask them to output numerical confidence scores. They analyze how those scores are distributed and observe strong discretization and round-number preferences. Then they systematically change the confidence scale along three factors—granularity (e.g., 0–20 vs 0–100), boundary placement (how endpoints are set), and range regularity (whether intervals are even or irregular)—and measure metacognitive sensitivity using meta-d' to evaluate how well confidence tracks correctness.
AI.result: They find that LLMs’ verbalized confidence is highly discretized, with over 78% of responses falling on three round numbers on typical 0–100 scales. A coarser 0–20 scale reliably increases metacognitive efficiency compared with the standard 0–100 scale. Compressing boundaries harms performance, and even when the range is made irregular, LLMs still show a strong preference for round numbers.
AI.conclusion: Confidence scale design is not a neutral choice: it directly affects the quality of LLMs’ reported uncertainty. Using a 0–20 scale can yield better metacognitive efficiency than 0–100, while some manipulations like boundary compression are harmful. Round-number biases are robust. Therefore, confidence scales should be actively designed and treated as key experimental variables in LLM evaluation, not fixed defaults.

title: ALARM: Audio-Language Alignment for Reasoning Models
summary: Large audio language models (ALMs) extend LLMs with auditory understanding. A common approach freezes the LLM and trains only an adapter on self-generated targets. However, this fails for reasoning LLMs (RLMs) whose built-in chain-of-thought traces expose the textual surrogate input, yielding unnatural responses. We propose self-rephrasing, converting self-generated responses into audio-understanding variants compatible with RLMs while preserving distributional alignment. We further fuse and compress multiple audio encoders for stronger representations. For training, we construct a 6M-instance multi-task corpus (2.5M unique prompts) spanning 19K hours of speech, music, and sound. Our 4B-parameter ALM outperforms similarly sized models and surpasses most larger ALMs on related audio-reasoning benchmarks, while preserving textual capabilities with a low training cost. Notably, we achieve the best open-source result on the MMAU-speech and MMSU benchmarks and rank third among all the models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new training strategy and architecture to build efficient large audio language models that work well with reasoning LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on several audio reasoning benchmarks with relatively small model size and training cost.
AI.motivation: Existing large audio language models usually adapt a frozen LLM with an audio adapter trained on self-generated text targets. This works poorly with reasoning LLMs whose chain-of-thought reveals the textual surrogate of the audio input, leading to unnatural responses and misalignment between audio understanding and reasoning behavior. There is a need for an audio-LLM integration method that preserves the reasoning LLM’s behavior while achieving strong audio understanding, all under modest compute budgets.
AI.method:
1) Propose a self-rephrasing strategy that converts the model’s own text responses into audio-understanding variants compatible with reasoning LLMs, hiding the explicit textual surrogate of the audio but preserving distributional alignment with the base RLM.
2) Design an ALM architecture that fuses and compresses multiple audio encoders to obtain stronger, more compact audio representations as input to the frozen RLM.
3) Build a large-scale, 6M-instance multi-task audio-text corpus (2.5M unique prompts, 19K hours) spanning speech, music, and general sounds for training.
4) Train a 4B-parameter ALM on this corpus with the above strategies, keeping training cost relatively low while maintaining the original textual capabilities of the underlying RLM.
AI.result: The resulting 4B-parameter audio language model outperforms other models of similar size and surpasses most larger open models on audio reasoning benchmarks. It achieves state-of-the-art open-source performance on MMAU-speech and MMSU, ranking third among all models (including closed-source), while retaining strong text-only performance. The fusion/compression of multiple audio encoders and the self-rephrasing training procedure contribute to these gains without requiring expensive full-LLM finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Self-rephrasing is an effective way to align audio understanding with reasoning LLMs without exposing textual surrogate inputs or degrading the model’s reasoning behavior. Combined with fused, compressed audio encoders and a large multi-task audio corpus, it enables a relatively small (4B) ALM to match or exceed much larger models on key audio reasoning benchmarks while keeping training costs low and preserving text capabilities. This provides a practical recipe for building efficient, high-performing ALMs on top of reasoning LLMs.

title: Curveball Steering: The Right Direction To Steer Isn't Always Linear
summary: Activation steering is a widely used approach for controlling large language model (LLM) behavior by intervening on internal representations. Existing methods largely rely on the Linear Representation Hypothesis, assuming behavioral attributes can be manipulated using global linear directions. In practice, however, such linear interventions often behave inconsistently. We question this assumption by analyzing the intrinsic geometry of LLM activation spaces. Measuring geometric distortion via the ratio of geodesic to Euclidean distances, we observe substantial and concept-dependent distortions, indicating that activation spaces are not well-approximated by a globally linear geometry. Motivated by this, we propose "Curveball steering", a nonlinear steering method based on polynomial kernel PCA that performs interventions in a feature space, better respecting the learned activation geometry. Curveball steering consistently outperforms linear PCA-based steering, particularly in regimes exhibiting strong geometric distortion, suggesting that geometry-aware, nonlinear steering provides a principled alternative to global, linear interventions.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that LLM activations live in a geometrically distorted, nonlinear space, making global linear steering unreliable, and introduces a nonlinear, geometry-aware steering method called Curveball steering that works better.
AI.motivation: Activation steering is widely used to control LLM behavior, and most methods assume that desired behaviors can be controlled by moving along global linear directions in activation space (Linear Representation Hypothesis). Empirically, however, linear interventions often fail or behave inconsistently. The authors are motivated to understand whether this failure comes from a mismatch between the assumed linear geometry and the true intrinsic geometry of LLM activations, and to design a steering method that respects this geometry.
AI.method: 1) Analyze the intrinsic geometry of LLM activation spaces by comparing geodesic distances (distances along the manifold) to Euclidean distances, thereby quantifying geometric distortion. 2) Show that these distortions are substantial and depend on the concept/attribute. 3) Based on this, propose Curveball steering: instead of intervening linearly in the raw activation space, they map activations into a nonlinear feature space using polynomial kernel PCA and perform steering there, which better aligns with the curved geometry of the manifold. 4) Compare Curveball steering with standard linear PCA-based steering on LLM control tasks, especially in regimes with high geometric distortion.
AI.result: They find that LLM activation spaces exhibit strong, concept-dependent geometric distortion, contradicting the assumption that a single global linear geometry is adequate. Curveball steering, which operates in a nonlinear feature space via polynomial kernel PCA, consistently outperforms linear PCA-based steering on steering tasks. The performance gains are especially large in settings where the measured geometric distortion is high.
AI.conclusion: The Linear Representation Hypothesis is an oversimplification for LLM activation spaces, which are better viewed as curved manifolds than as globally linear subspaces. Because of this, global linear interventions are inherently limited and can be unreliable. Geometry-aware, nonlinear interventions like Curveball steering—implemented via kernel methods that respect activation manifold curvature—offer a more principled and more effective alternative for controlling LLM behavior, particularly in highly distorted regions of activation space.

title: Build, Borrow, or Just Fine-Tune? A Political Scientist's Guide to Choosing NLP Models
summary: Political scientists increasingly face a consequential choice when adopting natural language processing tools: build a domain-specific model from scratch, borrow and adapt an existing one, or simply fine-tune a general-purpose model on task data? Each approach occupies a different point on the spectrum of performance, cost, and required expertise, yet the discipline has offered little empirical guidance on how to navigate this trade-off. This paper provides such guidance. Using conflict event classification as a test case, I fine-tune ModernBERT on the Global Terrorism Database (GTD) to create Confli-mBERT and systematically compare it against ConfliBERT, a domain-specific pretrained model that represents the current gold standard. Confli-mBERT achieves 75.46% accuracy compared to ConfliBERT's 79.34%. Critically, the four-percentage-point gap is not uniform: on high-frequency attack types such as Bombing/Explosion (F1 = 0.95 vs. 0.96) and Kidnapping (F1 = 0.92 vs. 0.91), the models are nearly indistinguishable. Performance differences concentrate in rare event categories comprising fewer than 2% of all incidents. I use these findings to develop a practical decision framework for political scientists considering any NLP-assisted research task: when does the research question demand a specialized model, and when does an accessible fine-tuned alternative suffice? The answer, I argue, depends not on which model is "better" in the abstract, but on the specific intersection of class prevalence, error tolerance, and available resources. The model, training code, and data are publicly available on Hugging Face.

AI.tldr: The paper compares fine-tuning a general-purpose language model (ModernBERT) to using a specialized domain model (ConfliBERT) for conflict event classification, and proposes a decision framework for choosing between them based on task needs and constraints.
AI.motivation: Political scientists increasingly use NLP but lack empirical guidance on whether to build domain-specific models, adapt existing ones, or fine-tune general-purpose models. Each option involves trade-offs in accuracy, cost, and expertise, and the discipline needs concrete evidence and actionable criteria to navigate these choices.
AI.method: The author fine-tunes ModernBERT on the Global Terrorism Database to create Confli-mBERT for conflict event classification, then systematically compares its performance to ConfliBERT, a domain-specific pretrained model. The comparison focuses on overall accuracy and per-class F1 scores across event types, paying special attention to how performance varies between frequent and rare classes. Based on these empirical results, the author then derives a practical framework for deciding when specialized or fine-tuned general-purpose models are appropriate.
AI.result: Confli-mBERT reaches 75.46% accuracy, slightly below ConfliBERT’s 79.34%. For common attack types like Bombing/Explosion and Kidnapping, their F1 scores are nearly identical, while performance gaps are concentrated in rare event categories that make up less than 2% of incidents. This shows that a fine-tuned general-purpose model can closely match a specialized model on frequent classes but lags on rare, data-scarce categories.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that model choice should be driven by the specific research context rather than abstract notions of which model is best. In particular, the importance of rare classes, tolerance for errors in them, and constraints on data, expertise, and compute should determine whether a specialized domain model is necessary or whether a more accessible fine-tuned general-purpose model is sufficient. The work also contributes practical resources by releasing the model, training code, and data.

title: Robust Regularized Policy Iteration under Transition Uncertainty
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) enables data-efficient and safe policy learning without online exploration, but its performance often degrades under distribution shift. The learned policy may visit out-of-distribution state-action pairs where value estimates and learned dynamics are unreliable. To address policy-induced extrapolation and transition uncertainty in a unified framework, we formulate offline RL as robust policy optimization, treating the transition kernel as a decision variable within an uncertainty set and optimizing the policy against the worst-case dynamics. We propose Robust Regularized Policy Iteration (RRPI), which replaces the intractable max-min bilevel objective with a tractable KL-regularized surrogate and derives an efficient policy iteration procedure based on a robust regularized Bellman operator. We provide theoretical guarantees by showing that the proposed operator is a $γ$-contraction and that iteratively updating the surrogate yields monotonic improvement of the original robust objective with convergence. Experiments on D4RL benchmarks demonstrate that RRPI achieves strong average performance, outperforming recent baselines including percentile-based methods such as PMDB on the majority of environments while remaining competitive on the rest. Moreover, RRPI exhibits robust behavior. The learned $Q$-values decrease in regions with higher epistemic uncertainty, suggesting that the resulting policy avoids unreliable out-of-distribution actions under transition uncertainty.

AI.tldr: They propose a robust offline RL algorithm (RRPI) that treats dynamics as uncertain, optimizes policies for worst-case transitions via a KL-regularized robust Bellman operator, and empirically outperforms strong baselines on D4RL while avoiding out-of-distribution actions.
AI.motivation: Offline RL is attractive because it can learn policies safely and data-efficiently from fixed datasets without online exploration, but standard methods struggle when the learned policy visits state-action pairs not well-covered by the dataset. In such out-of-distribution regions, both value estimates and learned dynamics models become unreliable, leading to performance degradation. Existing approaches typically address either policy-induced extrapolation error or model uncertainty, but not both in a unified and principled way. The authors are motivated to create a framework that simultaneously handles distribution shift and transition uncertainty in offline RL with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: They formulate offline RL as a robust policy optimization problem where the transition kernel is treated as a decision variable within an uncertainty set, and the policy is optimized against the worst-case dynamics in that set. Directly solving the resulting max-min bilevel optimization is intractable, so they introduce a KL-regularized surrogate objective that makes the problem tractable. Based on this surrogate, they develop Robust Regularized Policy Iteration (RRPI), which uses a robust regularized Bellman operator to perform policy evaluation and improvement. They analyze this operator and design an iterative procedure that alternates between updating value estimates and improving the policy with respect to the robust objective.
AI.result: Theoretically, they prove that the robust regularized Bellman operator is a γ-contraction, which implies that iteratively applying it converges to a unique fixed point. They further show that using this surrogate operator in RRPI yields monotonic improvement with respect to the original robust objective and converges to a robust policy. Empirically, on D4RL offline RL benchmarks, RRPI achieves strong average performance, outperforming recent baseline methods, including percentile-based approaches like PMDB, on most environments and remaining competitive in the others. The learned Q-functions under RRPI exhibit decreased values in regions with high epistemic uncertainty, indicating that the method encourages avoidance of unreliable out-of-distribution actions.
AI.conclusion: Offline RL can be effectively improved by casting it as robust policy optimization over uncertain dynamics and solving a tractable KL-regularized surrogate via policy iteration. The proposed RRPI algorithm is theoretically sound—guaranteeing contraction, monotonic improvement, and convergence—and empirically strong on standard benchmarks, providing robust behavior under distribution shift. By down-weighting Q-values in high-uncertainty regions, RRPI naturally steers policies away from out-of-distribution actions, mitigating extrapolation and transition uncertainty in a unified way.

title: Surgical Repair of Collapsed Attention Heads in ALiBi Transformers
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures, 2 supplementary figures. Code: https://github.com/Palmerschallon/bloom-head-surgery Checkpoints: https://huggingface.co/TheNexus42/bloom-1b7-head-surgery
summary: We identify a systematic attention collapse pathology in the BLOOM family of transformer language models, where ALiBi positional encoding causes 31-44% of attention heads to attend almost entirely to the beginning-of-sequence token. The collapse follows a predictable pattern across four model scales (560M to 7.1B parameters), concentrating in head indices where ALiBi's slope schedule imposes the steepest distance penalties. We introduce surgical reinitialization: targeted Q/K/V reinitialization with zeroed output projections and gradient-masked freezing of all non-surgical parameters. Applied to BLOOM-1b7 on a single consumer GPU, the technique recovers 98.7% operational head capacity (242 to 379 of 384 heads) in two passes. A controlled comparison with C4 training data confirms that reinitialization -- not corpus content -- drives recovery, and reveals two distinct post-surgical phenomena: early global functional redistribution that improves the model, and late local degradation that accumulates under noisy training signal. An extended experiment reinitializing mostly-healthy heads alongside collapsed ones produces a model that transiently outperforms stock BLOOM-1b7 by 25% on training perplexity (12.70 vs. 16.99), suggesting that pretrained attention configurations are suboptimal local minima. Code, checkpoints, and diagnostic tools are released as open-source software.

AI.tldr: The paper discovers and fixes a systematic "attention collapse" problem in BLOOM models using ALiBi, by surgically reinitializing problematic attention heads, largely restoring and even temporarily improving performance.
AI.motivation: Investigate why many attention heads in BLOOM models using ALiBi positional encoding behave pathologically—focusing almost only on the beginning-of-sequence token—and determine whether and how this failure mode can be repaired efficiently after pretraining.
AI.method: 1) Empirically analyze attention patterns across four BLOOM model sizes to characterize the collapse and its relation to ALiBi slope schedules. 2) Propose "surgical reinitialization", where selected Q/K/V projections of collapsed heads are reinitialized, their output projections are zeroed, and all non-targeted parameters are frozen via gradient masking. 3) Retrain only these surgical components on C4 data using a single consumer GPU and compare with a control condition to isolate the effect of reinitialization from data. 4) Run an extended variant that reinitializes mostly-healthy heads together with collapsed ones to test whether pretrained attention configurations are globally optimal.
AI.result: They find that 31–44% of attention heads across 560M–7.1B BLOOM models collapse to attending almost exclusively to the BOS token, especially where ALiBi imposes steep distance penalties. With surgical reinitialization on BLOOM-1b7, operational attention heads increase from 242/384 to 379/384 (98.7% capacity) in two passes. A controlled C4 experiment shows that recovery is driven by the reinitialization process itself, not corpus selection, and reveals distinct phases of post-surgical behavior: (a) early global functional redistribution that improves performance and (b) later local degradation due to noisy training signals. Reinitializing mostly-healthy heads as well yields a model that temporarily beats stock BLOOM-1b7 by ~25% in training perplexity (12.70 vs. 16.99).
AI.conclusion: ALiBi-based BLOOM models exhibit a predictable, large-scale attention collapse affecting up to ~44% of heads, tied to specific ALiBi slope indices. This pathology can be substantially and efficiently repaired post hoc by targeted surgical reinitialization of attention heads, without full retraining, and the resulting models can even outperform the original, implying that standard pretraining leaves attention configurations stuck in suboptimal local minima. The work suggests that post-hoc structural interventions and diagnostics are a promising direction for improving pretrained transformers and understanding their inductive biases.

title: AI Act Evaluation Benchmark: An Open, Transparent, and Reproducible Evaluation Dataset for NLP and RAG Systems
summary: The rapid rollout of AI in heterogeneous public and societal sectors has subsequently escalated the need for compliance with regulatory standards and frameworks. The EU AI Act has emerged as a landmark in the regulatory landscape. The development of solutions that elicit the level of AI systems' compliance with such standards is often limited by the lack of resources, hindering the semi-automated or automated evaluation of their performance. This generates the need for manual work, which is often error-prone, resource-limited or limited to cases not clearly described by the regulation. This paper presents an open, transparent, and reproducible method of creating a resource that facilitates the evaluation of NLP models with a strong focus on RAG systems. We have developed a dataset that contain the tasks of risk-level classification, article retrieval, obligation generation, and question-answering for the EU AI Act. The dataset files are in a machine-to-machine appropriate format. To generate the files, we utilise domain knowledge as an exegetical basis, combining with the processing and reasoning power of large language models to generate scenarios along with the respective tasks. Our methodology demonstrates a way to harness language models for grounded generation with high document relevancy. Besides, we overcome limitations such as navigating the decision boundaries of risk-levels that are not explicitly defined within the EU AI Act, such as limited and minimal cases. Finally, we demonstrate our dataset's effectiveness by evaluating a RAG-based solution that reaches 0.87 and 0.85 F1-score for prohibited and high-risk scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new, open and reproducible dataset for evaluating NLP, especially RAG systems, on tasks derived from the EU AI Act, including risk classification, article retrieval, obligation generation, and QA.
AI.motivation: AI systems are being rapidly deployed across public and societal sectors, increasing the need to comply with regulatory standards like the EU AI Act. Existing solutions to assess compliance are constrained by a lack of structured resources, making evaluation manual, error-prone, and incomplete, especially in edge cases not clearly specified in the regulation. There is a need for machine-readable datasets that enable semi‑automated or automated evaluation of AI systems’ regulatory compliance.
AI.method: The authors construct a machine-to-machine formatted dataset centered on the EU AI Act. They use domain expertise as an interpretive (exegetical) basis and combine it with large language models’ processing and reasoning capabilities to generate realistic scenarios and associated tasks. The dataset supports four tasks: (1) risk-level classification of AI scenarios, (2) retrieval of relevant EU AI Act articles, (3) generation of obligations that apply to a scenario, and (4) question answering over the Act. Their methodology focuses on grounded generation: prompting LLMs constrained by the source regulatory text and expert understanding, and explicitly addressing ambiguous risk boundaries, such as limited and minimal risk categories not directly defined in the Act.
AI.result: They produce a publicly available, transparent, and reproducible dataset encoded for machine-to-machine use, covering multiple EU AI Act–related tasks. Using this dataset, they evaluate a RAG-based system, which achieves F1-scores of 0.87 for prohibited-risk and 0.85 for high-risk classifications, showing that the dataset is suitable to benchmark and stress-test compliance-oriented NLP/RAG systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that their methodology effectively harnesses large language models, grounded in expert knowledge and the EU AI Act text, to create high-relevance, regulation-based scenarios and tasks. The resulting dataset fills an important gap for evaluating NLP and RAG systems on regulatory compliance, including nuanced risk-level boundaries that are under-specified in the law. Their initial RAG evaluation indicates that the dataset is practical and informative for measuring performance on compliance-related tasks, and they position it as a reusable resource for future research and system development in AI regulation compliance.

title: Tracking Cancer Through Text: Longitudinal Extraction From Radiology Reports Using Open-Source Large Language Models
summary: Radiology reports capture crucial longitudinal information on tumor burden, treatment response, and disease progression, yet their unstructured narrative format complicates automated analysis. While large language models (LLMs) have advanced clinical text processing, most state-of-the-art systems remain proprietary, limiting their applicability in privacy-sensitive healthcare environments. We present a fully open-source, locally deployable pipeline for longitudinal information extraction from radiology reports, implemented using the \texttt{llm\_extractinator} framework. The system applies the \texttt{qwen2.5-72b} model to extract and link target, non-target, and new lesion data across time points in accordance with RECIST criteria. Evaluation on 50 Dutch CT Thorax/Abdomen report pairs yielded high extraction performance, with attribute-level accuracies of 93.7\% for target lesions, 94.9\% for non-target lesions, and 94.0\% for new lesions. The approach demonstrates that open-source LLMs can achieve clinically meaningful performance in multi-timepoint oncology tasks while ensuring data privacy and reproducibility. These results highlight the potential of locally deployable LLMs for scalable extraction of structured longitudinal data from routine clinical text.

AI.tldr: An open-source, locally deployable LLM pipeline extracts longitudinal RECIST-style lesion information from radiology reports with high accuracy, enabling privacy-preserving oncology analytics.
AI.motivation: Radiology reports contain rich longitudinal information on tumor status but are written as unstructured narratives, making it hard to automatically track lesion burden, treatment response, and disease progression over time. Existing high-performing clinical NLP systems are often proprietary cloud services, which are problematic for privacy-sensitive healthcare settings and limit reproducibility. There is a need for an open-source, locally deployable solution that can perform clinically meaningful longitudinal information extraction aligned with oncology standards like RECIST.
AI.method: The authors build a fully open-source pipeline on top of the llm_extractinator framework, using the qwen2.5-72b open-source LLM. The system is designed to parse radiology reports, identify and extract attributes of target, non-target, and new lesions, and link these entities across multiple time points according to RECIST criteria. They evaluate the pipeline on a dataset of 50 Dutch CT Thorax/Abdomen report pairs, measuring attribute-level extraction accuracy for each lesion category.
AI.result: On 50 pairs of Dutch CT Thorax/Abdomen radiology reports, the system achieved high attribute-level accuracy: 93.7% for target lesions, 94.9% for non-target lesions, and 94.0% for new lesions. These results indicate that the open-source qwen2.5-72b-based pipeline can reliably capture detailed longitudinal lesion information from real-world clinical reports.
AI.conclusion: Open-source, locally deployable LLMs can deliver clinically meaningful performance on complex longitudinal oncology NLP tasks, such as RECIST-style lesion tracking from narrative radiology reports. The proposed pipeline shows that privacy-preserving, reproducible solutions are feasible without relying on proprietary models, and highlights the potential for scalable extraction of structured longitudinal data from routine clinical text in healthcare settings.

title: An Empirical Study and Theoretical Explanation on Task-Level Model-Merging Collapse
summary: Model merging unifies independently fine-tuned LLMs from the same base, enabling reuse and integration of parallel development efforts without retraining. However, in practice we observe that merging does not always succeed: certain combinations of task-specialist models suffer from catastrophic performance degradation after merging. We refer to this failure mode as merging collapse. Intuitively, collapse arises when the learned representations or parameter adjustments for different tasks are fundamentally incompatible, so that merging forces destructive interference rather than synergy. In this paper, we identify and characterize the phenomenon of task-level merging collapse, where certain task combinations consistently trigger huge performance degradation across all merging methods. Through extensive experiments and statistical analysis, we demonstrate that representational incompatibility between tasks is strongly correlated with merging collapse, while parameter-space conflict metrics show minimal correlation, challenging conventional wisdom in model merging literature. We provide a theoretical explanation on this phenomenon through rate-distortion theory with a dimension-dependent bound, establishing fundamental limits on task mergeability regardless of methodology.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why merging separately fine-tuned LLMs sometimes fails drastically, identifying a phenomenon called task-level merging collapse and explaining it theoretically via representational incompatibility and rate-distortion limits.
AI.motivation: Model merging promises to reuse and integrate many independently fine-tuned models without expensive joint training, but in practice some merges catastrophically degrade performance. Existing work lacks a clear understanding of when and why such failures occur, and relies on parameter-space heuristics that may not capture true incompatibilities between tasks. The paper aims to systematically identify these failure cases and uncover the underlying principles governing when tasks are mergeable.
AI.method: The authors empirically examine many pairs/sets of task-specialized LLMs fine-tuned from a common base and apply a wide range of existing merging techniques. They define and detect "task-level merging collapse" as large, consistent performance drops across methods for specific task combinations. They compute both representational incompatibility measures (e.g., comparing internal activations/representations between task models) and parameter-space conflict metrics, and statistically correlate these with observed collapse. Finally, they develop a theoretical framework based on rate-distortion theory with a dimension-dependent bound to formalize limits on how multiple tasks can be jointly represented in a shared model without excessive loss.
AI.result: Experiments show that certain task combinations lead to severe performance collapse after merging, regardless of the specific merging algorithm used. Representational incompatibility between the task models strongly predicts these collapses, whereas common parameter-space conflict metrics exhibit little correlation with failure. This contradicts much prior intuition that focuses on weight-space similarity as the main indicator of mergeability. The theoretical analysis derives a rate-distortion-based bound that depends on representation dimensionality, showing that there are inherent limits to how accurately multiple tasks can be jointly encoded when their optimal representations are too incompatible.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that task-level merging collapse is a real and systematic phenomenon, driven primarily by incompatibilities in learned representations rather than simple parameter conflicts. This implies that many existing weight-space heuristics are insufficient for predicting mergeability. Their rate-distortion-based theory establishes fundamental, architecture- and method-agnostic limits on which tasks can be successfully merged, suggesting future work should focus on representation-aware metrics and training strategies to improve mergeability rather than only designing new weight-merging formulas.

title: Understanding the Interplay between LLMs' Utilisation of Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: A keynote at ECIR 2025
summary: Language Models (LMs) acquire parametric knowledge from their training process, embedding it within their weights. The increasing scalability of LMs, however, poses significant challenges for understanding a model's inner workings and further for updating or correcting this embedded knowledge without the significant cost of retraining. Moreover, when using these language models for knowledge-intensive language understanding tasks, LMs have to integrate relevant context, mitigating their inherent weaknesses, such as incomplete or outdated knowledge. Nevertheless, studies indicate that LMs often ignore the provided context as it can be in conflict with the pre-existing LM's memory learned during pre-training. Conflicting knowledge can also already be present in the LM's parameters, termed intra-memory conflict. This underscores the importance of understanding the interplay between how a language model uses its parametric knowledge and the retrieved contextual knowledge. In this talk, I will aim to shed light on this important issue by presenting our research on evaluating the knowledge present in LMs, diagnostic tests that can reveal knowledge conflicts, as well as on understanding the characteristics of successfully used contextual knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how language models use and sometimes conflict between their internal (parametric) knowledge and external contextual information, and proposes ways to evaluate and diagnose these knowledge conflicts.
AI.motivation: As language models grow larger, it becomes harder to understand, update, or correct the knowledge stored in their parameters, and they often ignore provided context when it conflicts with what they already "know", leading to unreliable behavior in knowledge-intensive tasks.
AI.method: The work evaluates what knowledge is stored in language model parameters, designs diagnostic tests to surface conflicts between internal model memory and external or internal conflicting facts, and analyzes contextual examples where the model successfully uses retrieved information instead of its own conflicting knowledge.
AI.result: They identify and characterize situations where language models favor their parametric knowledge over contextual input, show how different kinds of knowledge conflicts manifest (including intra-memory conflict within the model’s own parameters), and demonstrate diagnostic tools that can reveal these conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Understanding and diagnosing how language models reconcile parametric and contextual knowledge is crucial for building more reliable knowledge-intensive systems, and the presented evaluations and tests offer a path toward better control over when and how models use external context versus internal memory.

title: Telogenesis: Goal Is All U Need
summary: Goal-conditioned systems assume goals are provided externally. We ask whether attentional priorities can emerge endogenously from an agent's internal cognitive state. We propose a priority function that generates observation targets from three epistemic gaps: ignorance (posterior variance), surprise (prediction error), and staleness (temporal decay of confidence in unobserved variables). We validate this in two systems: a minimal attention-allocation environment (2,000 runs) and a modular, partially observable world (500 runs). Ablation shows each component is necessary. A key finding is metric-dependent reversal: under global prediction error, coverage-based rotation wins; under change detection latency, priority-guided allocation wins, with advantage growing monotonically with dimensionality (d = -0.95 at N=48, p < 10^-6). Detection latency follows a power law in attention budget, with a steeper exponent for priority-guided allocation (0.55 vs. 0.40). When the decay rate is made learnable per variable, the system spontaneously recovers environmental volatility structure without supervision (t = 22.5, p < 10^-6). We demonstrate that epistemic gaps alone, without external reward, suffice to generate adaptive priorities that outperform fixed strategies and recover latent environmental structure.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an internal, unsupervised attention-priority mechanism based purely on epistemic gaps (ignorance, surprise, staleness) and shows it can outperform fixed attention strategies and recover latent environmental structure without external rewards.
AI.motivation: Most goal-conditioned and attention systems assume externally specified goals or rewards to decide what to attend to. The authors are motivated by the question of whether an agent can endogenously decide where to allocate attention purely from its own uncertainty and changing beliefs, without any externally defined reward signal or task. They aim to understand if such internal epistemic signals can generate adaptive, scalable attention policies and uncover hidden structure in the environment.
AI.method: They define a priority function that scores each observable variable based on three epistemic quantities: ignorance (posterior variance), surprise (prediction error), and staleness (how long since a variable was last observed, modeled as a temporal decay of confidence). This priority function is then used to choose which variables to observe at each timestep under an attention budget. They test this mechanism in two settings: (1) a minimal attention-allocation environment with many randomized runs, and (2) a more complex modular, partially observable world. They conduct ablation studies by removing each epistemic component in turn, and compare against fixed policies such as coverage-based rotation. They analyze metrics like global prediction error, change detection latency, scaling with dimensionality, and how performance changes when decay rates are allowed to be learned per variable.
AI.result: The authors find that all three epistemic components—ignorance, surprise, and staleness—are necessary for best performance; removing any one degrades results. They discover a metric-dependent reversal: if evaluated on global prediction error, a simple coverage-based rotation policy performs best, but if evaluated on change detection latency, the priority-based scheme is superior, and its advantage grows with state dimensionality (correlation d = -0.95 at N = 48, p < 10^-6). Detection latency scales as a power law with attention budget, with the priority-guided allocation having a steeper, more favorable exponent (0.55 vs 0.40). When they allow the confidence decay rate to be learned separately for each variable, the system recovers the true volatility structure of the environment without explicit supervision (t = 22.5, p < 10^-6).
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that purely epistemic signals—uncertainty, surprise, and time-varying confidence—are sufficient to generate internal attentional priorities without any external reward. These endogenously generated priorities can outperform simple fixed strategies on relevant metrics like change detection, especially in high-dimensional settings, and can even uncover latent properties of the environment such as variable-specific volatilities. This supports a view of attention as driven by internal epistemic gaps rather than solely by task-defined goals.

title: Automatic Cardiac Risk Management Classification using large-context Electronic Patients Health Records
summary: To overcome the limitations of manual administrative coding in geriatric Cardiovascular Risk Management, this study introduces an automated classification framework leveraging unstructured Electronic Health Records (EHRs). Using a dataset of 3,482 patients, we benchmarked three distinct modeling paradigms on longitudinal Dutch clinical narratives: classical machine learning baselines, specialized deep learning architectures optimized for large-context sequences, and general-purpose generative Large Language Models (LLMs) in a zero-shot setting. Additionally, we evaluated a late fusion strategy to integrate unstructured text with structured medication embeddings and anthropometric data. Our analysis reveals that the custom Transformer architecture outperforms both traditional methods and generative \acs{llm}s, achieving the highest F1-scores and Matthews Correlation Coefficients. These findings underscore the critical role of specialized hierarchical attention mechanisms in capturing long-range dependencies within medical texts, presenting a robust, automated alternative to manual workflows for clinical risk stratification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated text-based system to classify geriatric cardiovascular risk from unstructured EHR notes, where a custom long-context Transformer performs best.
AI.motivation: Manual administrative coding for cardiovascular risk management in older adults is time-consuming, error-prone, and does not scale well when relying on unstructured EHR narratives. There is a need for automated methods that can accurately classify patient risk from rich clinical text, and to understand whether general-purpose LLMs can replace or complement specialized models in this domain.
AI.method: Using longitudinal Dutch clinical narratives from 3,482 patients, the authors compare three modeling paradigms for automated risk classification: (1) classical machine learning baselines applied to text features, (2) specialized deep learning architectures tailored for long-context sequences (including a custom Transformer with hierarchical attention), and (3) general-purpose generative LLMs used in a zero-shot configuration. They also explore a late fusion approach that integrates representations from unstructured text with structured medication embeddings and anthropometric variables. Performance is assessed using F1-score and Matthews Correlation Coefficient.
AI.result: The custom Transformer-based architecture, designed with hierarchical attention for long-range dependency modeling, outperforms traditional machine learning baselines and zero-shot generative LLMs on the classification task, achieving the highest F1-scores and Matthews Correlation Coefficients across models. The late fusion with structured data is investigated but the abstract emphasizes the superiority of the specialized Transformer over the alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Specialized deep learning models that incorporate hierarchical attention and are optimized for long medical narratives provide a robust automated alternative to manual administrative coding for geriatric cardiovascular risk stratification. General-purpose LLMs in zero-shot mode are less effective for this specific classification task, highlighting the need for domain-adapted, architecture-aware solutions when working with lengthy clinical text in EHRs.

title: GenePlan: Evolving Better Generalized PDDL Plans using Large Language Models
summary: We present GenePlan (GENeralized Evolutionary Planner), a novel framework that leverages large language model (LLM) assisted evolutionary algorithms to generate domain-dependent generalized planners for classical planning tasks described in PDDL. By casting generalized planning as an optimization problem, GenePlan iteratively evolves interpretable Python planners that minimize plan length across diverse problem instances. In empirical evaluation across six existing benchmark domains and two new domains, GenePlan achieved an average SAT score of 0.91, closely matching the performance of the state-of-the-art planners (SAT score 0.93), and significantly outperforming other LLM-based baselines such as chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting (average SAT score 0.64). The generated planners solve new instances rapidly (average 0.49 seconds per task) and at low cost (average $1.82 per domain using GPT-4o).

AI.tldr: GenePlan is a framework that uses LLM-assisted evolutionary algorithms to automatically synthesize generalized planners in Python for PDDL domains, achieving performance close to state-of-the-art specialized planners and better than other LLM prompting methods.
AI.motivation: Generalized planning aims to create a single planner that can solve many instances of a planning domain, but existing approaches either require substantial human engineering, are domain-specific, or are hard to interpret and adapt. With the advent of LLMs, there is an opportunity to automatically induce such planners, yet naive prompting strategies perform poorly and are costly or unreliable across diverse instances. The paper is motivated by the need for an automated, interpretable, and efficient way to generate high-quality generalized planners for classical planning domains, leveraging LLM capabilities in a structured optimization framework.
AI.method: The authors cast generalized planning as an optimization problem where the objective is to minimize plan length over a set of training problem instances in a given PDDL domain. They propose GenePlan, which uses an evolutionary algorithm guided by an LLM (e.g., GPT-4o) to iteratively mutate and recombine candidate Python planners. The LLM helps generate and refine code variants, while a fitness function evaluates each candidate planner by running it on multiple planning tasks and scoring it based on solution quality (e.g., plan length and success). Over successive generations, the population of planners evolves toward higher-performing, domain-dependent generalized planners that remain human-interpretable Python programs.
AI.result: Across six standard benchmark domains and two newly introduced ones, GenePlan achieves an average SAT score of 0.91, which is close to that of state-of-the-art hand-crafted or specialized generalized planners (0.93). It clearly outperforms other LLM-based baselines like chain-of-thought prompting, which only reaches an average SAT score of 0.64. The resulting planners generalize to new problem instances, solving them quickly (average 0.49 seconds per task) and at relatively low monetary cost for training (around $1.82 per domain using GPT-4o).
AI.conclusion: GenePlan demonstrates that combining evolutionary search with LLM-guided code generation is an effective way to automatically synthesize domain-specific generalized planners for classical planning in PDDL. The framework produces interpretable Python planners that perform close to state-of-the-art systems while significantly surpassing naive LLM prompting baselines, and it does so with low inference and monetary cost. This suggests that LLM-assisted evolutionary optimization is a promising direction for automating the design of high-quality planning algorithms and potentially other decision-making procedures.

title: Fusing Semantic, Lexical, and Domain Perspectives for Recipe Similarity Estimation
comment: Preprint version submitted to IEEE Big Data 2025
summary: This research focuses on developing advanced methods for assessing similarity between recipes by combining different sources of information and analytical approaches. We explore the semantic, lexical, and domain similarity of food recipes, evaluated through the analysis of ingredients, preparation methods, and nutritional attributes. A web-based interface was developed to allow domain experts to validate the combined similarity results. After evaluating 318 recipe pairs, experts agreed on 255 (80%). The evaluation of expert assessments enables the estimation of which similarity aspects--lexical, semantic, or nutritional--are most influential in expert decision-making. The application of these methods has broad implications in the food industry and supports the development of personalized diets, nutrition recommendations, and automated recipe generation systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes methods to measure recipe similarity using multiple dimensions (semantic, lexical, nutritional) and validates them with expert judgments via a web interface.
AI.motivation: Existing recipe similarity measures are limited, often relying on a single type of information such as ingredients or text similarity. There is a need for more comprehensive, multi-faceted similarity models to better support applications like personalized nutrition, recommendation systems, and automated recipe design.
AI.method: The authors combine semantic, lexical, and domain-specific (nutritional and preparation-related) similarity metrics for recipes. They compute similarity based on ingredients, preparation methods, and nutritional attributes, then provide an integrated similarity score. A web-based interface is built so domain experts can inspect recipe pairs and indicate whether they consider them similar. A set of 318 recipe pairs is evaluated this way, and the expert agreements with the automatic similarity estimates are recorded and analyzed to infer the relative importance of each similarity component.
AI.result: Out of 318 evaluated recipe pairs, human experts agreed on 255 similarity decisions, reflecting about 80% agreement with the combined similarity approach. Analysis of expert feedback reveals which types of similarity (lexical, semantic, nutritional) carry more weight in human judgments of recipe similarity.
AI.conclusion: Multi-dimensional similarity assessment that integrates semantic, lexical, and nutritional information provides an effective way to capture human notions of recipe similarity. The validated model has practical applications in the food industry, facilitating personalized diet design, nutrition recommendation systems, and automated recipe generation, where accurate assessment of recipe similarity is crucial.

title: Vibe-Creation: The Epistemology of Human-AI Emergent Cognition
summary: The encounter between human reasoning and generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) cannot be adequately described by inherited metaphors of tool use, augmentation, or collaborative partnership. This article argues that such interactions produce a qualitatively distinct cognitive-epistemic formation, designated here as the Third Entity: an emergent, transient structure that arises from the transductive coupling of two ontologically incommensurable modes of cognition. Drawing on Peirce semiotics, Polanyi theory of tacit knowledge, Simondon philosophy of individuation, Ihde postphenomenology, and Morin complexity theory, we develop a multi-layered theoretical account of this formation. We introduce the concept of vibe-creation to designate the pre-reflective cognitive mode through which the Third Entity navigates high-dimensional semantic space and argue that this mode constitutes the automation of tacit knowledge - a development with far-reaching consequences for epistemology, the philosophy of mind, and educational theory. We further propose the notion of asymmetric emergence to characterize the agency of the Third Entity: genuinely novel and irreducible, yet anchored in human intentional responsibility. The article concludes by examining the implications of this theoretical framework for the transformation of educational institutions and the redefinition of intellectual competence in the age of GenAI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that human interactions with generative AI create a new, emergent "Third Entity" of cognition that is neither simply human nor machine, transforming how knowledge and expertise should be understood, especially in education.
AI.motivation: Existing metaphors for GenAI (tool, augmentation, partner) are insufficient to capture the qualitative novelty of human-AI cognitive interaction. The authors aim to develop a richer conceptual framework to understand what kind of cognitive and epistemic structure emerges when humans and GenAI systems interact, and what this implies for knowledge, mind, and education.
AI.method: The paper is theoretical and conceptual rather than empirical. It synthesizes and extends multiple philosophical and theoretical traditions—Peirce's semiotics, Polanyi's tacit knowledge, Simondon's individuation, Ihde's postphenomenology, and Morin's complexity theory—to build a multi-layered account of the emergent "Third Entity" formed by human–GenAI coupling. It introduces new concepts like "vibe-creation" and "asymmetric emergence" to frame these interactions.
AI.result: The authors articulate the "Third Entity" as an emergent, transient cognitive-epistemic formation that arises when human cognition couples with GenAI. They conceptualize "vibe-creation" as the pre-reflective, high-dimensional semantic navigation characteristic of this entity and argue that it amounts to the automation of tacit knowledge. They also define "asymmetric emergence" to describe the Third Entity's agency as both genuinely novel and irreducibly new, yet still grounded in and constrained by human intentional responsibility.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that human–GenAI interaction should be understood as giving rise to a distinct emergent cognitive entity with partial agency, rather than as simple tool use or collaboration. This reconceptualization has major implications for epistemology, theories of mind, and especially education: institutions and notions of intellectual competence must be reshaped to account for the Third Entity, the automation of tacit knowledge, and the new forms of reasoning and responsibility that arise in the age of generative AI.

title: ESAinsTOD: A Unified End-to-End Schema-Aware Instruction-Tuning Framework for Task-Oriented Dialog Modeling
comment: Published at International Journal of Machine Learning and Cybernetics (IJMLC)
summary: Existing end-to-end modeling methods for modular task-oriented dialog systems are typically tailored to specific datasets, making it challenging to adapt to new dialog scenarios. In this work, we propose ESAinsTOD, a unified End-to-end Schema-Aware Instruction-tuning framework for general Task-Oriented Dialog modeling. This framework introduces a structured methodology to go beyond simply fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs), enabling flexible adaptation to various dialogue task flows and schemas. Specifically, we leverage full-parameter fine-tuning of LLMs and introduce two alignment mechanisms to make the resulting system both instruction-aware and schema-aware: (i) instruction alignment, which ensures that the system faithfully follows task instructions to complete various task flows from heterogeneous TOD datasets; and (ii) schema alignment, which encourages the system to make predictions adhering to the specified schema. In addition, we employ session-level end-to-end modeling, which allows the system to access the results of previously executed task flows within the dialogue history, to bridge the gap between the instruction-tuning paradigm and the real-world application of TOD systems. Empirical results show that while a fine-tuned LLM serves as a strong baseline, our structured approach provides significant additional benefits. In particular, our findings indicate that: (i) ESAinsTOD outperforms state-of-the-art models by a significant margin on end-to-end task-oriented dialog modeling benchmarks: CamRest676, In-Car and MultiWOZ; (ii) more importantly, it exhibits superior generalization capabilities across various low-resource settings, with the proposed alignment mechanisms significantly enhancing zero-shot performance; and (iii) our instruction-tuning paradigm substantially improves the model's robustness against data noise and cascading errors.

AI.tldr: ESAinsTOD is a unified, schema-aware instruction-tuning framework that fine-tunes LLMs end-to-end for task-oriented dialogue, using instruction and schema alignment plus session-level modeling to improve adaptability, generalization, and robustness over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing end-to-end modular task-oriented dialog (TOD) systems are usually designed around specific datasets and schemas, which makes it hard to transfer them to new domains, task flows, or dialog schemas. The authors want a single, general framework that can handle heterogeneous TOD datasets, follow natural-language task instructions, obey structured schemas, and work robustly in realistic multi-turn, multi-task dialog scenarios, especially under low-resource and noisy conditions.
AI.method: They propose ESAinsTOD, which fully fine-tunes a large language model and adds two core alignment mechanisms: (1) instruction alignment, so the model reliably follows task instructions for different dialog task flows from heterogeneous datasets; and (2) schema alignment, so the model’s predictions conform to the given structured schema (e.g., slots, APIs). They adopt session-level end-to-end modeling, where the model conditions on the entire dialog history including outcomes of earlier task flows, thereby connecting instruction-tuning with realistic TOD execution. The approach is evaluated on standard TOD benchmarks against strong baselines, including straightforward LLM fine-tuning.
AI.result: ESAinsTOD significantly surpasses state-of-the-art models on CamRest676, In-Car, and MultiWOZ end-to-end TOD benchmarks. It generalizes better in low-resource regimes and yields notably higher zero-shot performance, especially due to the proposed instruction and schema alignment mechanisms. The framework also makes the model more robust to data noise and cascading errors compared with simple LLM fine-tuning baselines.
AI.conclusion: A structured, schema-aware instruction-tuning framework for end-to-end TOD, built on fully fine-tuned LLMs and equipped with explicit instruction and schema alignment plus session-level modeling, yields a more adaptable, generalizable, and robust TOD system than prior dataset-specific or naively fine-tuned approaches. ESAinsTOD provides a practical path toward unified TOD modeling across diverse dialog schemas and data conditions.

title: Enhancing Debunking Effectiveness through LLM-based Personality Adaptation
comment: In: Computational Intelligence. IJCCI 2025. Springer, Cham (2026)
summary: This study proposes a novel methodology for generating personalized fake news debunking messages by prompting Large Language Models (LLMs) with persona-based inputs aligned to the Big Five personality traits: Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, and Openness. Our approach guides LLMs to transform generic debunking content into personalized versions tailored to specific personality profiles. To assess the effectiveness of these transformations, we employ a separate LLM as an automated evaluator simulating corresponding personality traits, thereby eliminating the need for costly human evaluation panels. Our results show that personalized messages are generally seen as more persuasive than generic ones. We also find that traits like Openness tend to increase persuadability, while Neuroticism can lower it. Differences between LLM evaluators suggest that using multiple models provides a clearer picture. Overall, this work demonstrates a practical way to create more targeted debunking messages exploiting LLMs, while also raising important ethical questions about how such technology might be used.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an LLM-based method to turn generic fake‑news debunking texts into personality-tailored messages and evaluates them automatically with another LLM.
AI.motivation: Generic fact-checking or debunking messages often fail to persuade diverse audiences because they ignore individual psychological differences, such as personality traits. Conducting large-scale human studies to test personalized messaging is expensive and slow. With the rise of LLMs and concerns about misinformation, there is a need for scalable, ethically-aware methods to create and assess personalized debunking content that might more effectively counter fake news.
AI.method: The authors (1) design prompts that encode personas along the Big Five personality traits (Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Neuroticism, Openness); (2) feed generic fake-news debunking messages plus these persona prompts into an LLM, which rewrites the debunking content in a style tailored to the specified personality profile; (3) use a separate LLM as an automated evaluator, also prompted to simulate specific personality traits, to judge the relative persuasiveness of the personalized versus generic messages; and (4) compare results across traits and across different LLM evaluators to understand consistency and trait effects on perceived persuasiveness.
AI.result: The personalized debunking messages are typically rated by the LLM evaluators as more persuasive than the original generic messages. Among traits, higher Openness is associated with greater perceived persuadability, whereas higher Neuroticism is associated with lower persuadability. Evaluations differ across LLM models, indicating that relying on a single model could give a biased or incomplete view; using multiple evaluators yields a more nuanced picture.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that LLMs can be used both to generate and to evaluate personality-tailored fake‑news debunking messages at scale, without relying solely on human evaluation panels. This personalization seems to increase perceived persuasiveness and exhibits systematic differences across personality traits. At the same time, the work highlights ethical concerns around deploying such psychologically targeted messaging technologies in real-world information environments, implying a need for safeguards and responsible use guidelines.

title: Evaluation of LLMs in retrieving food and nutritional context for RAG systems
comment: This is the preprint for our conference paper for IEEE International Conference on Big Data
summary: In this article, we evaluate four Large Language Models (LLMs) and their effectiveness at retrieving data within a specialized Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system, using a comprehensive food composition database. Our method is focused on the LLMs ability to translate natural language queries into structured metadata filters, enabling efficient retrieval via a Chroma vector database. By achieving high accuracy in this critical retrieval step, we demonstrate that LLMs can serve as an accessible, high-performance tool, drastically reducing the manual effort and technical expertise previously required for domain experts, such as food compilers and nutritionists, to leverage complex food and nutrition data. However, despite the high performance on easy and moderately complex queries, our analysis of difficult questions reveals that reliable retrieval remains challenging when queries involve non-expressible constraints. These findings demonstrate that LLM-driven metadata filtering excels when constraints can be explicitly expressed, but struggles when queries exceed the representational scope of the metadata format.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well four LLMs translate natural language nutrition-related queries into structured metadata filters for a RAG system, finding strong performance on expressible constraints but failures on queries whose constraints exceed the metadata’s representational capacity.
AI.motivation: Domain experts in food and nutrition (e.g., food compilers and nutritionists) need to query complex food composition databases, but traditional methods require significant manual effort and technical expertise in database query languages and data structures. The paper aims to see if LLMs can bridge this gap by automatically converting natural language questions into machine-usable filters for retrieval in a RAG pipeline.
AI.method: The authors build a specialized Retrieval-Augmented Generation system on top of a comprehensive food composition database and a Chroma vector store. They test four different Large Language Models on their ability to convert natural language user queries into structured metadata filters, which are then used to retrieve relevant entries. Performance is evaluated across easy, moderately complex, and difficult queries, focusing on retrieval accuracy and analyzing where and why failures occur, particularly for queries with non-expressible constraints.
AI.result: The evaluated LLMs achieve high accuracy in generating correct metadata filters for easy and moderately complex queries, enabling effective retrieval from the food composition database. However, for more difficult questions, especially those whose constraints cannot be fully represented in the available metadata schema, retrieval performance drops and becomes unreliable.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven metadata filtering is a powerful and accessible way to query complex food composition data, significantly reducing the need for manual curation and technical database expertise among domain experts. Nonetheless, its reliability is bounded by the expressiveness of the underlying metadata format: when user queries require constraints that the metadata cannot represent, LLMs struggle, exposing a key limitation and suggesting that improvements must either enhance metadata schemas or design systems that can reason beyond simple filter expressions.

title: Context Engineering: From Prompts to Corporate Multi-Agent Architecture
summary: As artificial intelligence (AI) systems evolve from stateless chatbots to autonomous multi-step agents, prompt engineering (PE), the discipline of crafting individual queries, proves necessary but insufficient. This paper introduces context engineering (CE) as a standalone discipline concerned with designing, structuring, and managing the entire informational environment in which an AI agent makes decisions. Drawing on vendor architectures (Google ADK, Anthropic, LangChain), current academic work (ACE framework, Google DeepMind's intelligent delegation), enterprise research (Deloitte, 2026; KPMG, 2026), and the author's experience building a multi-agent system, the paper proposes five context quality criteria: relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, and provenance, and frames context as the agent's operating system. Two higher-order disciplines follow. Intent engineering (IE) encodes organizational goals, values, and trade-off hierarchies into agent infrastructure. Specification engineering (SE) creates a machine-readable corpus of corporate policies and standards enabling autonomous operation of multi-agent systems at scale. Together these four disciplines form a cumulative pyramid maturity model of agent engineering, in which each level subsumes the previous one as a necessary foundation. Enterprise data reveals a gap: while 75% of enterprises plan agentic AI deployment within two years (Deloitte, 2026), deployment has surged and retreated as organizations confront scaling complexity (KPMG, 2026). The Klarna case illustrates a dual deficit, contextual and intentional. Whoever controls the agent's context controls its behavior; whoever controls its intent controls its strategy; whoever controls its specifications controls its scale.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that as AI systems evolve into autonomous agents, focusing only on single prompts is inadequate and proposes a broader discipline called context engineering, along with related disciplines, to reliably scale enterprise AI agents.
AI.motivation: Prompt engineering focuses on individual queries, which is insufficient for multi-step, autonomous AI agents operating in complex enterprise environments. Organizations are eager to deploy agentic AI but are struggling with scaling, reliability, and alignment due to poorly designed informational environments, unclear goals, and missing machine-readable policies. The paper is motivated by this practical deployment gap and the lack of a coherent framework to manage the full environment in which agents act.
AI.method: The paper is largely conceptual and framework-building. It synthesizes insights from vendor architectures (Google ADK, Anthropic, LangChain), existing academic frameworks (ACE, intelligent delegation), and enterprise reports (Deloitte, KPMG), combined with the author’s own experience building a multi-agent system. From this synthesis, it defines new disciplines (CE, IE, SE) and proposes a maturity pyramid for agent engineering along with five specific criteria for high-quality context.
AI.result: The work yields: (1) a definition of context engineering as distinct from prompt engineering; (2) five criteria for evaluating agent context—relevance, sufficiency, isolation, economy, and provenance; (3) the introduction of two higher-order disciplines, intent engineering and specification engineering; and (4) a cumulative pyramid maturity model where PE < CE < IE < SE. It also surfaces an empirical enterprise gap: most firms plan agentic AI deployment, but many roll back deployments due to scaling complexity, as illustrated by the Klarna case.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that in agentic AI, controlling individual prompts is far less important than controlling the broader context, encoded intent, and formal specifications that govern agent behavior. Context functions as the agent’s operating system; intent determines its strategy; specifications enable safe scalability. Robust enterprise deployment therefore requires a layered discipline of agent engineering, where prompt engineering is just a foundational first step and higher-order engineering of context, intent, and specifications is necessary to achieve reliable, scalable, and aligned multi-agent systems.

title: RbtAct: Rebuttal as Supervision for Actionable Review Feedback Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used across the scientific workflow, including to draft peer-review reports. However, many AI-generated reviews are superficial and insufficiently actionable, leaving authors without concrete, implementable guidance and motivating the gap this work addresses. We propose RbtAct, which targets actionable review feedback generation and places existing peer review rebuttal at the center of learning. Rebuttals show which reviewer comments led to concrete revisions or specific plans, and which were only defended. Building on this insight, we leverage rebuttal as implicit supervision to directly optimize a feedback generator for actionability. To support this objective, we propose a new task called perspective-conditioned segment-level review feedback generation, in which the model is required to produce a single focused comment based on the complete paper and a specified perspective such as experiments and writing. We also build a large dataset named RMR-75K that maps review segments to the rebuttal segments that address them, with perspective labels and impact categories that order author uptake. We then train the Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct model with supervised fine-tuning on review segments followed by preference optimization using rebuttal derived pairs. Experiments with human experts and LLM-as-a-judge show consistent gains in actionability and specificity over strong baselines while maintaining grounding and relevance.

AI.tldr: They introduce RbtAct, a method to generate concrete, actionable peer-review comments for scientific papers by learning from how authors respond in rebuttals.
AI.motivation: LLMs are already used to draft peer reviews, but their feedback is often vague and not directly helpful for authors to revise their papers. There is a need for review-generation systems that produce specific, implementable suggestions instead of generic remarks.
AI.method: They define a new task: perspective-conditioned, segment-level review feedback generation, where a model produces one focused comment about a paper from a specific perspective (e.g., experiments, writing). They construct RMR-75K, a dataset that links review segments to rebuttal segments, annotated with perspectives and impact (how much the author acted on the comment). They fine-tune Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct with supervised learning on review segments and then perform preference optimization using rebuttal-derived pairs that encode which feedback led to more concrete author actions.
AI.result: The trained model, RbtAct, generates more actionable and specific review feedback than strong baseline models, while preserving factual grounding and relevance to the paper, as confirmed by both human expert evaluations and LLM-based judging.
AI.conclusion: Using rebuttals as implicit supervision is an effective way to optimize LLMs for actionable review feedback. Their dataset and training pipeline yield reviews that better support authors in making concrete revisions without sacrificing accuracy or relevance.

title: PRECEPT: Planning Resilience via Experience, Context Engineering & Probing Trajectories A Unified Framework for Test-Time Adaptation with Compositional Rule Learning and Pareto-Guided Prompt Evolution
comment: 50 pages, 14 figures. Code and reproducibility resources: https://github.com/arash-shahmansoori/precept-framework
summary:
LLM agents that store knowledge as natural language suffer steep retrieval degradation as condition count grows, often struggle to compose learned rules reliably, and typically lack explicit mechanisms to detect stale or adversarial knowledge. We introduce PRECEPT, a unified framework for test-time adaptation with three tightly coupled components: (1) deterministic exact-match rule retrieval over structured condition keys, (2) conflict-aware memory with Bayesian source reliability and threshold-based rule invalidation, and (3) COMPASS, a Pareto-guided prompt-evolution outer loop. Exact retrieval eliminates partial-match interpretation errors on the deterministic path (0% by construction, vs 94.4% under Theorem~B.6's independence model at N=10) and supports compositional stacking through a semantic tier hierarchy; conflict-aware memory resolves static--dynamic disagreements and supports drift adaptation; COMPASS evaluates prompts through the same end-to-end execution pipeline.
  Results (9--10 seeds): PRECEPT achieves a +41.1pp first-try advantage over Full Reflexion (d>1.9), +33.3pp compositional generalization (d=1.55), 100% $P_1$ on 2-way logistics compositions (d=2.64), +40--55pp continuous learning gains, strong eventual robustness under adversarial static knowledge (100% logistics with adversarial SK active; partial recovery on integration), +55.0pp drift recovery (d=0.95, p=0.031), and 61% fewer steps. Core comparisons are statistically significant, often at p<0.001.

AI.tldr: PRECEPT is a framework that improves test-time adaptation of LLM agents by using structured, exact rule retrieval, conflict-aware memory, and an outer-loop prompt optimization method, leading to large gains in reliability, compositional generalization, robustness to adversarial and drifting knowledge, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: LLM agents that store and retrieve knowledge in free-form natural language degrade sharply as the number of conditions grows, have difficulty reliably composing multiple learned rules, and lack explicit mechanisms to detect, resolve, or discard stale or adversarial knowledge. This limits their reliability in dynamic or adversarial environments and in tasks requiring complex compositional reasoning. The paper aims to create a more reliable, precise, and adaptable test-time learning mechanism for LLM agents.
AI.method: The paper proposes PRECEPT, a unified test-time adaptation framework with three main components: (1) a deterministic, exact-match rule retrieval mechanism that indexes rules by structured condition keys and uses a semantic tier hierarchy to support compositional stacking; (2) a conflict-aware memory system that models source reliability in a Bayesian way and uses thresholds to invalidate or downgrade rules when conflicts or drift are detected, handling disagreements between static and dynamic knowledge; and (3) COMPASS, a Pareto-guided outer-loop procedure that evolves prompts by evaluating them end-to-end on the same execution pipeline, balancing multiple performance objectives. Together, these components replace noisy, heuristic natural-language retrieval with precise, interpretable rule management and systematic prompt optimization.
AI.result: Under extensive experiments with 9–10 random seeds, PRECEPT substantially outperforms baselines such as Full Reflexion across several dimensions: +41.1 percentage points first-try performance advantage (effect size d>1.9), +33.3pp improvement in compositional generalization (d=1.55), 100% first-try success (P1) on 2-way logistics composition tasks (d=2.64), and +40–55pp gains in continuous learning settings. It also shows strong robustness to adversarial static knowledge, maintaining 100% performance on logistics tasks even when adversarial static knowledge is active and achieving partial recovery on integration tasks. PRECEPT enables +55.0pp recovery after distribution drift (d=0.95, p=0.031) and reduces the number of steps required by 61%. Core experimental comparisons are statistically significant, often at p<0.001.
AI.conclusion: Structured, deterministic rule retrieval combined with conflict-aware memory and Pareto-guided prompt evolution yields large and statistically robust improvements in test-time adaptation for LLM agents. PRECEPT avoids partial-match interpretation errors inherent in natural-language retrieval, scales to more conditions while preserving reliability, composes rules effectively, and remains robust under adversarial or drifting knowledge, all while using fewer steps.
