The framework shows that principled representation and management of test-time knowledge can substantially enhance LLM agent performance beyond existing reflection-based or purely natural-language memory methods.

title: Beyond Fine-Tuning: Robust Food Entity Linking under Ontology Drift with FoodOntoRAG
summary: Standardizing food terms from product labels and menus into ontology concepts is a prerequisite for trustworthy dietary assessment and safety reporting. The dominant approach to Named Entity Linking (NEL) in the food and nutrition domains fine-tunes Large Language Models (LLMs) on task-specific corpora. Although effective, fine-tuning incurs substantial computational cost, ties models to a particular ontology snapshot (i.e., version), and degrades under ontology drift. This paper presents FoodOntoRAG, a model- and ontology-agnostic pipeline that performs few-shot NEL by retrieving candidate entities from domain ontologies and conditioning an LLM on structured evidence (food labels, synonyms, definitions, and relations). A hybrid lexical--semantic retriever enumerates candidates; a selector agent chooses a best match with rationale; a separate scorer agent calibrates confidence; and, when confidence falls below a threshold, a synonym generator agent proposes reformulations to re-enter the loop. The pipeline approaches state-of-the-art accuracy while revealing gaps and inconsistencies in existing annotations. The design avoids fine-tuning, improves robustness to ontology evolution, and yields interpretable decisions through grounded justifications.

AI.tldr: FoodOntoRAG is a retrieval-augmented, few-shot named entity linking pipeline for food ontologies that avoids fine-tuning while maintaining near state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Current food-domain NEL methods rely on fine-tuned LLMs, which are computationally expensive, tightly coupled to a fixed ontology version, and degrade as the ontology changes over time. There is a need for a more flexible, maintainable approach that remains accurate under ontology drift and can expose issues in existing annotations.
AI.method: FoodOntoRAG uses a model- and ontology-agnostic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline. A hybrid lexical–semantic retriever first gathers candidate ontology entities using labels, synonyms, definitions, and relations. A selector LLM agent then picks the best match with an explicit rationale. A separate scorer agent estimates confidence of the selected link. If confidence is low, a synonym-generator agent proposes reformulated mentions, which are fed back into the retrieval–selection loop to try again.
AI.result: The pipeline achieves accuracy close to state-of-the-art fine-tuned systems for food NEL while operating in a few-shot, no-fine-tuning regime. It also surfaces gaps and inconsistencies in existing ontology annotations, demonstrating diagnostic value.
AI.conclusion: A retrieval-augmented, multi-agent LLM pipeline can perform competitive food-domain named entity linking without fine-tuning, remain robust to ontology evolution, and produce interpretable, evidence-grounded justifications. This offers a practical alternative to fine-tuned models for standardized dietary assessment and safety reporting.

title: MiniAppBench: Evaluating the Shift from Text to Interactive HTML Responses in LLM-Powered Assistants
summary: With the rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) in code generation, human-AI interaction is evolving from static text responses to dynamic, interactive HTML-based applications, which we term MiniApps. These applications require models to not only render visual interfaces but also construct customized interaction logic that adheres to real-world principles. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on algorithmic correctness or static layout reconstruction, failing to capture the capabilities required for this new paradigm. To address this gap, we introduce MiniAppBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate principle-driven, interactive application generation. Sourced from a real-world application with 10M+ generations, MiniAppBench distills 500 tasks across six domains (e.g., Games, Science, and Tools). Furthermore, to tackle the challenge of evaluating open-ended interactions where no single ground truth exists, we propose MiniAppEval, an agentic evaluation framework. Leveraging browser automation, it performs human-like exploratory testing to systematically assess applications across three dimensions: Intention, Static, and Dynamic. Our experiments reveal that current LLMs still face significant challenges in generating high-quality MiniApps, while MiniAppEval demonstrates high alignment with human judgment, establishing a reliable standard for future research. Our code is available in github.com/MiniAppBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MiniAppBench, a benchmark and corresponding evaluation framework (MiniAppEval) to assess LLMs’ ability to generate realistic, interactive HTML-based applications (MiniApps) that respect real-world principles.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM code-generation benchmarks mostly test algorithmic correctness or static UI layout reconstruction. They do not evaluate whether models can build fully interactive web apps with coherent visual design plus correct, principle-driven interaction logic, which is increasingly important as LLMs are used to generate complex, real-world mini applications.
AI.method:
1) Construct MiniAppBench: collect and distill 500 real-world MiniApp generation tasks from a large-scale deployment (10M+ generations) across six domains such as Games, Science, and Tools.
2) Define a new evaluation target: principle-driven, interactive application generation that requires both interface rendering and dynamic behavior following real-world constraints.
3) Propose MiniAppEval, an agentic evaluation framework that uses browser automation to perform human-like exploratory testing. It systematically tests each generated MiniApp across three dimensions—Intention (does it satisfy the task goal), Static (layout/structure), and Dynamic (interaction logic and behavior)—without requiring a single ground-truth implementation.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on state-of-the-art LLMs show that: (a) models struggle to reliably produce high-quality, fully functional MiniApps across the benchmark’s tasks; and (b) MiniAppEval’s automated, agent-based evaluation yields scores that strongly correlate with human judgments, validating it as an effective automatic evaluator for this type of task.
AI.conclusion: MiniAppBench exposes a significant performance gap in current LLMs’ ability to generate realistic, interactive HTML-based apps, underscoring the need for new methods targeting principle-driven interaction logic. MiniAppEval provides a scalable, human-aligned evaluation framework, creating a solid foundation and standard for future research on LLM-based application generation and testing.

title: EPIC-EuroParl-UdS: Information-Theoretic Perspectives on Translation and Interpreting
comment: 16 pages with appendices, 8 figures to be published in LREC-2026 main conference proceedings
summary: This paper introduces an updated and combined version of the bidirectional English-German EPIC-UdS (spoken) and EuroParl-UdS (written) corpora containing original European Parliament speeches as well as their translations and interpretations. The new version corrects metadata and text errors identified through previous use, refines the content, updates linguistic annotations, and adds new layers, including word alignment and word-level surprisal indices. The combined resource is designed to support research using information-theoretic approaches to language variation, particularly studies comparing written and spoken modes, and examining disfluencies in speech, as well as traditional translationese studies, including parallel (source vs. target) and comparable (original vs. translated) analyses. The paper outlines the updates introduced in this release, summarises previous results based on the corpus, and presents a new illustrative study. The study validates the integrity of the rebuilt spoken data and evaluates probabilistic measures derived from base and fine-tuned GPT-2 and machine translation models on the task of filler particles prediction in interpreting.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an updated, merged English–German EPIC-UdS and EuroParl-UdS corpus of European Parliament speeches (spoken and written) with corrected metadata, refined content, enhanced linguistic annotation, and new layers such as word alignment and word-level surprisal, and illustrates its usefulness via a filler-prediction study using GPT‑2 and MT models.
AI.motivation: Existing EPIC-UdS (spoken) and EuroParl-UdS (written) corpora of European Parliament proceedings had known issues (metadata and text errors) and were separate resources, which limited their reliability and the types of comparative studies (e.g., written vs. spoken, translation vs. original, disfluency analysis) that could be robustly conducted. There was also a need for richer, information-theoretic annotations (e.g., surprisal) to support modern quantitative research on language variation and interpreting.
AI.method: The authors rebuild and merge the two corpora into a single bidirectional English–German resource. They correct previously detected metadata and textual errors, refine corpus content, update linguistic annotations, and add new annotation layers, including word alignments and word-level surprisal indices. They then conduct an illustrative empirical study: validating the integrity of the reconstructed spoken data and assessing probabilistic measures derived from base and fine-tuned GPT‑2 language models and machine translation models on the task of predicting filler particles in interpreting.
AI.result: The outcome is an enriched, harmonised English–German corpus covering both written and spoken European Parliament discourse, with corrected and enhanced annotations and additional layers useful for information-theoretic analyses. The illustrative study shows that the rebuilt spoken corpus behaves as expected (integrity is validated) and that probabilistic measures from GPT‑2-based and MT models are informative for predicting filler particles in interpreting, though the abstract does not provide specific numerical performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: The updated, combined EPIC-UdS and EuroParl-UdS corpus is a robust resource for studying language variation across written and spoken modes and for conducting translation and interpreting research, especially from an information-theoretic perspective. The added word alignments and surprisal indices broaden the analytical possibilities, and the filler-prediction study demonstrates both the quality of the rebuilt spoken data and the practical value of using modern probabilistic language and MT models with this corpus.

title: Logics-Parsing-Omni Technical Report
summary: Addressing the challenges of fragmented task definitions and the heterogeneity of unstructured data in multimodal parsing, this paper proposes the Omni Parsing framework. This framework establishes a Unified Taxonomy covering documents, images, and audio-visual streams, introducing a progressive parsing paradigm that bridges perception and cognition. Specifically, the framework integrates three hierarchical levels: 1) Holistic Detection, which achieves precise spatial-temporal grounding of objects or events to establish a geometric baseline for perception; 2) Fine-grained Recognition, which performs symbolization (e.g., OCR/ASR) and attribute extraction on localized objects to complete structured entity parsing; and 3) Multi-level Interpreting, which constructs a reasoning chain from local semantics to global logic. A pivotal advantage of this framework is its evidence anchoring mechanism, which enforces a strict alignment between high-level semantic descriptions and low-level facts. This enables ``evidence-based'' logical induction, transforming unstructured signals into standardized knowledge that is locatable, enumerable, and traceable. Building on this foundation, we constructed a standardized dataset and released the Logics-Parsing-Omni model, which successfully converts complex audio-visual signals into machine-readable structured knowledge. Experiments demonstrate that fine-grained perception and high-level cognition are synergistic, effectively enhancing model reliability. Furthermore, to quantitatively evaluate these capabilities, we introduce OmniParsingBench. Code, models and the benchmark are released at https://github.com/alibaba/Logics-Parsing/tree/master/Logics-Parsing-Omni.

AI.tldr: Omni Parsing proposes a unified, evidence-grounded framework and benchmark to parse multimodal (document, image, audio-visual) data into structured, machine-readable knowledge via progressive, three-level parsing.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal parsing suffers from fragmented task definitions (each task is defined separately, e.g., OCR, VQA, ASR, detection) and highly heterogeneous unstructured data across modalities. This fragmentation makes it difficult to build general, reliable systems that can handle documents, images, and audio-visual streams in a unified way and to connect low-level perception (e.g., detection, OCR/ASR) with high-level reasoning. The authors want a single paradigm that standardizes how multimodal signals are parsed into structured, traceable knowledge and that enforces that high-level semantic outputs are explicitly grounded in low-level evidence.
AI.method: They design the Omni Parsing framework, which defines a Unified Taxonomy that spans documents, images, and audio-visual streams, and a progressive, hierarchical parsing paradigm with three stages: (1) Holistic Detection: spatial-temporal grounding of objects and events, giving a geometric baseline for perception; (2) Fine-grained Recognition: symbolization (e.g., OCR for text in images, ASR for speech in audio) and attribute extraction on localized objects, completing structured entity parsing; (3) Multi-level Interpreting: building reasoning chains that link local semantics to global logic and high-level understanding. A key technical principle is evidence anchoring: every high-level semantic or logical statement must be strictly aligned with concrete low-level facts (detected regions, time segments, recognized text/speech), enabling evidence-based logical induction. On top of this framework, they build a standardized dataset, train and release the Logics-Parsing-Omni model that implements the parsing process on complex audio-visual inputs, and design OmniParsingBench as a benchmark to quantitatively evaluate fine-grained perception and high-level cognition within this framework.
AI.result: The Logics-Parsing-Omni model, based on the proposed framework and dataset, can convert complex audio-visual signals into machine-readable, structured knowledge that is explicitly grounded in low-level evidence. Experiments show that jointly modeling fine-grained perception (detection, symbolization, attribute extraction) and high-level cognition (reasoning, global logic construction) yields synergistic benefits: models become more reliable and perform better than treating these components separately. The OmniParsingBench benchmark provides quantitative evaluation across the proposed hierarchy, demonstrating the effectiveness of the evidence anchoring mechanism and the progressive parsing design.
AI.conclusion: Omni Parsing offers a general, unified solution for multimodal parsing that bridges low-level perception and high-level cognition across documents, images, and audio-visual streams. By enforcing evidence anchoring and using a three-level progressive parsing pipeline, it can transform raw, heterogeneous signals into standardized, locatable, enumerable, and traceable knowledge. The released model (Logics-Parsing-Omni), dataset, and OmniParsingBench benchmark show that this approach improves reliability and provides a practical foundation for future multimodal reasoning and knowledge extraction systems.

title: One-Eval: An Agentic System for Automated and Traceable LLM Evaluation
summary: Reliable evaluation is essential for developing and deploying large language models, yet in practice it often requires substantial manual effort: practitioners must identify appropriate benchmarks, reproduce heterogeneous evaluation codebases, configure dataset schema mappings, and interpret aggregated metrics. To address these challenges, we present One-Eval, an agentic evaluation system that converts natural-language evaluation requests into executable, traceable, and customizable evaluation workflows. One-Eval integrates (i) NL2Bench for intent structuring and personalized benchmark planning, (ii) BenchResolve for benchmark resolution, automatic dataset acquisition, and schema normalization to ensure executability, and (iii) Metrics \& Reporting for task-aware metric selection and decision-oriented reporting beyond scalar scores. The system further incorporates human-in-the-loop checkpoints for review, editing, and rollback, while preserving sample evidence trails for debugging and auditability. Experiments show that One-Eval can execute end-to-end evaluations from diverse natural-language requests with minimal user effort, supporting more efficient and reproducible evaluation in industrial settings. Our framework is publicly available at https://github.com/OpenDCAI/One-Eval.

AI.tldr: One-Eval is an agent-based system that turns natural-language evaluation requests for LLMs into fully executable, traceable evaluation workflows with minimal user effort.
AI.motivation: Evaluating large language models is crucial but currently cumbersome: users must manually choose benchmarks, wrangle heterogeneous evaluation codebases, align dataset schemas, and interpret raw metrics. This makes evaluations slow, irreproducible, and hard to scale, especially in industrial environments. The paper aims to automate and standardize this process so that non-expert practitioners can reliably evaluate models via simple natural-language queries.
AI.method: The authors design One-Eval, an agentic evaluation framework composed of three main components: (i) NL2Bench, which parses a user’s natural-language evaluation request, structures the intent, and generates a personalized benchmark plan; (ii) BenchResolve, which automatically locates and acquires appropriate benchmarks, normalizes dataset schemas, and ensures the resulting pipelines are executable; and (iii) Metrics & Reporting, which selects task-appropriate metrics and produces decision-focused reports beyond single scalar scores. The system also adds human-in-the-loop checkpoints for reviewing, editing, and rolling back workflow steps, while storing sample-level evidence for future debugging and audits. They then empirically test its ability to execute end-to-end evaluations from varied natural-language requests.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that One-Eval can successfully translate diverse natural-language evaluation instructions into complete, working evaluation workflows with little additional user configuration. It reliably acquires benchmarks, normalizes schemas, runs evaluations, and generates meaningful reports, thereby reducing manual engineering effort and improving consistency of evaluations in industrial settings.
AI.conclusion: One-Eval provides a practical, agent-based solution for automating LLM evaluation from natural-language specifications. By integrating intent understanding, benchmark resolution, schema normalization, and task-aware reporting—while keeping humans in the loop and preserving evidence trails—it enables more efficient, reproducible, and auditable evaluation pipelines. The framework is released as open source, encouraging adoption and extension by the community.

title: Chow-Liu Ordering for Long-Context Reasoning in Chain-of-Agents
comment: Published as a workshop paper at ICLR 2026 Workshop MemAgents
summary:
Sequential multi-agent reasoning frameworks such as Chain-of-Agents (CoA) handle long-context queries by decomposing inputs into chunks and processing them sequentially using LLM-based worker agents that read from and update a bounded shared memory. From a probabilistic perspective, CoA aims to approximate the conditional distribution corresponding to a model capable of jointly reasoning over the entire long context. CoA achieves this through a latent-state factorization in which only bounded summaries of previously processed evidence are passed between agents. The resulting bounded-memory approximation introduces a lossy information bottleneck, making the final evidence state inherently dependent on the order in which chunks are processed.
  In this work, we study the problem of chunk ordering for long-context reasoning. We use the well-known Chow-Liu trees to learn a dependency structure that prioritizes strongly related chunks. Empirically, we show that a breadth-first traversal of the resulting tree yields chunk orderings that reduce information loss across agents and consistently outperform both default document-chunk ordering and semantic score-based ordering in answer relevance and exact-match accuracy across three long-context benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to optimally order text chunks in sequential multi-agent (CoA-style) long-context reasoning, using Chow-Liu trees to determine a breadth-first chunk processing order that better preserves information and improves QA accuracy.
AI.motivation: Sequential multi-agent frameworks like Chain-of-Agents handle long contexts by chunking documents and using bounded shared memory, which creates an information bottleneck and makes performance sensitive to the order in which chunks are processed. There is little principled work on how to choose this chunk order to minimize information loss and maximize reasoning quality.
AI.method: Model the ideal long-context reasoner as a joint probabilistic model and interpret CoA as a bounded-memory latent-state factorization that processes chunks sequentially. Learn inter-chunk dependency structure using Chow-Liu trees trained on chunk co-occurrence or relatedness statistics. Use a breadth-first traversal of the learned tree to produce a chunk ordering that prioritizes processing strongly related chunks close together, thereby reducing information lost at the memory bottleneck.
AI.result: Across three long-context benchmarks, breadth-first chunk ordering derived from Chow-Liu trees leads to lower information loss across agents and consistently higher performance in answer relevance and exact-match accuracy when compared with default (document-order) chunking and semantic similarity-based ordering baselines.
AI.conclusion: A principled, probabilistic treatment of chunk ordering in bounded-memory multi-agent reasoning shows that order matters substantially, and that Chow-Liu-tree-based breadth-first orderings effectively mitigate information bottlenecks. This approach improves long-context QA performance over common heuristic orderings, suggesting that dependency-aware chunk scheduling is a key component for scalable multi-agent long-context reasoning systems.

title: OOD-MMSafe: Advancing MLLM Safety from Harmful Intent to Hidden Consequences
summary: While safety alignment for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has gained significant attention, current paradigms primarily target malicious intent or situational violations. We propose shifting the safety frontier toward consequence-driven safety, a paradigm essential for the robust deployment of autonomous and embodied agents. To formalize this shift, we introduce OOD-MMSafe, a benchmark comprising 455 curated query-image pairs designed to evaluate a model's ability to identify latent hazards within context-dependent causal chains. Our analysis reveals a pervasive causal blindness among frontier models, with the highest 67.5% failure rate in high-capacity closed-source models, and identifies a preference ceiling where static alignment yields format-centric failures rather than improved safety reasoning as model capacity grows. To address these bottlenecks, we develop the Consequence-Aware Safety Policy Optimization (CASPO) framework, which integrates the model's intrinsic reasoning as a dynamic reference for token-level self-distillation rewards. Experimental results demonstrate that CASPO significantly enhances consequence projection, reducing the failure ratio of risk identification to 7.3% for Qwen2.5-VL-7B and 5.7% for Qwen3-VL-4B while maintaining overall effectiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a consequence-driven safety paradigm for multimodal LLMs, introduces a benchmark for latent hazard detection, finds major causal reasoning failures in existing models, and presents CASPO, a new alignment method that greatly reduces safety failures without hurting performance.
AI.motivation: Existing safety alignment for multimodal LLMs mostly focuses on filtering explicit malicious intent or obvious policy violations. However, real-world autonomous or embodied agents must anticipate the downstream consequences of seemingly benign actions in complex environments. Current models show weak causal reasoning about hazards in context, leading to unsafe behavior even when they pass standard intent-based safety filters. The authors aim to systematically expose and quantify this gap and to develop an alignment method that specifically targets consequence understanding rather than just surface-level refusal behavior.
AI.method: 1) Construct OOD-MMSafe, a benchmark of 455 image–query pairs that encode latent, context-dependent hazards requiring multi-step causal reasoning (out-of-distribution relative to common safety datasets). 2) Evaluate several frontier MLLMs on this benchmark to measure their ability to identify and articulate potential risks and consequences, analyzing failure modes such as causal blindness and format-centric failures that emerge as model capacity grows. 3) Propose CASPO (Consequence-Aware Safety Policy Optimization), a training framework that uses the model’s own chain-of-thought style internal reasoning as a dynamic reference. CASPO applies token-level self-distillation rewards to shape the model toward better consequence projection and safety reasoning rather than only applying static safety policies. 4) Train Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Qwen3-VL-4B with CASPO and compare their performance to baselines on OOD-MMSafe and general effectiveness metrics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that state-of-the-art closed-source MLLMs exhibit high failure rates (up to 67.5%) on OOD-MMSafe, confirming widespread causal blindness and a preference ceiling where more capacity improves formatting but not safety reasoning. After applying CASPO, the Qwen2.5-VL-7B model’s failure rate on risk identification drops to 7.3%, and Qwen3-VL-4B’s drops to 5.7%, while their overall task performance remains strong. This indicates that consequence-aware alignment can substantially improve hazard detection without sacrificing general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Consequence-driven safety is both necessary and tractable for MLLMs, particularly those intended for autonomous and embodied use. Standard intent-based or static alignment approaches leave large gaps in causal understanding of hazards. By introducing OOD-MMSafe, the authors give the community a focused testbed for consequence reasoning. CASPO shows that leveraging a model’s own reasoning for dynamic, token-level safety optimization can dramatically reduce causal safety failures and overcome the preference ceiling, pointing toward more robust, consequence-aware alignment strategies.

title: N-gram-like Language Models Predict Reading Time Best
summary: Recent work has found that contemporary language models such as transformers can become so good at next-word prediction that the probabilities they calculate become worse for predicting reading time. In this paper, we propose that this can be explained by reading time being sensitive to simple n-gram statistics rather than the more complex statistics learned by state-of-the-art transformer language models. We demonstrate that the neural language models whose predictions are most correlated with n-gram probability are also those that calculate probabilities that are the most correlated with eye-tracking-based metrics of reading time on naturalistic text.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that human reading time is better predicted by simple n-gram statistics than by the complex probabilities of modern transformer language models, and shows that models whose probabilities align more with n-gram statistics also better predict eye-tracking reading times.
AI.motivation: Recent transformer-based language models are highly accurate at next-word prediction, but their probability estimates paradoxically sometimes align less well with human reading times than simpler models. This raises the question of whether the cognitive processes underlying reading are more sensitive to simpler, surface-level statistical regularities (like n-grams) than to the richer representations learned by state-of-the-art models.
AI.method: The authors compare various neural language models and compute, for each, how strongly their word probability estimates correlate with n-gram probabilities. They then relate these correlations to how well each model’s probabilities predict human reading behavior, using eye-tracking-based measures of reading time on naturalistic text. Essentially, they conduct a correlational analysis across models: (1) model–n-gram probability correlation and (2) model–reading-time correlation, and examine their relationship.
AI.result: They find that language models whose word probabilities are more strongly correlated with n-gram probabilities also show stronger correlations with eye-tracking metrics of reading time. In other words, the closer a model’s statistics are to simple n-gram statistics, the better it predicts human reading times.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the hypothesis that human reading time is particularly sensitive to simple n-gram statistics rather than the more complex distributional information captured by advanced transformer models. This suggests a potential mismatch between the objectives and representations of state-of-the-art language models and the mechanisms underlying human sentence processing, and indicates that simpler statistical structure may be more cognitively relevant for predicting reading behavior.

title: Does the Question Really Matter? Training-Free Data Selection for Vision-Language SFT
summary: Visual instruction tuning is crucial for improving vision-language large models (VLLMs). However, many samples can be solved via linguistic patterns or common-sense shortcuts, without genuine cross-modal reasoning, limiting the effectiveness of multimodal learning. Prior data selection methods often rely on costly proxy model training and focus on difficulty or diversity, failing to capture a sample's true contribution to vision-language joint reasoning. In this paper, we propose CVS, a training-free data selection method based on the insight that, for high-quality multimodal samples, introducing the question should substantially alter the model's assessment of answer validity given an image. CVS leverages a frozen VLLM as an evaluator and measures the discrepancy in answer validity with and without conditioning on the question, enabling the identification of samples that require vision-language joint reasoning while filtering semantic-conflict noise. Experiments on Vision-Flan and The Cauldron show that CVS achieves solid performance across datasets. On Vision-Flan, CVS outperforms full-data training by 3.5% and 4.8% using only 10% and 15% of the data, respectively, and remains robust on the highly heterogeneous Cauldron dataset. Moreover, CVS reduces computational cost by 17.3% and 44.4% compared to COINCIDE and XMAS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CVS, a training-free method to select high-quality visual instruction-tuning data by checking how much a question changes a VLLM’s assessment of candidate answers given an image.
AI.motivation: Existing visual instruction tuning datasets contain many samples solvable by language patterns or common sense alone, providing little benefit for genuine vision-language reasoning. Current data selection techniques are expensive (requiring proxy model training) and focus mainly on difficulty or diversity, not on whether a sample truly demands joint visual and linguistic reasoning or contains semantic noise.
AI.method: The authors propose CVS, which uses a frozen vision-language large model as an evaluator. For each candidate sample (image, question, answer), CVS measures the discrepancy between answer validity scores conditioned on the image alone and on both the image and the question. A larger discrepancy indicates that the question significantly affects answer assessment and thus that the sample likely requires real vision-language joint reasoning. CVS then ranks or filters samples based on this discrepancy, while also filtering out semantic-conflict noise, all without additional model training.
AI.result: On the Vision-Flan and The Cauldron datasets, CVS-selected subsets enable models to achieve strong performance while using only a small fraction of the original data. On Vision-Flan, training on just 10% and 15% of data selected by CVS surpasses full-dataset training by 3.5% and 4.8%, respectively. CVS also maintains robust performance on the heterogeneous Cauldron dataset and lowers computation costs by 17.3% relative to COINCIDE and 44.4% relative to XMAS.
AI.conclusion: Targeting samples that truly require joint vision-language reasoning—measured via question-induced changes in answer validity—allows more effective and efficient visual instruction tuning. CVS provides a training-free, VLLM-based data selection strategy that improves accuracy over full-data baselines while significantly reducing computational overhead, and it generalizes well across different multimodal datasets.

title: Do What I Say: A Spoken Prompt Dataset for Instruction-Following
summary: Speech Large Language Models (SLLMs) have rapidly expanded, supporting a wide range of tasks. These models are typically evaluated using text prompts, which may not reflect real-world scenarios where users interact with speech. To address this gap, we introduce DoWhatISay (DOWIS), a multilingual dataset of human-recorded spoken and written prompts designed to pair with any existing benchmark for realistic evaluation of SLLMs under spoken instruction conditions. Spanning 9 tasks and 11 languages, it provides 10 prompt variants per task-language pair, across five styles. Using DOWIS, we benchmark state-of-the-art SLLMs, analyzing the interplay between prompt modality, style, language, and task type. Results show that text prompts consistently outperform spoken prompts, particularly for low-resource and cross-lingual settings. Only for tasks with speech output, spoken prompts do close the gap, highlighting the need for speech-based prompting in SLLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DoWhatISay (DOWIS), a multilingual dataset of spoken and written prompts meant to evaluate Speech Large Language Models (SLLMs) more realistically, showing that text prompts still outperform speech, especially in low-resource and cross-lingual cases.
AI.motivation: Existing SLLM evaluations mostly use text prompts, which fail to capture real-world usage where users interact via speech. There is a need for a realistic, multilingual, speech-based benchmark that can plug into existing task benchmarks and expose how SLLMs behave under spoken instructions.
AI.method: The authors construct DOWIS, a dataset of human-recorded spoken and written prompts aligned with existing benchmarks. It spans 9 tasks and 11 languages, with 10 prompt variants per task-language pair covering five speaking/writing styles. They then benchmark state-of-the-art SLLMs across different combinations of prompt modality (speech vs text), style, language resource level, and task type (including tasks with and without speech outputs).
AI.result: Across evaluations, text prompts consistently yield better model performance than spoken prompts. The performance gap is larger for low-resource languages and cross-lingual tasks. For tasks involving speech output, spoken prompts perform closer to text prompts, reducing the gap but not fully eliminating it.
AI.conclusion: Current SLLMs are still significantly weaker when prompted with speech than with text, particularly in low-resource and cross-lingual scenarios. The DOWIS dataset enables more realistic and fine-grained evaluation, and the findings underscore the importance of incorporating speech-based prompting into SLLM evaluation and development going forward.

title: AutoAgent: Evolving Cognition and Elastic Memory Orchestration for Adaptive Agents
summary: Autonomous agent frameworks still struggle to reconcile long-term experiential learning with real-time, context-sensitive decision-making. In practice, this gap appears as static cognition, rigid workflow dependence, and inefficient context usage, which jointly limit adaptability in open-ended and non-stationary environments. To address these limitations, we present AutoAgent, a self-evolving multi-agent framework built on three tightly coupled components: evolving cognition, on-the-fly contextual decision-making, and elastic memory orchestration. At the core of AutoAgent, each agent maintains structured prompt-level cognition over tools, self-capabilities, peer expertise, and task knowledge. During execution, this cognition is combined with live task context to select actions from a unified space that includes tool calls, LLM-based generation, and inter-agent requests. To support efficient long-horizon reasoning, an Elastic Memory Orchestrator dynamically organizes interaction history by preserving raw records, compressing redundant trajectories, and constructing reusable episodic abstractions, thereby reducing token overhead while retaining decision-critical evidence. These components are integrated through a closed-loop cognitive evolution process that aligns intended actions with observed outcomes to continuously update cognition and expand reusable skills, without external retraining. Empirical results across retrieval-augmented reasoning, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments show that AutoAgent consistently improves task success, tool-use efficiency, and collaborative robustness over static and memory-augmented baselines. Overall, AutoAgent provides a unified and practical foundation for adaptive autonomous agents that must learn from experience while making reliable context-aware decisions in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: AutoAgent is a self-evolving multi-agent framework that unifies evolving cognition, contextual decision-making, and elastic memory to enable adaptive, efficient, long-horizon autonomous agents without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous agent frameworks struggle to jointly support long-term experiential learning and real-time, context-sensitive decision-making. This leads to static cognition, rigid workflows, and inefficient use of context, limiting adaptability in open-ended, non-stationary environments. The paper aims to build an agent system that can continually learn from experience, use context effectively, and remain robust and flexible in dynamic tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces AutoAgent, a multi-agent framework with three tightly coupled components: (1) Evolving cognition: each agent maintains structured prompt-level knowledge about tools, its own abilities, peers' expertise, and task knowledge; this cognition is updated over time via a closed-loop process that compares intended actions with outcomes. (2) On-the-fly contextual decision-making: during execution, the agent fuses its cognition with current task context to choose actions from a unified action space encompassing tool calls, LLM generations, and inter-agent communication. (3) Elastic Memory Orchestrator: a memory management layer that maintains interaction history by preserving raw records, compressing redundant trajectories, and constructing reusable episodic abstractions to support long-horizon reasoning with lower token cost. These pieces are integrated to enable continual skill acquisition without external retraining.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations are conducted on retrieval-augmented reasoning tasks, tool-augmented agent benchmarks, and embodied task environments. Across these diverse settings, AutoAgent achieves higher task success rates, more efficient tool usage, and stronger collaborative robustness compared to static baselines and memory-augmented agent systems.
AI.conclusion: AutoAgent offers a unified, practical foundation for building adaptive autonomous agents that can simultaneously learn from their accumulated experience and make reliable, context-aware decisions in dynamic, open-ended environments. Its combination of evolving cognition, contextual action selection, and elastic memory provides a pathway beyond static or purely memory-augmented agent architectures.

title: Benchmarking Political Persuasion Risks Across Frontier Large Language Models
summary: Concerns persist regarding the capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to sway political views. Although prior research has claimed that LLMs are not more persuasive than standard political campaign practices, the recent rise of frontier models warrants further study. In two survey experiments (N=19,145) across bipartisan issues and stances, we evaluate seven state-of-the-art LLMs developed by Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and xAI. We find that LLMs outperform standard campaign advertisements, with heterogeneity in performance across models. Specifically, Claude models exhibit the highest persuasiveness, while Grok exhibits the lowest. The results are robust across issues and stances. Moreover, in contrast to the findings in Hackenburg et al. (2025b) and Lin et al. (2025) that information-based prompts boost persuasiveness, we find that the effectiveness of information-based prompts is model-dependent: they increase the persuasiveness of Claude and Grok while substantially reducing that of GPT. We introduce a data-driven and strategy-agnostic LLM-assisted conversation analysis approach to identify and assess underlying persuasive strategies. Our work benchmarks the persuasive risks of frontier models and provides a framework for cross-model comparative risk assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper tests how persuasive several frontier LLMs are on bipartisan political issues, finding that they can be more persuasive than standard campaign ads, with Claude most and Grok least persuasive, and that information-rich prompts help some models but hurt others.
AI.motivation: There is growing concern that advanced LLMs might significantly influence or manipulate people’s political attitudes, especially as newer, more capable models are deployed at scale. Previous work, largely based on earlier-generation models, suggested LLMs were not more persuasive than typical political campaign tools. With rapid model advances and differing architectures and training approaches across providers, there is a need to reassess persuasive power, compare models systematically, and understand which prompting strategies amplify or mitigate their persuasive impact.
AI.method: The authors conduct two large-scale survey experiments with a combined sample of 19,145 participants, covering multiple bipartisan political issues and both sides of each issue. Participants are randomly exposed either to standard political campaign advertisements or to messages generated by one of seven state-of-the-art LLMs (from Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, and xAI). They manipulate prompt types, including information-based prompts, to test how different instructions affect persuasive impact. They then compare attitude change across conditions and use a data-driven, model-agnostic conversation analysis pipeline to extract and evaluate the rhetorical and persuasive strategies used by each model.
AI.result: Across issues and stances, LLM-generated messages generally produce greater attitude change than standard political campaign advertisements, demonstrating that frontier LLMs can be highly persuasive. There is substantial variation between models: Claude models are the most persuasive, while Grok is the least. The impact of information-based prompts is not uniform: these prompts increase persuasiveness for Claude and Grok models but markedly reduce persuasiveness for GPT models. The identified effects are robust across a range of topics and experimental conditions. The analysis pipeline uncovers diverse persuasive strategies and reveals systematic differences in how models attempt to persuade.
AI.conclusion: Frontier LLMs pose heightened persuasive risks relative to conventional political campaign content, and their influence varies meaningfully across providers and model families. Prompt design interacts with model-specific behaviors, so information-rich instructions cannot be assumed to universally increase persuasiveness and may even backfire for some models. The proposed LLM-assisted conversation analysis framework offers a scalable, strategy-agnostic way to benchmark and compare persuasive capabilities across models, providing a foundation for more rigorous risk assessment, governance, and safety interventions around politically relevant LLM deployments.

title: World2Mind: Cognition Toolkit for Allocentric Spatial Reasoning in Foundation Models
summary: Achieving robust spatial reasoning remains a fundamental challenge for current Multimodal Foundation Models (MFMs). Existing methods either overfit statistical shortcuts via 3D grounding data or remain confined to 2D visual perception, limiting both spatial reasoning accuracy and generalization in unseen scenarios. Inspired by the spatial cognitive mapping mechanisms of biological intelligence, we propose World2Mind, a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit. At its core, World2Mind leverages 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation models to construct structured spatial cognitive maps, empowering MFMs to proactively acquire targeted spatial knowledge regarding interested landmarks and routes of interest. To provide robust geometric-topological priors, World2Mind synthesizes an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST) that uses elliptical parameters to model the top-down layout of landmarks accurately. To mitigate the inherent inaccuracies of 3D reconstruction, we introduce a three-stage reasoning chain comprising tool invocation assessment, modality-decoupled cue collection, and geometry-semantics interwoven reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that World2Mind boosts the performance of frontier models, such as GPT-5.2, by 5%~18%. Astonishingly, relying solely on the AST-structured text, purely text-only foundation models can perform complex 3D spatial reasoning, achieving performance approaching that of advanced multimodal models.

AI.tldr: World2Mind is a training-free toolkit that equips multimodal and even text-only foundation models with explicit 3D spatial representations so they can reason about landmarks and routes more robustly.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal foundation models struggle with robust 3D spatial reasoning: methods that use 3D grounding often overfit dataset-specific shortcuts, while approaches limited to 2D perception cannot generalize well to unseen spatial layouts. The paper aims to get beyond these limitations by giving models an explicit, structured representation of 3D space inspired by biological spatial cognition, without retraining the models themselves.
AI.method: The authors design World2Mind, a training-free spatial intelligence toolkit that sits on top of existing MFMs. It first uses 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation to build structured spatial cognitive maps of a scene. From these, it synthesizes an Allocentric-Spatial Tree (AST), a top-down, allocentric representation that encodes the layout of landmarks using elliptical parameters to capture geometric and topological relations. For reasoning, it introduces a three-stage chain: (1) decide what tools or spatial information are needed (tool invocation assessment), (2) collect cues from each modality separately (modality-decoupled cue collection), and (3) perform intertwined geometric and semantic reasoning over the AST and language to answer spatial questions or plan routes.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments and benchmarks, incorporating World2Mind improves the spatial reasoning performance of strong multimodal models (e.g., GPT-5.2) by about 5%–18% over their baselines. Moreover, when only the AST-structured textual description is provided (no raw images), purely text-only foundation models can solve complex 3D spatial reasoning tasks nearly as well as advanced multimodal models that see the full visual input.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, biologically inspired spatial cognitive maps and allocentric geometric-topological priors can significantly enhance the spatial reasoning capabilities of foundation models without additional training. By externalizing 3D structure into an AST representation and guiding models through a structured reasoning chain, even text-only models can approach multimodal performance on challenging 3D spatial tasks. This suggests a promising direction of augmenting foundation models with structured external spatial tools rather than trying to learn all spatial competence end-to-end from data.

title: Thinking to Recall: How Reasoning Unlocks Parametric Knowledge in LLMs
summary: While reasoning in LLMs plays a natural role in math, code generation, and multi-hop factual questions, its effect on simple, single-hop factual questions remains unclear. Such questions do not require step-by-step logical decomposition, making the utility of reasoning highly counterintuitive. Nevertheless, we find that enabling reasoning substantially expands the capability boundary of the model's parametric knowledge recall, unlocking correct answers that are otherwise effectively unreachable. Why does reasoning aid parametric knowledge recall when there are no complex reasoning steps to be done? To answer this, we design a series of hypothesis-driven controlled experiments, and identify two key driving mechanisms: (1) a computational buffer effect, where the model uses the generated reasoning tokens to perform latent computation independent of their semantic content; and (2) factual priming, where generating topically related facts acts as a semantic bridge that facilitates correct answer retrieval. Importantly, this latter generative self-retrieval mechanism carries inherent risks: we demonstrate that hallucinating intermediate facts during reasoning increases the likelihood of hallucinations in the final answer. Finally, we show that our insights can be harnessed to directly improve model accuracy by prioritizing reasoning trajectories that contain hallucination-free factual statements.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why enabling chain-of-thought style reasoning helps large language models answer even simple factual questions, finding that reasoning acts both as a computational buffer and as factual priming, but also increases hallucination risks; the authors then use these insights to improve accuracy by selecting safe reasoning paths.
AI.motivation: Although reasoning in LLMs is known to help with complex tasks like math and multi-hop QA, its benefits for simple, single-hop factual questions are puzzling because these questions do not obviously require step-by-step reasoning; the authors want to understand whether and why reasoning still helps, and what mechanisms are responsible, especially given concerns about hallucinations.
AI.method: The authors design hypothesis-driven controlled experiments on LLMs answering single-hop factual questions with and without explicit reasoning; they construct interventions to test two hypothesized mechanisms—computational buffering and factual priming—by manipulating the presence, length, and topicality of reasoning tokens, and by analyzing how hallucinated intermediate facts affect final answers; they then develop a technique that prioritizes reasoning trajectories with hallucination-free factual statements to empirically validate performance gains.
AI.result: They find that enabling reasoning significantly increases the range of questions for which the model can correctly recall parametric knowledge, even when the questions are simple; experiments support two mechanisms: (1) a computational buffer effect, where extra reasoning tokens allow latent computation regardless of token meaning, and (2) factual priming, where generating related facts semantically guides the model toward the correct answer; they also show that hallucinated intermediate facts correlate with higher hallucination rates in final answers and that filtering for hallucination-free reasoning trajectories boosts accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning benefits LLMs on simple factual QA not because the task is logically complex, but because reasoning tokens provide additional computational budget and semantic scaffolding for knowledge retrieval; however, the same mechanisms that help recall can also amplify hallucinations when intermediate reasoning is factually wrong; by steering models toward accurate, hallucination-free reasoning paths, one can systematically improve factual accuracy in parametric knowledge recall tasks.

title: Quantifying the Necessity of Chain of Thought through Opaque Serial Depth
summary: Large language models (LLMs) tend to externalize their reasoning in their chain of thought, making the chain of thought a good target for monitoring. This is partially an inherent feature of the Transformer architecture: sufficiently long serial cognition must pass through the chain of thought (Korbak et al., 2025). We formalize this argument through the notion of opaque serial depth, given by the length of the longest computation that can be done without the use of interpretable intermediate steps like chain of thought. Given this formalization, we compute numeric upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of Gemma 3 models, as well as asymptotic results for additional architectures beyond standard LLMs. We also open-source an automated method that can calculate upper bounds on the opaque serial depth of arbitrary neural networks, and use it to demonstrate that Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower depth than dense models. Overall, our results suggest that opaque serial depth is a useful tool for understanding the potential for models to do significant reasoning that is not externalized.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and quantifies “opaque serial depth,” a measure of how much internal sequential reasoning a model can perform without exposing interpretable intermediate steps (like chain-of-thought), and applies it to Gemma 3 and other architectures.
AI.motivation: LLMs often externalize reasoning in chain-of-thought, which makes them monitorable. However, models might still perform substantial internal reasoning that is not visible in their outputs. To assess and constrain this hidden reasoning, the authors need a formal way to measure how much sequential computation can remain opaque inside the model.
AI.method: They define a formal concept called opaque serial depth: the length of the longest computation a model can carry out without relying on interpretable intermediate representations such as chain-of-thought tokens. Using this framework, they derive numeric upper bounds on opaque serial depth for Gemma 3 models and obtain asymptotic bounds for broader classes of architectures. They also build and release an automated tool that takes an arbitrary neural network as input and computes an upper bound on its opaque serial depth, then apply it to compare dense versus Mixture-of-Experts models.
AI.result: For Gemma 3, they obtain concrete numeric upper bounds on opaque serial depth, indicating how much internal serial reasoning the models can hide. Their asymptotic analysis extends these bounds to other architectures beyond standard LLMs. Applying their automated tool, they find that Mixture-of-Experts architectures likely exhibit lower opaque serial depth than comparable dense models, suggesting they have less capacity for hidden sequential reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Opaque serial depth is a practical and informative measure of a model’s capacity for non-externalized reasoning. The computed bounds for Gemma 3 and other architectures, along with the automated analysis tool, support using opaque serial depth to understand and compare models’ potential for hidden serial cognition, and indicate that MoE models may be more limited in such opaque reasoning than dense models.

title: Model Merging in the Era of Large Language Models: Methods, Applications, and Future Directions
summary: Model merging has emerged as a transformative paradigm for combining the capabilities of multiple neural networks into a single unified model without additional training. With the rapid proliferation of fine-tuned large language models~(LLMs), merging techniques offer a computationally efficient alternative to ensembles and full retraining, enabling practitioners to compose specialized capabilities at minimal cost. This survey presents a comprehensive and structured examination of model merging in the LLM era through the \textbf{FUSE} taxonomy, a four-dimensional framework organized along \textbf{F}oundations, \textbf{U}nification Strategies, \textbf{S}cenarios, and \textbf{E}cosystem. We first establish the theoretical underpinnings of merging, including loss landscape geometry, mode connectivity, and the linear mode connectivity hypothesis. We then systematically review the algorithmic landscape, spanning weight averaging, task vector arithmetic, sparsification-enhanced methods, mixture-of-experts architectures, and evolutionary optimization approaches. For each method family, we analyze the core formulation, highlight representative works, and discuss practical trade-offs. We further examine downstream applications across multi-task learning, safety alignment, domain specialization, multilingual transfer, and federated learning. Finally, we survey the supporting ecosystem of open-source tools, community platforms, and evaluation benchmarks, and identify key open challenges including theoretical gaps, scalability barriers, and standardization needs. This survey aims to equip researchers and practitioners with a structured foundation for advancing model merging.

AI.tldr: Survey of model merging techniques for large language models, organized by a new FUSE taxonomy (Foundations, Unification Strategies, Scenarios, Ecosystem).
AI.motivation: Fine-tuned LLMs are proliferating, but combining their specialized abilities traditionally requires costly ensembles or retraining. Model merging promises a cheaper way to fuse capabilities into a single model, yet the field is fragmented and lacks a unified conceptual and practical overview.
AI.method: Construct a four-dimensional taxonomy (FUSE: Foundations, Unification Strategies, Scenarios, Ecosystem); review theoretical work on loss landscapes and mode connectivity; systematically categorize and compare merging algorithms (weight averaging, task vectors, sparsification-based methods, MoE-style approaches, evolutionary methods); and survey practical applications and supporting tools/benchmarks.
AI.result: Provides an organized synthesis of the literature on LLM model merging, mapping methods to theory, use cases, and tooling; clarifies trade-offs between method families; and surfaces gaps in theory, scalability, and standardization revealed by the survey.
AI.conclusion: Model merging is a promising, computationally efficient alternative to ensembles and retraining for composing LLM capabilities, but it remains theoretically underdeveloped and practically fragmented. A structured FUSE taxonomy and consolidated view of methods, applications, and tools can guide future research toward better-understood, scalable, and standardized merging techniques.

title: LCA: Local Classifier Alignment for Continual Learning
summary: A fundamental requirement for intelligent systems is the ability to learn continuously under changing environments. However, models trained in this regime often suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Leveraging pre-trained models has recently emerged as a promising solution, since their generalized feature extractors enable faster and more robust adaptation. While some earlier works mitigate forgetting by fine-tuning only on the first task, this approach quickly deteriorates as the number of tasks grows and the data distributions diverge. More recent research instead seeks to consolidate task knowledge into a unified backbone, or adapting the backbone as new tasks arrive. However, such approaches may create a (potential) \textit{mismatch} between task-specific classifiers and the adapted backbone. To address this issue, we propose a novel \textit{Local Classifier Alignment} (LCA) loss to better align the classifier with backbone. Theoretically, we show that this LCA loss can enable the classifier to not only generalize well for all observed tasks, but also improve robustness. Furthermore, we develop a complete solution for continual learning, following the model merging approach and using LCA. Extensive experiments on several standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method often achieves leading performance, sometimes surpasses the state-of-the-art methods with a large margin.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new loss function, Local Classifier Alignment (LCA), to better align task-specific classifiers with an evolving backbone in continual learning, improving generalization and robustness while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
AI.motivation: Continual learning systems must adapt to new tasks over time without forgetting previously learned tasks. Existing methods either freeze most of a pre-trained model or continually adapt the backbone, but both can cause performance drops as tasks accumulate: freezing harms adaptation and backbone adaptation can create a mismatch between the changing feature extractor and fixed or partially updated classifiers. This mismatch degrades performance and robustness across tasks. The authors aim to address this classifier–backbone mismatch while still leveraging pre-trained models for continual learning.
AI.method: The authors introduce a Local Classifier Alignment (LCA) loss that explicitly encourages the task-specific classifiers to stay aligned with the current state of the backbone’s feature space during continual learning. They embed LCA into a model-merging continual learning framework built on pre-trained backbones. As new tasks arrive, models or task heads are adapted/merged while LCA regularizes the classifier parameters to maintain compatibility with the evolving shared backbone features. They provide a theoretical analysis showing that minimizing LCA leads to improved generalization and robustness for all observed tasks.
AI.result: Across several standard continual learning benchmarks, the proposed method with LCA achieves leading or state-of-the-art performance, often outperforming prior approaches by a considerable margin. Empirical results show reduced catastrophic forgetting and improved robustness when compared with methods that either only fine-tune on early tasks or that adapt the backbone without explicit classifier–backbone alignment.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly aligning classifiers with an evolving backbone via the proposed LCA loss is an effective strategy for continual learning with pre-trained models. It alleviates catastrophic forgetting, improves robustness, and scales better as the number of tasks and distributional shifts increase, achieving strong or state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks. The work highlights the importance of addressing classifier–backbone mismatch in continual learning architectures.

title: CREATE: Testing LLMs for Associative Creativity
summary: A key component of creativity is associative reasoning: the ability to draw novel yet meaningful connections between concepts. We introduce CREATE, a benchmark designed to evaluate models' capacity for creative associative reasoning. CREATE requires models to generate sets of paths connecting concepts in a model's parametric knowledge. Paths should have high specificity (distinctiveness and closeness of the concept connection) and high diversity (dissimilarity from other paths), and models are scored more highly if they produce a larger set of strong, diverse paths. This task shares demands of real creativity tasks like hypothesis generation, including an extremely large search space, but enables collection of a sizable benchmark with objective answer grading. Evaluation of frontier models shows that the strongest models achieve higher creative utility than others, with the high multiplicity of answers and complexity of the search making benchmark saturation difficult to achieve. Furthermore, our results illustrate that thinking models are not always more effective on our task, even with high token budgets. Recent approaches for creative prompting give some but limited additional improvement. CREATE provides a sandbox for developing new methods to improve models' capacity for associative creativity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CREATE, a benchmark to evaluate how well models perform creative associative reasoning by generating multiple, specific, and diverse conceptual paths between ideas.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on accuracy or simple generation and do not directly evaluate a model’s ability to make novel but meaningful conceptual connections, which is central to creativity and tasks like hypothesis generation. There is a need for an objective, scalable way to measure associative creativity in models.
AI.method: The authors design CREATE, a benchmark where models must generate sets of paths that connect given concept pairs using their parametric knowledge. Each path is evaluated for specificity (how distinct and tightly connected the concepts are) and diversity (how different each path is from the others). The scoring rewards models that can output a larger number of high-quality, diverse paths. They test various frontier models, including “thinking” models and models with creative prompting strategies, and compare their performance.
AI.result: Frontier models differ in their performance on CREATE, with stronger models showing higher creative utility by producing better and more diverse connection paths. However, the large search space and high multiplicity of valid answers prevent easy benchmark saturation. Thinking-style models with long reasoning chains are not consistently better on this task, and recent creative prompting methods yield only modest performance gains.
AI.conclusion: CREATE is a challenging, high-multiplicity benchmark that captures important aspects of associative creativity and does not saturate easily. It reveals that current reasoning and prompting approaches are not sufficient to fully solve creative associative reasoning, making CREATE a useful sandbox for developing and testing new methods to enhance models’ creative capacities.

title: Influencing LLM Multi-Agent Dialogue via Policy-Parameterized Prompts
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have emerged as a new paradigm for multi-agent systems. However, existing research on the behaviour of LLM-based multi-agents relies on ad hoc prompts and lacks a principled policy perspective. Different from reinforcement learning, we investigate whether prompt-as-action can be parameterized so as to construct a lightweight policy which consists of a sequence of state-action pairs to influence conversational behaviours without training. Our framework regards prompts as actions executed by LLMs, and dynamically constructs prompts through five components based on the current state of the agent. To test the effectiveness of parameterized control, we evaluated the dialogue flow based on five indicators: responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, and stance shift. We conduct experiments using different LLM-driven agents in two discussion scenarios related to the general public and show that prompt parameterization can influence the dialogue dynamics. This result shows that policy-parameterised prompts offer a simple and effective mechanism to influence the dialogue process, which will help the research of multi-agent systems in the direction of social simulation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a policy-style, parameterized “prompt-as-action” framework to systematically control how LLM-based agents behave in multi-agent dialogues, showing that structured prompts can steer dialogue dynamics without additional training.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM multi-agent work mostly uses ad hoc, static prompts and lacks a principled, policy-based way to control or analyze agent behavior. The authors want a lightweight, reinforcement-learning-style notion of policy for LLM agents that allows explicit, tunable control over dialogue behaviors without costly training or fine-tuning.
AI.method: They conceptualize prompts as actions taken by an LLM agent and build a framework where these prompts are dynamically constructed from five components, conditioned on the agent’s current state. This yields a parameterized sequence of state–action (state–prompt) pairs, analogous to a policy. They then define five dialogue-quality indicators—responsiveness, rebuttal, evidence usage, non-repetition, and stance shift—and design two multi-agent discussion scenarios involving general public topics. Multiple LLM-based agents are run with different prompt parameterizations to observe how dialogue trajectories change under these policies.
AI.result: Experiments show that changing the parameters of the prompt-as-action policy measurably affects the resulting dialogue along the five indicators. The controlled prompt policies can encourage more responsiveness or rebuttal, alter evidence usage, reduce repetition, and affect stance shift patterns, demonstrating that structured prompt design can systematically steer conversation dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that policy-parameterized prompts provide an effective, lightweight mechanism to influence the behavior of LLM-based multi-agent systems without training. This establishes a more principled policy perspective for LLM agents, and supports future work on using such parameterized prompt policies for social simulation and analysis of interaction dynamics in LLM multi-agent environments.

title: MedMASLab: A Unified Orchestration Framework for Benchmarking Multimodal Medical Multi-Agent Systems
summary: While Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) show potential for complex clinical decision support, the field remains hindered by architectural fragmentation and the lack of standardized multimodal integration. Current medical MAS research suffers from non-uniform data ingestion pipelines, inconsistent visual-reasoning evaluation, and a lack of cross-specialty benchmarking. To address these challenges, we present MedMASLab, a unified framework and benchmarking platform for multimodal medical multi-agent systems. MedMASLab introduces: (1) A standardized multimodal agent communication protocol that enables seamless integration of 11 heterogeneous MAS architectures across 24 medical modalities. (2) An automated clinical reasoning evaluator, a zero-shot semantic evaluation paradigm that overcomes the limitations of lexical string-matching by leveraging large vision-language models to verify diagnostic logic and visual grounding. (3) The most extensive benchmark to date, spanning 11 organ systems and 473 diseases, standardizing data from 11 clinical benchmarks. Our systematic evaluation reveals a critical domain-specific performance gap: while MAS improves reasoning depth, current architectures exhibit significant fragility when transitioning between specialized medical sub-domains. We provide a rigorous ablation of interaction mechanisms and cost-performance trade-offs, establishing a new technical baseline for future autonomous clinical systems. The source code and data is publicly available at: https://github.com/NUS-Project/MedMASLab/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MedMASLab, a unified framework and benchmark for evaluating and standardizing multimodal medical multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: Research in medical multi-agent systems is fragmented, with heterogeneous architectures, non-uniform data pipelines, inconsistent evaluation of visual reasoning, and no broad cross-specialty benchmarks. This makes it hard to compare methods, measure progress, and deploy robust clinical decision-support systems across medical sub-domains.
AI.method: The authors design MedMASLab, which includes: (1) a standardized multimodal agent communication protocol that allows 11 different MAS architectures to interoperate over 24 medical data modalities; (2) an automated, zero-shot clinical reasoning evaluator that uses large vision-language models to assess diagnostic logic and visual grounding instead of relying on string matching; and (3) a large-scale benchmark that aggregates and standardizes data from 11 existing clinical benchmarks, spanning 11 organ systems and 473 diseases. They conduct systematic experiments, ablations on interaction mechanisms, and analyze cost-performance tradeoffs.
AI.result: Using MedMASLab, the authors show that existing medical MAS architectures benefit in reasoning depth but are fragile when transferred across different medical specialties and sub-domains. Their evaluations quantify this domain-specific performance gap and characterize how different interaction designs and computational budgets affect performance.
AI.conclusion: MedMASLab provides a unified infrastructure and the largest multimodal benchmark so far for medical multi-agent systems, revealing important weaknesses in current approaches and establishing a technical baseline for future research on robust, autonomous clinical decision support. The open-source release is intended to facilitate standardized evaluation, comparison, and development of more generalizable medical MAS.

title: PathMem: Toward Cognition-Aligned Memory Transformation for Pathology MLLMs
summary: Computational pathology demands both visual pattern recognition and dynamic integration of structured domain knowledge, including taxonomy, grading criteria, and clinical evidence. In practice, diagnostic reasoning requires linking morphological evidence with formal diagnostic and grading criteria. Although multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong vision language reasoning capabilities, they lack explicit mechanisms for structured knowledge integration and interpretable memory control. As a result, existing models struggle to consistently incorporate pathology-specific diagnostic standards during reasoning. Inspired by the hierarchical memory process of human pathologists, we propose PathMem, a memory-centric multimodal framework for pathology MLLMs. PathMem organizes structured pathology knowledge as a long-term memory (LTM) and introduces a Memory Transformer that models the dynamic transition from LTM to working memory (WM) through multimodal memory activation and context-aware knowledge grounding, enabling context-aware memory refinement for downstream reasoning. PathMem achieves SOTA performance across benchmarks, improving WSI-Bench report generation (12.8% WSI-Precision, 10.1% WSI-Relevance) and open-ended diagnosis by 9.7% and 8.9% over prior WSI-based models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PathMem, a memory-centric multimodal large language model framework for computational pathology that explicitly integrates structured pathology knowledge into visual-text reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on pathology benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large language models in computational pathology can recognize visual patterns and perform vision-language reasoning but lack explicit mechanisms to integrate structured pathology knowledge such as taxonomies, grading systems, and clinical evidence. This limitation leads to unstable use of domain-specific diagnostic standards and weak interpretability, whereas human pathologists rely on hierarchical, memory-like processes that connect long-term knowledge with case-specific working memory during diagnosis.
AI.method: The authors design PathMem, a multimodal framework that treats structured pathology knowledge as long-term memory (LTM) and introduces a Memory Transformer module. This module dynamically activates and refines relevant knowledge into a working memory (WM) guided by both image and text inputs. The system performs multimodal memory activation, context-aware grounding of knowledge to the current case, and iterative WM refinement, after which the refined memory is used for downstream reasoning tasks such as report generation and diagnosis in whole-slide imaging (WSI).
AI.result: PathMem achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple computational pathology benchmarks. On WSI-Bench report generation, it improves WSI-Precision by 12.8% and WSI-Relevance by 10.1% compared to previous models. For open-ended diagnosis tasks using whole-slide images, PathMem outperforms prior WSI-based models by 9.7% and 8.9% on the reported metrics, showing that explicit memory modeling and knowledge integration substantially enhance diagnostic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly organizing pathology knowledge as long-term memory and modeling its dynamic transition into working memory enables multimodal LLMs to better align with human-like diagnostic reasoning. PathMem’s memory-centric architecture improves both performance and interpretability in computational pathology, demonstrating that structured knowledge integration is crucial for robust, pathology-specific vision-language reasoning and suggesting a promising direction for future medical AI systems.

title: The Confidence Gate Theorem: When Should Ranked Decision Systems Abstain?
summary: Ranked decision systems -- recommenders, ad auctions, clinical triage queues -- must decide when to intervene in ranked outputs and when to abstain. We study when confidence-based abstention monotonically improves decision quality, and when it fails. The formal conditions are simple: rank-alignment and no inversion zones. The substantive contribution is identifying why these conditions hold or fail: the distinction between structural uncertainty (missing data, e.g., cold-start) and contextual uncertainty (missing context, e.g., temporal drift). Empirically, we validate this distinction across three domains: collaborative filtering (MovieLens, 3 distribution shifts), e-commerce intent detection (RetailRocket, Criteo, Yoochoose), and clinical pathway triage (MIMIC-IV). Structural uncertainty produces near-monotonic abstention gains in all domains; structurally grounded confidence signals (observation counts) fail under contextual drift, producing as many monotonicity violations as random abstention on our MovieLens temporal split.
